Elden Ring feels very distinct from the rest of the Souls games and their imitators and is incredibly successful at really selling a feeling of pure adventure and exploration, on top of having a top-notch art style and combat mechanics. It's not perfect, some bosses are grueling, the map is big enough that it can be a turn-off to some, and the side quests are maddeningly easy to screw up. But it's as close to my ideal version of this game as it can get without reading my mind and that's gotta count for something.

This game is what started my complete fixation with souls games, I’ve hardly played any other type of game since this released, purely for the fact that no other game excites me like Elden ring or the previous titles. Absolute peak gaming experience.

Little needs to be said about this game.  If you enjoy exploration, challenging combat that can be offset by potentially broken power-scaling mechanics, and a story that hints at complexity while remaining vague enough to engage your imagination, this is your game.

Elden Ring is genuinely one of the best games I’ve ever played.

It’s not perfect, some bosses are annoying and the questlines are basically impossible without looking stuff up, but the game is still insane. The map feels massive without being empty, every random cave or elevator can lead to something cool, and there’s always some weird weapon, boss, armor set, or hidden area to find.

The combat is hard but addicting. You can get destroyed for an hour, change your build a little, learn the fight, then suddenly win and feel like you actually earned it. That’s what makes the game so good. It doesn’t just hand you wins, it makes you lock in.

The build variety is probably my favorite part. You can run strength, bleed, magic, faith, dex, whatever cursed setup you want, and somehow it can still work if you build it right.

Honestly, I get why people call this game a masterpiece. It’s frustrating sometimes, but in the best way. One of those games where you think you’re only playing for 30 minutes and then somehow it’s 4 AM.

Dark Souls 3 is one of my favorite games of all time and one of the best action RPGs I have ever played.

What makes it so special is how addictive the progression feels. Exploring new areas, finding bonfires, opening shortcuts, learning enemy patterns, and slowly pushing forward is incredibly satisfying. The enemy variety is excellent, and the game constantly forces you to adapt.

The visual side is also a huge strength. The world is varied, memorable, and beautiful in that dark, ruined Dark Souls way.

And then there are the boss fights. This is probably the game I think of first when I hear the word “bossfight.” Dark Souls 3 has some of the best bosses I have ever fought in a game.

My only real criticism is that the game can sometimes feel a bit too harsh, and occasionally even slightly unfair. Still, the challenge is a huge part of what makes it so rewarding.

Even as someone who is not an elite Souls player, I was still able to beat the base game and almost every boss. For me, that says a lot: the game is difficult, but not impossibly so.

If you enjoy challenging games with great exploration, amazing bosses, and a memorable world, this is an easy recommendation.

[h3] This Game Is Good [/h3]
How have I not reviewed this game yet? Its good, very good. The net-code and PVP work smoother than in 1 or 2, no contest. Its a more modern title and it shows, leave behind your nostalgia for 1's chain-stabs and look past 2's charm and you will realize this game is a beautiful redhead two lockers down.

[h3] The Good With The Bad [/h3] 
The rolling mechanics are over-tuned but not atrocious, SOME of the PVP factions are loads of fun, the bosses have oodles of personality, two of the bosses in 3 are contenders for best boss design in the entire series (but one of these is a secret boss and the other is in a DLC), the lore was interesting but not to my taste (deal with it), and the DLCs are peak dark souls content but accordingly expensive.

[h3] Honorable Mentions [/h3]
The soundtrack slaps, the area design is a breath of fresh air after 2's unfortunate setbacks, the servers still work (for now), there is still a healthy mix of new players and experienced PVP veterans (so you can noob stomp or match wits with pvp gods, your choice), there are not many (overt) cheaters based on my anecdotal experience, there are a healthy spread of endings and the end cut-scenes are beautiful (so your wretched hollow weirdo player character can shine).

┏━━━━━━━━━━━┓ puzzle for thine mind:
┃ ✠ /\ ✠ ┃
┃ /A \ ┃ "A linear path of sorrow, leading ever onward
┃ /___\ ┃ to a foe blinded by thunder... Mmm... Mmm..."
┃ ✠ ✠ ┃
┗━━━━━━━━━━━┛ Thus would I imagine Siegward of Catarina
describing the journey through Dark Souls III.

I loved every minute of this game.

By today's standards, I understand many of the criticisms often directed at Dark Souls III. Some say it is too linear. Others point to Miyazaki's other masterpieces as superior evolutions of the formula. Yet none of that diminished the strange magic this game held over me.

Even after venturing into NG++, I still found myself wanting more.

What can I possibly say that countless reviewers and Souls veterans have not already expressed more eloquently? Entire essays have been Youtubed dissecting its level design, combat systems, soundtrack, symbolism and lore. I cannot hope to improve upon them.

══════════ ✠ ══════════

What I can offer is something simpler.

If you are standing at the edge of this journey, uncertain whether to begin, then kindle the flame and press onward.

If life has been heavy lately...
If you are tired, lost, or carrying burdens that feel too great...

Dark Souls III has a peculiar way of meeting you in that darkness.

There is solace in the crackling of bonfires. There is comfort in the melancholy beauty of a dying world. There is something deeply human about falling,  then rising again, and taking one more step forward.

The sound design alone is enough to transport you elsewhere for an evening. Add to that unforgettable boss encounters, haunting landscapes, and a world filled with wonder hidden beneath its ruin, and you have an experience that stays with you long after the credits roll.

══════════ ✠ ══════════

Trust those who love this series.

Trust those who still return to Lothric years later.

And trust this Ashen One when I say:

Dark Souls III is more than a game.

It is a journey worth taking.

Can DS3 preserve what made DS1 special while modernising the formula and wrapping up the trilogy? No pressure lol.

I jumped into DS3 unsure of how it was going to handle things. As the direct sequel to DS1, would it feel too similar? Would it feel too different? Thematically and mechanically it does feel like a more refined, modernised DS1, but where DS3 really shined for me was its atmosphere. It didn’t just feel like I was revisiting familiar places; it genuinely felt like the world I once knew had moved on without me.

DS3 provides the most dynamic and fast-paced bosses from across the trilogy. Pace was clearly the catalyst used to modernise the Souls formula. I don’t think this always landed; I do think DS3 occasionally mistook relentless aggression for meaningful, interactive difficulty. Some fights become so focused on spectacle, long combo strings and constant pressure that interaction starts to break down. That being said, at its best, it created some of the best bosses in the entire series.

The game also felt a little too linear for my taste, especially compared to DS1 and DS2. However, if your main interest is getting stuck into polished modern Souls bosses, this may actually be a positive.

As a conclusion to the series? [spoiler] DS3 really hits the nail on the head. DS3 wraps up the trilogy not with a bang, but with a whimper. A pyrrhic, or perhaps hollow (ha), victory. The ending captures exactly what makes Dark Souls special: melancholy, exhaustion, and the Sisyphean task of clinging to a world long past its prime. [/spoiler]

Overall, DS3 is a modern, polished, and very fitting end to an amazing trilogy.

[h1]All Joke, No Game[/h1]

Even knowing Goat Simulator is meant to be absurd and casual, I couldn’t find a single moment of real enjoyment. The novelty wore off fast, and what’s left feels like a clunky, directionless experience. I genuinely don’t understand what sets it apart from the sea of other indie “joke” games - aside from a bigger marketing push.

The physics-based chaos, while initially amusing, quickly becomes repetitive and shallow. Glitches that are supposed to be funny just feel lazy after a while, and there’s little sense of progression or purpose to keep you engaged. It feels more like a tech demo with meme appeal than a game designed to be played for more than a few minutes.

Goat Simulator is not a game – it’s pure, unfiltered chaos in interactive form. In other games you get story, character progression, sensible mechanics… here you’re a goat, destroying a small town, exploding for no reason, and earning points for knocking over a human with your tongue.

I remember playing this for the first time on my phone sometime around 2018 (maybe even earlier), and even back then I knew this was the definition of “stupid fun done right.” Now on PC, everything is even more ridiculous, and I’m having just as much fun as I did years ago.

From the very first minutes, you can tell this game was made with the philosophy of “the dumber, the better.” The physics are so absurd that even Newton would roll in his grave – with full ragdoll effects. Every glitch and bug is intentional, and the more chaos you create, the better the game gets.

The map is basically a playground for your creativity (and destruction). You can bounce off trampolines, launch fireworks from your goat mouth, perform satanic rituals in a barn, or become the goat queen of the universe. And trust me, that’s just the beginning.

Goat Simulator is the perfect example that games don’t have to be serious to be brilliant. It’s not about goals or story – it’s about spending an hour feeling like the horned king of chaos.

Score: 9/10 – because it’s the only game where I can accidentally blow up a gas station and then end up on the moon a minute later… and everyone’s laughing about it.

Video games peaked on april 1st, 2014, and have gone downhill since

When Roger Ebert boldly declared that "Video games can never be art" in 2010, he CLEARLY never could have anticipated this artistic masterpiece. Goat Simulator sits in the cannon alongside the greatest western artistic works such as the Sistine Chapel, the Mona Lisa, The Divine Comedy, Romeo & Juliet, Da Vinci's The Last Supper, Skibidi Toilet, Dostoevsky's Crimes And Punishment, and the cinematic works of the great filmmakers such as Tarkovsky, Kubrick, and Lynch as well as The Minecraft Movie. It really taps into the cultural zeitgeist of being a human living under late stage capitalism and the painful struggles for survival that the poor and working middle classes face when the goat says "BAAAAAAA!!!!!" We feel his RAGE living in a system designed to keep us down, as similar to the lyrics of The Smashing Pumpkins in their song "Bullet with Butterfly Wings" which says "Despite all my rage, I am still just a rat in a cage."


When David Lynch (RIP) said “Ideas are like fish. If you want to catch little fish, you can stay in the shallow water. But if you want to catch the big fish, you've got to go deeper. Down deep, the fish are more powerful and more pure", he was CLEARLY talking about Goat Simulator (2014), which is the artistic biproduct of a team not afraid to take true risks and dive into those deeper more serene artistic waters. 

Someday, Goat Simulator will be taught as a MANDATORY course in art schools around the world. A masterpiece of Dadaist satire that Duchamp would have been proud of and thought of as naturally, the spiritual successor to his groundbreaking Urinal masterwork known as "Fountain" from 1917. 

This PROFOUND insight into the human condition really elevates Goat Simulator to the highest of literary prose. 
I cannot recommend Goat Simulator enough!

it all began in late 2015, i was a youngling (4 years old) on an old kindle fire that barely worked. i had the goat simulator moblie edition but they were a lot different than what we have today for our mobile version. none of the games were connected in any sense. what i mean by this is, i had to install all the dlcs as different games and when i clicked on, say goatville, you couldnt load up mmo or waste of space (etc etc) from it unless you closed the game and went to those ones. 

my favorite of them all to play was mmo because at the time i thought it was an actual online game. i didnt learn until years later that it was not but had so much fun nonetheless. i always ran the microwave class for the explode ability. i hunted down for every goat across every game and completed my quest in early 2016. i was so happy when i got that last goat. it was the one in GoatZ when you survive enough days and it was some sort of magician i think, i dont play GoatZ often enough anymore.

these days, i still think of goat simulator and have even bought and played goat sim 3 with the digital downgrade dlc, that was in 2023 i think. im happy that games like these will be perserved on game stores for all generations to enjoy and hope that all generations DO enjoy this absolute masterpiece of a franchise.

A rather ridiculous, physics-based surgery game, in which you wonkily control a single arm.

[hr][/hr]
You can move your arm around and contract its fingers, with your biggest enemy being the clunkiness of the controls. Your movement feels floaty and grabbing an object often results in holding nothing or whatever lay next to the object. Naturally, this doesn’t mix well with the task of transplanting organs without killing your patient.

Missions are quite varied, having you perform transplants of different organs, each with its own anatomical challenges. Every kind of surgery is also available in a variety of locations, with objects and your arm being sent flying from side to side in a moving ambulance or everything floating around freely inside a space station. Keeping your patient from bleeding out will often be less of a challenge than not losing your tools and the organs you’re meant to implant.

The game also challenges you with completing surgeries in the smallest amount of time and/or harming your patient as little as possible to set new high scores and earn achievements. This gives it quite a bit of replay value, beyond trying out different tools and fooling around with your unconscious patient.

Surgeon Simulator’s main menu is also navigated just like the surgeries, having you use your arm to interact with objects on a desk. This gives you a surprisingly fun playground, with more and more objects being added to it as you complete each mission.

The game features a fantastic soundtrack, which kicks your mind right into performance mode. Combined with its decent graphics and fitting soundscapes, the game feels quite satisfying to play. Finishing a level also really feels like you accomplished something.

[hr][/hr]
Surgeon Simulator is hilarious, challenging and certainly unique.

How to make crack cocaine

1: Mix cocaine and baking soda at a 4:1 ratio (4 parts cocaine to one part baking soda). Make sure they are chopped into fine powder and mixed well, then combine them into the glass container.

2: Add a few drops of water until the mixed powder becomes like thin mud.

3: Heat the bottom of the container, evenly, until the entire mixture begins to boil.

4: Once the mix starts to boil, remove from heat and let the mixture cool. After cooling, it should be a mass of hardened crack that still has bubbles of air-pockets in it from boiling.

5: Remove the air bubbles. To remove the bubble air-pockets in the crack rock, simply add more water into the glass container and reheat the mixture until you have a solid yellow crack-blob floating in the water.

6: Let the water and crack-blob cool, then remove the hardened mass.

7: Your crack is now ready to be packaged and sold.

My rating for the game is neutral, but I can’t recommend it.

Persona 5 Royal is an enhanced re-release including all DLC from the original, gameplay changes, and major story additions. It is without a doubt a masterpiece, and as someone who plays lots of JRPGs, this game sits among my favourites. I will not reiterate things most people have said already, but everything from the music, gameplay, art style, and story is phenomenal. Despite having played Persona 5 vanilla and Royal on my PS4, I enjoyed my third playthrough on Steam almost as much as the first time I experienced the game.

Compared to vanilla Persona 5, Royal has a myriad of additions and changes that I all loved. The new character, Kasumi ([spoiler]or her real name, Sumire[/spoiler]), turned out to be my favourite girl in the game, and the extended story and new palace were an amazing experience that elevated the ending. With the addition of the third semester, the party can receive third-tier personas, which are a great callback to the first awakenings and give a strong skill upgrade. The grappling hook is also an amazing addition, and I am a sucker for hook mechanics in games. Every palace has been altered to incorporate it and makes exploration more fun. The new will seeds located in palaces are sometimes hidden behind grapple points and make the flow of exploration more interesting as well. Another addition that stood out to me and improved battles was Showtime Attacks that deal massive damage along with a fun and flashy animation to go along with it.

I only have a few complaints with this game, and even then it is not really a huge deal. While I think the story is mostly well paced, the start is a bit slow even if it does a good job of establishing things. I did not really mind it that much on my first playthrough, but upon replays it felt a bit like a slog. My other complaint is that I kind of find mementos boring. Sometimes I like it because it is a great place to easily level up to max with Ryuji's instant kill ability and lets me fight again before another palace in the story, but it is a slog to complete all stamps for Jose because they're randomized. The map is also designed like Persona 3's Tartarus, which is kind of mediocre and uninteresting, but at least it helps me partially shut my brain off and watch something on the side while I collect all stamps. Lastly, I think merciless difficulty is way too easy. Due to the damage multiplier, I can kill most things with one attack aimed at a weakness, and it provides less of a challenge than hard difficulty.

In the end, Persona 5 Royal is a fantastic game that I would highly recommend to both JRPG fans and beginners looking to get into one or experience some more. Despite Persona 4 being my favourite, Persona 5 clearly has the best gameplay and is the easiest difficulty-wise. Even though I am the type to start or recommend things in release order, Persona 5 is self-contained like Persona 3 and 4, and since it is well refined, I can comfortably recommend this as a good starting point.

If you're looking for a turn-based JRPG that completely respects your time while blowing you away visually, this is it. Persona 5 Royal takes an already incredible game and refines it to near perfection.

The Art & Design are absolutely unmatched. From the brilliant UI (even the shop menus look like high-fashion magazines) to the vibrant, comic-book-inspired aesthetic, the game oozes style from every single pixel. The soundtrack effortlessly ties the whole visual vibe together.

The Battle System is incredibly fluid and satisfying. The turn-based combat is snappy, fast-paced, and rewards tactical thinking. Exploiting enemy weaknesses to trigger a "1 More" or executing a stylized All-Out Attack never gets old, even a hundred hours in.
I never see this coming

I'm not sure what exactly to say about this game. I took over my life for months. I would think about it constantly. Wonder what turns the plot would take next. Try to plan what I would do when I got home from work, or got up in the morning.
It's an RPG, but it's not your typical RPG. It cares more about you going shopping with your friend, or helping someone work in the garden then it does about grinding for XP or getting a better weapon. There are so many intricate systems, and they are all intertwined.
At the heart of the game are the stories. The main plot (OMG, so huge, so epic), and the stories of all the characters. The game rewards you with various bonuses (from extra stats, to someone to do your laundry for you) for progressing these stories (confidants). 
Beautiful game. Absolutely love it. It will steal your heart if you let it.
One warning: the game is full of insane twists, if you get a walkthrough/guide (I did) make sure it is spoiler free. You do not want to be robed of any of the fun this game has to offer.
game is peak 100% recommend

Persona 5 is a good game overall. I have many small nitpicks and a few gripes, but besides that it's well done. I do believe it's slightly overhyped. 

The story has deep commentary on society that makes you think, but only if you're willing as it's never explicitly gone deeper in the game. Some themes are heavy but often juxtaposed with playful banter from characters, which can take away from the gravity of the situation at hand. While appropriate at times, I feel like there are important moments where it takes away from a character's development or authenticity. The overarching plot is very well written though, I highly recommend playing/looking into it. 

Mechanics are fun! Weaknesses, crits, baton passes, etc... were incredibly fun! I wish the game tapped into ailments more, there is a long line of bosses/mini bosses that are immune to it for what feels like 'just because'. I also feel like items aren't introduced well, but at the same time it's pretty intuitive.

The pacing becomes pretty poor near the ending parts. Some areas can become incredibly grueling and grindy. There were times where I felt like the game was dragging on for too long, yet couldn't save at that specific time, and often got very frustrated wondering why the specific part or even the whole games wasn't over yet. It's incredibly important to take your time, as it can be easy to get frustrated. There is also un-skippable dialogue scenes as well as general dialogue that just feels unneeded or annoying.

World building is pretty good, but I never found myself entranced or overly attached to characters. That being said I did not finish every single confidant, but still found this notable.

The art, music, and sound effects are phenomenal, ATLUS cooked with these!!

I strongly dislike the fact that you can romance adults and your own teacher in this game. The main character is a minor, and I feel like people often overlook this a lot!! It's very odd!!

TLDR: 
-Fun mechanics.
-Take your time, it can get grindy and annoying, so be sure to take breaks! Pacing can be grueling.
-PHENOMENAL MUSIC!!
-Good overall story. Psychology and social commentary themes.

This game is extremely fun (also a bit infuriating at times). I am new to PC gaming. Hollow Knight is the first game I am playing on PC. I have also never played a game like Hollow Knight before. So this has been all sorts of a learning curve for me. However, it has been such a rewarding experience. The way the game leads you into learning the mechanics feels so good. It does not hold your hand, but progresses you through different scenarios where you are required to have more precision with the mechanics. Sometimes you just have to think outside of the box to progress to certain areas. The same can be said for combat as you come across new creatures. Since I am new to PC and this type of game, I am constantly getting my butt whooped in fights. Or, I take damage from the same creatures that I fought I at the beginning of the game that don't even attack. However, this is not a problem with the game its just not what I am used to playing. So, beware. This game is not for the faint of heart, but man is it rewarding to progress through.

coming off the heels of a long line of souls likes. this game was such a nice refreshing game to play.

i can say, it is not a souls like. it is dark and grim, but playing it again, and with a ton of souls experience, it is just a difficult 2d platforming metroidvania. but i think it got labeled a souls like just because it is hard.

just like dust, this game is all hand drawn and it is gorgeous for that. areas are unique enough but can kind of blend together a little bit. even though the areas all have variation and are completely different from one another, everything does happen underground.

the music is great. it is strong and sharp and clear and it stands out especially in boss fights. there area a good amount of bosses which have their own unique look to them, and there are a ton of different enemy types. even if reusing some designs, they change them in a way that visually makes them stand out, especially since they get new moves or abilities as you progress.

even though i haven't 100% the game, it has so much content to it that you can absolutely keep entertained by it for a long time, and many playthroughs. since you kind of can choose to progress in almost any way you feel. that being easier once you have knowledge of the game and where things are.

the gameplay is VERY good. it is fast and sharp and nothing feels like it has delay or is sluggish, or you have some weird momentum or anything. it is so fine tuned that it really makes getting into you flow state in this game feel so good.

This is a Masterpiece! 
It's art in the best sense: entertaining you with a gripping story and interesting characters, humour and sincerity, hope and hopelessness in an unknown world much like ours - while inviting you to take a stance on a number of social isues, cultural issues, philosophical issues. Making you think, making you laugh, making you feel.

I didn't know what to expect when I started the game, stumbled into it blindly and was instantly sucked in. (Guess I was living under a rock - now I realize it's a pretty well known game. So maybe, when you're going in expecting something grande, it won't have the same effect on you of course. But I think it will still be really good.)
The writing, the voice acting, the graphic style that fits so well in a kinda post-apocalyptic setting - everything is just so well done!

Sometimes the political and geographical world-building was a bit much for me. Even though I admired the creativity, I found myself impatiently clicking faster through some topics because I wanted the story to go on. Of course there is no need to go through all dialogue options, but often it will give you experience points you can use to level up your skills. And sometimes something interesting might pop up - so I clicked through everything available to me even if I was already about to loose patience and wanted to move on. So ultimately I did this to myself and can't really complain. It also still contributed to that world feeling more complete and real.

Honestly I just love this game, go and give it a try!

At first, the game seems very interesting: it features intriguing concepts, a captivating opening, a fascinating world begging to be explored, colorful characters, and an excellent visual style. However, at a certain point, the game enters a "bad" phase where progress either grinds to a complete halt or it becomes incredibly difficult to figure out exactly what needs to be done to move forward. The problem lies in the fact that, within this vast open world, there is often only a single specific location that serves as a "progress trigger." For instance, you might have skipped a dialogue with a particular character, deeming it unimportant or uninteresting yet that very conversation was essential for the plot to advance. Consequently, you end up spending over an hour scouring the entire world in search of a way to proceed; and when you finally stumble upon the right spot, all the interest you previously held in the game has completely evaporated during that wasted hour. The game ceases to be an engaging adventure filled with discovery; instead, it transforms into a world where you spend hours treading water, making absolutely no headway. Ultimately, even once you get back on the path of progress, you no longer derive the same enjoyment from the game as you did before. At least, that was my experience.

İLKEL SÜRÜNGEN BEYNİ: Bir bilgisayar oyununa saplanmış hâlde dünyayı yıkıcı eleştirilerinle dizginlediğin bir yanılsamanın içindesin uzun zamandır. Kolay para arzusuyla hareket edenlerden, kadınlarını kaybetme korkusundan dolayı bin bir şekle giren delikanlılardan ve sermayeyi sürekli garanti altında tutması için erkekleri sonsuz bir ıstırabın gölgesinde serinletmeye çalışan kadınlardan kaçmak için saatlerdir buradasın. Seni hüzne aşık eden bir sistemin seslerini duymamak için de burada var olduğun anlara şahit oldum. Bir hayalin zihninde ışıldamasını istemediğini öğrendim bu aralıkta. Karaya ulaştığın vakit geri dönmek için yalvardığın fakat birkaç dakika içinde boğulacak şekilde evrimleştiğin için dizlerinin bağını çözecek kadar seni depresif yapan bir hayal. Üzgünüm.

[b]CONCEPTUALIZATION[/b] [Medium: Success] – Imagine a world painted in the colours of an oil spill on wet asphalt. It is beautiful, it is toxic, and it is entirely beyond saving. The *Décadence* is in the air. This is Revachol. This is [i]Disco Elysium[/i]. It is not a game; it is an interactive autopsy of the 20th century.

[b]RHETORIC[/b] [Challenging: Success]- And what do you do with that breath of yours? You use it to tear down every altar humanity has ever built. The game is a slaughterhouse for ideologies. It constructs brilliant, airtight arguments for Communism, Fascism, Ultraliberalism, and Moralism, only to hand you the hammer to smash them. It is a merciless satire that spares absolutely no one—not even the fervent, aching leftist beliefs of its own creators. It mocks the Fascist for his fragility, the Communist for his nostalgia, and the Moralist for his cowardice. It even mocks the player for wanting to be a hero in a world that only needs a janitor. It tears down the Fourth Wall and uses the bricks to pelt the *audience*!

[b]DRAMA[/b] [Medium: Success] – Oh, what a *magnificent*, heart-rending tragedy, sire! A play in three agonizing acts! The starving artists pour their souls onto the stage, only for the theater to be stolen by the men who count the ticket stubs! Raider investors! Villains of the lowest, most uninspired order!

[b]RHETORIC[/b] [Formidable: Success] – It is the ultimate, sickening punchline to their own *dialectic*. They warned *you*, didn't they? Right there in the text. Capital has the ability to subsume all critiques into itself. The money-men didn't just steal a company; they commodified the very anti-capitalist lament that made the work matter. They bought the revolution and fired the revolutionaries.

[b]AUTHORITY[/b] [Challenging: Success] – It’s a mutiny! A total lack of respect for the chain of command! The creators—the authors—were the rightful kings of this wretched dockside. They allowed soft-handed, dead-eyed usurpers to change the locks on their own [i]citadel[/i]. A king must hold his crown with an iron grip, or a merchant will simply buy it out from under him by his own money!

[b]RHETORIC[/b] [Formidable: Success] – Careful, Authority. Don’t canonize them just yet. Let’s not pretend the original creators were saints living in a socialist utopia. It was a meat-grinder of ego, crunch, and *artistic temperament*. They commodified the class struggle, wrote a brilliant manifesto against exploitation, and simultaneously crushed their own proletariat beneath their boots. You couldn't pen a more perfect, cynical deconstruction of modern ideological hypocrisy if you tried.

[b]CONCEPTUALIZATION[/b] [Legendary: Success] – It leaves a profound, aching sadness. The artifact —the game itself — remains a singular, luminous achievement. A mirror held up to a broken world. But the glass is cracked, and the hands that held it are bleeding. You play [i]Disco Elysium[/i] now not just as a detective solving a murder, but as a witness to the murder of the art itself.

Disco Elysium isn't a detective game, not entirely at least. It is a game about a sad, loser detective who got so messed up over the course of his life that he decided that getting so drunk that you forget who you are as a person while in the middle of a murder case was, in fact, the right way to go. It isn't a story about the fantastical, about grand discoveries (well...), about the extraordinary. It is about people in a city in a world which has reached a boiling point due to the brewing political wars which have existed for centuries, millennia even. It is about a man, a drunkard, a bastard who can't grapple with his past re-learning the very meaning of existence. It is about him internalizing, discarding, and re-internalizing different thoughts until he comes to a sound enough conclusion that fits what he now chooses to believe and work towards with this morbid second chance he has gotten. 
The game doesn't shy away from being overly political and outright calling out everything wrong with every ideology under the sun, but don't let that fool you into thinking that the game isn't woke in any regard. The creators are communists, the most respectable characters in the game are those who are what we define in our current world to be "woke", the most laughable characters in the entire game are those who fall anywhere to the right of the midpoint of the political compass, all while still showing that on an individual level these people aren't the worst things to grace the earth. And don't think they don't critique communism either. That's the thing with communism. Complaining about other communists is one of the most important parts of being a communist. The world reflects this too, it is a beautiful mess of a world which has such incredible depth and breadth that you could write your own textbook detailing the history of the world from start to finish with all of the minute details included as well. The world of Disco is beautiful, and the politics only heighten your care for it.
The philosophies of the game are bleak. There is no grand turn around for many characters. For some it's a degradation, a spiral into something worse. For others it's the small chance for something better amid the chaos. For the main character... Well that all depends on how he (you) internalize it. In what is my favorite sub-plot of the game, there is a certain someone who our MC mourns. It is someone who is still very much alive, someone who in years he has yet to fully move on from. It is messy, and in the end there is no closure. There is no grand be all end all. Everything already happened. There is no forgetting it. You exist in this world and they exist in it somewhere else, away from you, with someone else, and they are happy. Without you. All you can do is put one foot in front of the other and keep going. Something beautiful is going to happen. It is a message of hope in disparaging times, to keep going even when it seems like there is no point. Because in spite it all, you have a vast, oceanic soul. You have to keep going and let people hear about it, and allow yourself to hear about their souls as well.
In other, more miniscule notes; The music is gorgeous. Sea Power outdid themselves with the soundtrack for this one. The writing is genius, I don't really have anything more I can say about it besides how constantly I was blown away. The gameplay was amazing. Maybe I am insane but the fact that this was basically just an interactive book with very little action really hooked me more than if there were constant firefights going on. The skill and thoughts system really played into the politics and philosophies of the game perfectly. I can say wholeheartedly that it is my favorite implementation of a skill system I've experienced in a game because of how well it ties into the rest of the story. The art style is gorgeous as well. It has given me that drive to pick back up the brush and practice again. This time I might stick to it.
As a final closing remark, I really don't think this game, like my other favorite game, Outer Wilds, is for everyone. It requires a very specific type of person to enjoy it an I'm thankful that I was that kind of person. It resonated with me deeply and even when I took longer breaks from it I found it impossible to not stop thinking about it and analyzing it. If you are interested the please PLAY this game, maybe steer away from the capital aspect. I'll leave it at that. I hadn't known about the major controversy before getting it. 
I'll leave a quote here, my personal favorite from the game. One that I think encapsulates everything about it, especially the lead up. You may not understand it, but hopefully it gives the push for you to want to play the game to understand it. (of course it is just a small snippet and the most memorable part. The entire quote is far too long to justify fitting into this review.)
"I NEED YOU. YOU CAN KEEP ME ON THIS EARTH. BE VIGILANT. I LOVE YOU." - LA REVACHOLIERE
Extra note: The MC is my favorite written character in fiction without a doubt.

I've held off of reviewing Disco Elysium for a long while. I fear that whatever I try to say will be worth nothing and it'll never capture the feeling I had playing this living novel. I try anyways. Its a different kind of special, its my favorite game ever as of now and it doesn't even feel like one, I want ten more things like this and recognize that that will never happen. 

It is writing first and foremost, you will be reading for hours on end. The narration and art make up so much but the gameplay loop is reading on reading. I love it personally, if you will is to be determined. 

I've loved and continue to love the city of Revachol, I've played as a detective twice through this world and still struggle to fully remember all of the parts that make up this city. Expansive, overwhelming, I'm completely in awe and still there is a another half of this world another side of these characters I'll never see with how I played through the game. 

The soundtrack is haunting, its innately nostalgic I feel and when one of its many pieces fall upon my youtube playlists I am taken back to a place of misery and solace. When I listen I can almost make out the many powerful voice performances that plague this game ringing in my ear. Pictures in my mind of the portraits dutifully rendering the casts of this experience, you will meet someone with a halo like effect behind their face; I have cemented them as a point of reference for the ideal character in all of media. 

The beats of the Whirling-in-Rags play, I walk to the streets of Martinaise and Shivers tell me of a district known by its bullet hole filled walls, cold, collapsing, hopeful. I miss it already and I'll always will.

Logic: "This game has a lot of intrigue and makes interesting meta-commentary. It covers a wide range of topics, and makes hyperbolic but accurate statements. I think it is good."

Encyclopaedia: "As far as I'm aware, barely any of the original names get money from this due to a hostile corporate takeover. You can probably get it 'cheaper' elsewhere.

Conceptualization: "You have to have a very high IQ to understand Disco Elysium."

Inland Empire: "It's a psychedelic kaleidoscope of suffering and beauty!"

Half Light: "What the hell did that last guy say? I don't like big words. Kill him."

Electrochemistry: "Why are you SOBER??? Play it with your favourite drugs or go the ♥♥♥♥ home!"

Shivers: "Remain vigilant. I love you."

Composure: "The game is good and I THINK you can safely recommend it."

Savoir Faire: "Dance. Dance like your life depends on it!"

[b] Overhyped. [/b]

I have been trying to finish this since it first came out in 2020. I put 10-15 hours in initially and fell off. I then came back during some exams, fell of again and finally, in 2026 I have finished it.

This is a mammoth game and I really admire what is being attempted here. It is unique and there is nothing else quite like it. However, for every interesting moment or thread there is another inconsequential plotline that pulls you away from the main event. The issue is those lesser threads can last for hours at a time. 

The story of a damaged cop trying to solve a case is nothing new, but the way this game handles him with his constant internal monologue is genius. The internal thoughts bounce around off each other and everything responds to every decision you make. I would love to see the developers written plan for the script, it must be insane.

The game looks great, almost like a moving tapestry and the music is haunting and well utilised throughout. What was unexpected is that throughout the games more sombre moments, this is not a happy game, were the moments of comedy which were nicely interspersed.

This game does crawl at a snails pace and it is all dialogue driven. There is no action or any traditional gameplay. There are parts where you will be locked in and parts where you will be beating your head against the wall in boredom. There is an incredibly large focus on politics and communism which I will admit did not really engage me. 

Performance wise I have run this on Mac and PC and have not noticed any issue apart from one hard crash near the end, but the game had luckily quick saved before it.

This one is a hard one to recommend and has probably been overhyped to a certain extent. Did I enjoy it? I think so? I think everyone should give this a go but be prepared to eject if it does not float your boat as it is a slow, slow, slow burn.

Pros
- I really liked the art style of character portraits and overall color palette
- The voice acting is top notch and the effort put is very noticeable 
- A lot of different styles of play are available. For example you can focus on leveling up physical, mental, or emotional traits, all of which come with different pros and cons to help you solve the case.
- The story itself (murder mystery) is solid and there are many different way on going about solving it, depending on the traits you choose to level up and how you interact with the other characters.
- Had quite a few laughs playing this came. Characters say some absurd stuff in this game (in a good way)
- A lot of political and moral philosophies are presented throughout the game, which gives a lot to think about. It's also not done in an overly annoying way or trying to be preachy which is nice.

Cons
- One of the most poorly optimized games I've ever played. For what the graphics are and what the gameplay consists of (walking around and talking) it was frying my CPU. I have a 3090 and a i9-12900, and this game would get my CPU to 90 degrees Celsius or higher. Which is insane.
- Game is bit buggy, had to restart game a few times throughout the playthrough because camera would randomly get locked or character walking animation would get bugged and wouldn't move properly. Not the biggest deal in the world but became more and more annoying as the game went on.
- It's also possible to get soft/hard locked where you can no longer progress and have to go back to a previous load spot in the game. This almost made me give up, I had to look up how to get another dice roll to continue the story as I had done all the side quests available up to that point and had no way of leveling up anymore.

Overall I had a great time "playing" this game and would recommend to anyone that likes narrative rich and focused games. Take into account that this game is essentially a walking/reading simulator and the only gameplay is walking/exploring and dialogue options. With that being said it was 1 of the best walking simulators I have ever played, with solid writing, character depth and options. 8/10

Where do I even begin. Disco Elysium is a game that has been sitting in my backlog for years, and I regret not finishing it sooner. First off, this game is beautiful. The art in this game is so stylistically unique from anything I had ever seen before. Each character portrait simultaneously conforms to the overarching aesthetic and yet instantly recognizable. 

The game play is what kept me from finishing the game. The beginning of the game is slow. There were times in the beginning where I was just aimless wondering around the world. But this game definitely makes up for its slow start. In fact, the narrative rewards dedication and experimenting. It took me a bit to truly understand the skill tree but, once I did, I was quickly figuring out the plot.

The political commentary obviously was the highlight of this game. I was definitely confused how this game would incorporate its political message after only a few hours into the game. At the beginning it was hard to see how they would fit an explicitly communist ideology into a detective game. But ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, they did. Marx would be so proud. The political commentary is so perfectly written. [spoiler]Dros exposing your hypocrisy at the end, being a communist cop and all, was so intelligently written. He was a dying old man who had a lot of time to articulate his political ideology. He so perfectly dissected Harry.[/spoiler]

This game also made me sob like a little ♥♥♥♥♥ when [spoiler]Harry's girlfriend left him in his dream[/spoiler].

Undertale isn’t just a game — it’s an experience that stays with you long after the credits roll.

At first it looks like a simple retro RPG, but the characters, music, humor, and emotional moments are on another level. Every choice actually matters, and the game constantly surprises you in ways most RPGs never do. The soundtrack is genuinely one of the best I’ve heard in gaming.

What makes Undertale special is how attached you become to the world and its characters. One moment you’re laughing at a joke, and the next you’re questioning every decision you made.

Funny, emotional, creative, and unforgettable.

10/10 — you owe it to yourself to play this at least once.

“What Remains of Edith Finch” was my first experience into narrative-driven games, and I have to admit that it didn’t resonate with me as much as I’d hoped.

While the game tells a compelling and emotional story, there’s very little in the way of actual gameplay. You’re essentially a passive observer, walking through a predetermined series of events without any real influence or control. The interactivity is minimal, and for someone who prefers engaging mechanics or player-driven choices, this can feel underwhelming.

That said, the story itself is well-crafted, with a haunting atmosphere and creative storytelling techniques. As someone who enjoys narrative movies, I could easily see this working better as a short film rather than a game. It’s a beautifully told tale, but not quite what I look for in a gaming experience.

If you're a fan of interactive storytelling with minimal gameplay, this might be your thing. But if you're seeking a more traditional game experience, I wouldn't recommend it

For those few of you who don't know, What Remains of Edith Finch is a story driven narrative experience that is also a video game. It tells the story of a single family line's history and its many tragedies. If you have never heard of this game I would encourage you to go in blind and play it through. You don't have to have experience with video games to enjoy your playthrough. It doesn't take any kind of skill to make it through to the end. This is an interactive story driven experience, not a test of video game skills.

Words cannot describe how beautiful this game is in both music and visuals. The unfolding story really tugs at your heartstrings and the ending is also very moving.

Anyway, thank you for reading my review and congratulations if you're about to play What Remains of Edith Finch for the first time. You won't regret it.

What Remains of Edith Finch is a masterful narrative experience that blends imaginative storytelling with simple, elegant interactions. The way it presents its story is exceptional; I was particularly captivated by the inventive chapter where gameplay is framed within a comic book, allowing you to both read the commentary and physically play out the scene across its pages. The game’s minimalist puzzles serve the story perfectly, never disrupting its haunting and beautiful flow. However, the experience is also profoundly visceral. As someone with a significant fear of heights, one segment involving a swing built inexplicably on a seaside cliff was so effectively immersive that it crossed from genius into genuine dread, perfectly illustrating the game's power to make its family's tragic lore feel intensely personal.

Overall, I came in with lowered expectations just because "how can it be all that good?", but I have to admit that they managed to combine a great narrative experience, fun and varied minigames, a deeper mystery, with a great sound design.

The first thing I'd like to compliment is the minigames, which I would say no two are that alike, and that all of the minigames actually contributed to the story (unlike some other games where the gameplay feels disconnected or just simply exists to say "hey we have a game here and not a movie")

The narration is great - definitely top tier.

And finally I think the overall story was well done in that there is an overall mystery to answer, there wasn't an over-explanation of ertain elements, and there's a fair amount of emotion as well as touching upon some darker elements of life

A short, impactful story-based walking simulator with occasional minigames that are used to tell the story.
It's a bit of an emotional experience as you recount the final moments of your past family members, young and old. Each life you see adds more depth to the story and it feels kind of real.

Visuals-wise, it looks very good, the environments are very well detailed and the game runs surprisingly well. If you're an achievement hunter, all of the achievements are very easy to get, it really doesn't take very long. The gameplay isn't very demanding, it's kind of chill.

Overall it's a wonderful experience, and I definitely recommend it to anyone who's willing to try it. The game is around two hours in length, so I would recommend buying the game on sale instead of full price.

As someone who has played this, and other paradox games a lot, I can confidently say Europa IV is an amazing and complex game for any strategy or grand strategy enjoyer out there. It’s not an easy game to get into, to learn. I remember spending months slowly learning the game and discovering new things about it even after years playing it. It’s deep and its systems don’t always make total sense. It has a board game feel to it that the new Europa V doesn’t have much. 

It’s distinct and an all-time classic for sure. 

The best thing about it? If you ever get tired of the vanilla game, you can just download awesome mods, like Anbennar, and spend way more hours in it than in vanilla.
Best game of all time. Worth spending thousands of hours on. Beware.

Europa Universalis IV is the high point of strategy. I love strategy games but I don't think I've ever found another game nearly as good as EUIV, not even its successor (which is quite bad imo).

The tutorials are bad, or at least they were years ago when I first played. I also have hundreds more hours on this game than it looks, I've played it for a very long time. (I played on another person's account at first) So it will take a long time to get a good hang of the game. For me, I had to mess around and use cheats in an attempt to understand the mechanics. I play without those now, but still find I have no clue how things work sometimes. It's just so deep you never fully understand it.

But few games let you do what this game does. Want to form Germany with Norway? You can do that. Want to invade Europe with the Aztecs? You can do that. Want to colonize the New World as India? Well, you won't be called India (it's a very diverse region) but you can do that.

My current run involves me playing as a Pueblo tribe in New Mexico and forming a theocrasy and declaring crusades against Florida, while I'm still a Totemist nation. Runs like that are why this game is so infinitely fun.

There are definitely downsides, like how you have to spend a fortune to own all the DLC. But the game is a blast with only a few basic DLC, so pick it up on sale with those DLC and you will have so much fun even if you can never buy the others. If you are a strategy lover, this is a must play.

Europa Universalis IV is, at its core, an excellent grand strategy experience, deep, rewarding, and still one of the best digital kriegsspiel style sandboxes out there. The way diplomacy, economy, and warfare interlock makes it genuinely satisfying to learn and master, and it’s the kind of game you keep coming back to for years.

But it’s hard to ignore the business model: the sheer volume and cost of DLC, combined with a subscription just to access what arguably feels like the “complete” version of the game, comes off as excessive. More fundamentally, the underlying engine 'Clausewitz' is showing its age, performance bottlenecks, especially tied to single core limitations, feel increasingly out of place given how successful Paradox Interactive has been. With that level of revenue, it’s reasonable to expect more investment into modernizing the tech stack. So while it’s still a great game, and one I genuinely enjoy playing, it also feels important to be honest: the monetization and technical limitations are real issues, and I hope they’re addressed in future titles rather than carried forward.

NOTE FOR THE PEOPLE WHO PAY THE SUBSCRIPTION FOR DLC : even owning a permanent license doesn’t give u real ownership since it can technically be revoked anytime, so paying a subscription just to access them for a month is legit an even bigger scam, only pay for it if you really think this is right : 'you'll own nothing and be happy'.

Doki Doki Literature Club! is not just a regular visual novel; it is a total game-changer that completely rewrites the rules of storytelling, and I am entirely, unapologetically, and universally obsessed with it. It hides behind a cute, colorful exterior, but underneath is a masterpiece of psychological depth and brilliant design. The game takes standard romance tropes and transforms them into an unforgettable artistic experience that completely grips you from start to finish, to the point where it has practically taken over my entire life, my mind, my soul, and my entire daily routine. I do not just play this game; I breathe it, I live it, and I let it consume every single waking second of my existence on this planet.I absolutely love how the characters are written with an incredible amount of detail. I found myself genuinely caring about what happens to them. I adore these characters so much that they honestly feel more real to me than actual people sometimes, and I would gladly cross dimensions and protect them at all costs. My loyalty to this literature club is completely infinite and unshakable. On top of that, the hidden lore in this game is absolutely massive, and exploring it has been an amazing experience. It is packed with secrets, subtle (or not so) clues, and hidden messages that reward you for paying close attention. This turns the game into a massive puzzle that keeps me thinking about it long after playing. Every single poem and line of dialogue feels like it was placed with absolute purpose, and I have spent countless late nights staring at the screen trying to unwrap every single layer of this universe, taking notes, and letting my brain completely melt over the genius of this world-building.What makes this game truly revolutionary is how it uses the actual mechanics of a video game to tell its story. It plays with expectations in ways that no other medium can copy. It is a stunning achievement in modern gaming, and it is easily one of my favorite games of all time. To be completely honest, this game has basically become the definitive meaning of life for me, the center of my universe, and the ultimate peak of human creation. I don't think any other piece of media will ever top the absolute perfection of this masterpiece, and I will gladly scream my love for this game into the void until the end of time itself.

i love this game to death. ignore my playtime, as i've done many, many modded playthroughs of this game over the years that do not show up here. 

anyways, i'm not entirely sure if i have ever related to a set of characters more than the cast in ddlc. i saw and continue to see so much of myself in the way these characters hold self hatred, guilt, depression, how they handle abuse in their lives, how they feel socially inept, the preconceived notions of worthlessness we struggle with inside of ourselves, and how our mental issues can really gnaw at us even when we want to share these things with the people around us, and yet are never able to, in fear of being rejected and thrown to the wayside, and confirming your own worst fears of being worthless through the other people you so desperately want to care about you.

if you have never played the salvation remake mod of this game, i think youre doing a very huge disservice to yourself, especially if you have ever struggled with mental health in your life and opening up to others. for me, it felt like a beam of sun had shone into my mind, and been depicted through this cast of characters, to the point I ended up crying full genuine sobs and ended up exploring my own loneliness and refusal to share with others what goes on in life in fear of being hurt. its an indescribable feeling how much i felt i was seeing the versions of my self being shown to me through all the trauma and unfortunate consequences of that trauma i've had to wade through for so long without being able to genuinely talk about it with anyone in my life without hiding parts of myself.

i really cant understate how much this game has meant to me over the years and how much i adore the characters, especially in the Plus version of this game with their backstories. Few things have really meant this much to me and have held this much value in trying to be more open with myself about the things i have gone through and continue to torment myself about in my own life.

I think this is definitely a game that isn't made for everyone, but if it is something that you end up enjoying and seeing yourself reflected back in, I think you'll love it as much as I did.

Sayori's struggle with trying to make others happy while battling with depression, Yuri's heavy addiction to self-harm and Natsuki's parental problems, every single one of their situations happens in real life, to real people. And that breaks my heart. I have never played a scarier and more dreadful game than Doki Doki Literature Club. It truly stays in your mind long after you finish it.

[h1]Helldivers 2[/h1]

[i]Recommended, especially if you have friends willing to die for democracy... or because of your terrible aim.[/i]

[hr][/hr]

[h2]Straight into hell, as advertised[/h2]

Helldivers 2 is addictive, chaotic and full of adrenaline. The name fits the experience perfectly, because most of the time it really does feel like you are diving straight into hell.

Bullets flying everywhere, explosions covering half the screen, enemies coming from every direction, teammates screaming, someone calling an airstrike in the worst possible place, and that lovely feeling that everything has gone horribly, horribly wrong.

In other words, democracy is working as intended.

[h2]Best played with friends[/h2]

When playing with friends, the game is excellent. That is where Helldivers 2 really shines.

A big part of the fun comes from the team chaos. The plan usually sounds great for about ten seconds, then someone panics, someone reloads at the wrong time, someone throws a stratagem too close, and suddenly the whole mission turns into a funeral with objectives.

Playing solo is not nearly as fun, at least for me. It is playable, of course, but a lot of the magic comes from the stupid situations that happen with other people. Especially when those situations somehow become your fault.

[h2]What the game does well[/h2]

[list]
[*][b]The gameplay is very good.[/b]

Shooting feels satisfying, movement feels good, and the missions usually manage to stay intense without becoming boring too quickly.

[*][b]It runs relatively well.[/b]

For a game with this much chaos on screen, performance is surprisingly decent most of the time.

[*][b]The graphics are nice.[/b]

The game looks good without trying too hard to be cinematic every five seconds. Explosions, planets, armour, enemies and effects all work well together.

[*][b]It is easy to learn, but hard to actually master.[/b]

At first, you think you understand the game. Then higher difficulties politely inform you that you do not.

[*][b]There is a good amount of content and grind.[/b]

If you enjoy unlocking things, upgrading gear, improving your loadout and watching numbers go up while pretending it is personal development, there is plenty here for you.
[/list]

[h2]The weak points[/h2]

[list]
[*][b]Random teammates can be painful.[/b]

If you play with randoms, sooner or later you will meet someone who makes you question democracy, humanity and your own life choices.

[*][b]There are still some bugs.[/b]

The game is much better now than it was before, but you can still run into bugs here and there. Nothing too shocking, but enough to remind you that perfection is apparently treason.

[*][b]Solo is not the best way to play.[/b]

You can play alone, but I honestly do not think that is where the game is at its strongest. Unless you enjoy suffering quietly like a proper gentleman.

[*][b]The game really depends on having a good squad.[/b]

With friends, it is fantastic. Without friends, it depends a lot on your patience and how much nonsense you can tolerate before closing the game and staring at the wall.
[/list]

[h2]Final thoughts[/h2]

Overall, Helldivers 2 is a very good, fun, chaotic and addictive game. It gives you great moments, stupid deaths, intense missions and the kind of teamwork that can either save the day or delete the entire squad in three seconds.

With friends, it is absolutely worth it.

Solo, it depends on your patience, your mood and your ability to remain calm while democracy collapses around you.

This game has been fun. However current issues keep piling up as the game's lifespan increases. Bug's are here and there that makes you question the stability, but not enough to turn you away so you bite through.

However the inconsistent way the game behaves makes you lose your cool and is increddibly frustrating to play with.

A couple examples:
 - You throw a strategem and when it lands it bounces of in a completly random direction sometimes losing it's inherent value due to misplacement. Some maps use "inaccessible" terrain and objects at eye height so you can't use those to position. But there is no way to determine what terrain is unsable and what is not.

 - Same issue applies to reinforcements. Sometime you can steer and make use of the strategic oppertunity of landing somewhere. other times you can't steer at all and get stuck in a completly useless position due to the pod not being able to steer over "inaccessable" terrain.

 - Large scale explosions and strategems behave inconsitently due to objects being able to block the whole explosion. Small rocks and even destructibles will sometime soak the damage and block you from completing certain objectives the first time. And there is no way of determining what can block what.
Example: Used a hellbomb on a shriekers nest and none of the nests got destroyed due to some small rocks (terrain, smaller then a helldiver) blocking the explosion.

 - Enemies despawn at random even when you engage them
 - Enemies spawn right on top of you sometimes when you engage with enemy bases or objectives (and not their reinforcements)
 - corpses interact weirdly with all kinds of things, especially in multiplayer

I'm disregarding balance of the game cause that's a whole different discussion and to me balance isn't the main issue I'm facing.
But every faction has things that are so incredibly inconsistent that it frustrates you
Add on that the bugs of getting through maps, underneath terrain, being unable to climb certain small ledges and bumps, etc...

It takes the fun right out of it. At this point it could use a lot more shoe polish instead of bringing big updates and balance changes with warbonds

Guys. Bottom line up front - you've got lightning in a bottle. Love the game. I keep coming back. I've bought every single warbond. But please. I know friendly fire is a feature, not a bug. Please do something about turret friendly fire. Maybe turrets can't - and shouldn't be - perfect. I get that vision. But it is very consistent that I get cut in half by nearly every sentry on the battlefield. I don't think turrets should be safe. I just want them to be smarter. AI is a thing. Automated targeting is a thing. Their target prioritization seems overly indifferent to Helldiver positioning. I get that positioning your turrets is a "skill issue", but felt player experience is that they AIM for Helldivers.

Also, please get rid of the Orbital Fluctuations modifier. It's literally gimping the very game mechanics that make your game fun. It is anti-fun. It plagues 70% of matches. Nearly everyone hates it.

Helldivers 2 is a fun, hectic and cinematic game. My friends and I have had a lot of fun playing it and spreading managed democracy. All that being said, the developers Arrowhead have really dropped the ball. 

They do nothing but lie to their player base and make empty promises to do better. Whenever they claim to give us a buff, it is met with other nerfs that are never announced and left to data miners to find and publish on the helldivers wiki. 

There is too much emphasis on War Bonds with no time dedicated to fixing the numerous bugs in the game, nor taking the time to make sure the content they do release is fully functional. 

Dev blogs are almost mocking in nature, dismissing player concerns while offering little substance. 

This is all the more frustrating since Helldivers 2 is a genuinely fun game yet like the Titanic, I am watching it slowly sink with the constant bad decisions of the puppet masters.

Until Arrowhead can pulls out the arrow affecting their current line of thinking and make better choices for the game I.E. LISTENING TO THEIR PLAYERS AND ACTING ACCORDINGLY, I cannot in good faith recommend this game.

Subject: Urgent Refund Request for "The Day Before" – Unfulfilled Promises and Studio Closure

Dear Customer Support,

I hope this message finds you well. I am reaching out to express my deep disappointment with the recent release of "The Day Before," a game I eagerly anticipated for five long years. The promises made by the developers and the subsequent closure of the studio have left me and countless other players in a state of frustration and disillusionment.

A recent Forbes article by Paul Tassi sheds light on the disheartening reality of the situation, highlighting the disparities between what was promised and what was delivered in the game. The article can be found here:

https://www.forbes.com/sites/paultassi/2023/12/08/the-day-before-is-real-but-lied-about-everything-else/?sh=1aa6b76e574b

Furthermore, the official announcement from the studio, FntasticHQ, regarding the closure can be accessed through their Twitter account:

https://twitter.com/FntasticHQ/status/1734265789237338453

It is disheartening to note that the game has violated numerous rules of early access, particularly in terms of making specific promises about future events and failing to set accurate expectations. The discrepancy between the promised MMO features, substantiated by video evidence, and the actual content upon release is evident in this YouTube video:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TmwmN8epgQU&ab_channel=Eroktic

Given the circumstances, I kindly request a refund for my purchase. While I would prefer a refund through my original payment method, I am open to receiving Steam funds if necessary.

I appreciate your prompt attention to this matter and hope for a swift resolution. Thank you for your understanding.

Sincerely,

[Your Name]

This is more of a 'ADD FU***NG THIS!' feature list for the developers @FNTASTIC.

So here goes FNTASTIC. I'll make this nice and short for your Developers/Volunteers, so read carefully:
- add Vaulting, Parkour (getting stuck in certain areas is not fun)
- add VOIP and proximity chat
- allow viewable Player tags in the city
- add Option to group up in the city
- Option to turn PvP off for PvE players
- FIX custom key-mapping not saving after exiting the game
- add quick key for drinking/eating/healing
- enable One Hit Head shot kill, 2 head shots to kill zombies wearing helmets
- Needs melee desperately! Currently running out of ammo means literally running away like headless chicken.
- De-spawned enemies should leave a loot bag if left un-looted by player
- Really needs a compass to assist in navigation to PIOs and Task locations
- UI Button to view the server name and the ability to Remember that server next time u login (favorite?)
- Suppressors for pistols and rifles
- More varied zombie types that provides a multi-layered threat vs reward game-play loop
- More quest variety other than fetch x-amount of y-items
- A barber shop to change facial features like hair, scars etc..
- A squad menu and the ability to issue way points and commands so everyone is on the same page and don't go running off.
- A toggle able mini map (not required and entirely optional)
- add wildlife around the city like wild dogs/wolves (maybe tamable pets?)
- Ability to eat/drink while walking/running
- ability to view the map while walking/running
- Various clothing items should give extra inventory slots depending on number of pockets
- Body armor should grant dedicated slots for ammo and extra handguns/attachments
- A flashlight to illuminate dark areas but may also attract more zombies (risk-reward game play)
- Remove ambient gunshot sounds, only player shots should be heard depending on the distance
- Ability to use furniture, sit on chairs and sleep on beds to regain stamina/heal wounds.
- Ability to use the musical instruments in the lucky shot bar (this is a stupid suggestion but there it is :P )
- Allow receiving quest from civilian NPCs around the hub not just the main NPCs
- Have traders with different selling prices (black market vendor should pay more for looted guns)
- A skill and xp system that unlocks new abilities (If this exists, it is not explained)
- A dynamic weather system that affects zombie spawns and movement speed (heavy rain increases spawn rate and drains stamina fater.. idk)
- More cosmetic items and a NPC to change into them
- ability to heal squad member and UI menu to trade with them
- allow the currency to be usable from the Stash (much convenience)
- A separate faction to join other than woodbury, like a Raider faction up north or a survivalist faction in the forest. Killing each other while we are all from woodbury makes no logical sense.
- Civilians surviving in the city that needs help or running away/dieing from zombies chasing them (aka dynamic events) occurring in the world as you travel. Solving them can give the player a reward (clothing, ammo, backpack, food/drink)
- Not losing your clothing and backpack when you die unless looted by a player (60-second death cool down to facilitate this idk). Armor and weapons should be lost tho.
- Add bug report UI button in game for current and future bug reporting in real-time
- add PvE BOSS events and random events on a notice board in the hub
- Correctly list the game as a 36-player Extraction shooter, you are only pissing off new players into refunding the game into oblivion. It is not an MMO.
- Add more varied city assets, your purchased assets are good but its a bit repetitive. Add more variety.
- Add more map. Just make it bigger and better with various biomes (snow, wetlands, forests etc.)
- make zombies a tiny bit slower. They run way to fast. (This isn't called '28 Days After')
- First person only servers for PvPvE and 3rd/1st person servers for PvE only players
- and lastly on this short humble list, but most importantly; communicate with your player base and post a road map for future and planned updates, fixes, patches etc. This will give those who still play a ray of hope that this game can and will be better. 

I am an aspiring indie game developer myself, so trust me bros.
Do not give up, what is here is NOT good to say the least. However, it is possible to turn a 180, and earn back some respect. Start with an genuine apology to the players, and move forward from there. (Also posted this list to TDB discord server)

One of the best quantity-quality-price ratios in the entirety of Steam.

ME1 is a good game, if a bit dated and simple in both gameplay and story, but enjoyable to play and with interesting concepts. 

ME2 is an improvement in every way over ME1; the combat is much better, characters are far richer and better explored, and both main  and side missions have a lot more care put into them. The DLCs are a welcome addition, for me the best missions alongside the last one. Very few games have an ending as good as this one.

ME3 is yet again a notable improvement over the last one. This game is what the franchise promised from the start, finally fully achieved and perfected. Engaging, tactical combat, meaningful choices even in the smallest moments, a renegade/paragon system that encourages you to organically decide what to do and expands what choices you can make instead of limiting you to one option, every mission is fun and interesting, and this game really excells at tying everything its predecesors built; it is incredible how decisions across the trilogy converge in this last installment.  ME3 feels like the best moments of ME2 but throughout the entire game.

The Citadel DLC deseves special mention, as it managed to make me smile for almost all its duration with its cheesy dialogue and heartwarming interactions between companions. This DLC is a love letter to both fans and the caracters themselves, with the party which serves as its climax being on par with the suicide mission when it comes to memorable moments in the trilogy (if for completely different reasons).

Every once in a while, I reinstall the game to see if EA has removed its client software.  Take Two did it with the old Bioshock games.  But, not EA.  It's still there.

Part of that EA software runs in Windows all the time, even if you're not using Steam or playing any games.  Check your Task Manager.  And if you try to disable the process permanently, it has a watchdog that pops up a notice about fixing it every time you restart your computer.

I didn't agree to ANY such behavior when I bought the ME games.  Those little yellow boxes on the store page that mention "background use required" are entirely too vague.  The average consumer would never expect it to mean something so invasive and unnecessary.
The game keeps taking me back to the menu whenever it's at a loading screen. I can't even play the game.

Oh man.. oh man.. I can easily say that the Mass Effect trilogy is one of my favorite game ever.

If you love sci-fi and an amazing story, you cannot miss this gem!

I love this series so much I had to take a break and slip a Dragon Age game in between just to recover enough to write this. I ended up with about 85 percent of the achievements without even trying but some of them need more than one playthrough and I’ve got a whole backlog of Bioware stuff to get through right now.

I played them in order and carried over my save like the loyal Commander I am. I went mostly renegade with some blue choices. Basically I was a badass who protected innocents but also kicked ass with zero hesitation. I romanced Kaidan from start to finish and wouldn’t trade my boy scout for anything. Garrus was a very close second and almost had me in ME2 when Kaidan and I were apart but my loyalty never wavered. Choices are important and impactful, world changing and world building. Classes are fun, i went sniper and had fun ruining peoples day but a friend who was playing at the same time went whatever the magic telekenetic death monster class is and i wished i had gotten to mess with that one, it sounded fun. 

It felt like a real universe to me and each game made it more diverse and interesting. The companions and relationships with NPCs are where it really shined. I loved learning about so many different species in a galaxy where racism was very real and even people on my own ship hated others. Some of my companions weren’t exactly subtle about it either. I’m looking at you Ashley. Seeing familiar faces pop up across games was a joy and I was extra happy when some stuck around to party with.

ME1 pros: Kaidan, Wrex, Garrus, Tali, and more lore and world building than I knew what to do with. Cons: surprise it’s an old game and the combat UI was clunky as hell. Honestly I thought it would bother me more than it did. Also I liked driving the Mako. Sue me.

ME2 upgraded combat and gave more lore and screen time to the companions we love. Except my precious boy scout. I don’t care if you don’t like him he is my angel and I’ll die for him. The DLC was sick too. Planet scanning sucked though. ngl.

ME3 my boy is back. Reunited and it feels so good. Combat got even better and the story felt real. Things were desperate. Things were scary. I was stressed. The last moments with my companions made me cry. It felt like I was saying goodbye to my friends. Letting this game go was hard which is probably why this review took so long. It sounds silly but I loved them. The DLC was everything. I know some people think it was fan service but I ate it up. Cons: the endings confused the hell out of me. I had to look up what they meant which is embarrassing but here we are. I like a HEA in my games. If I get that it’s easier to say goodbye and if not i feel like i failed those left behind.

Somewhere in my mind the crew is still together. The crew is playing poker, EDI is winning and Joker is losing. Wrex and Grunt are wrestling over the last piece of jerky while James and Jack referee. Liara is reading in the corner. Legion and Mordin are trying to figure out the new ice machine. Kasumi is stealthed nearby just watching the chaos. Shepard and Kaidan are looking out the star window smiling.

Thank you Mass Effect for permanently altering my brain chemistry.
