Mass Effect Legendary Edition brings the full trilogy into a single, modernized package. The visual upgrades are noticeable across environments, character models, and lighting. Textures are sharper and cutscenes look more cohesive, helping the narrative age gracefully without altering its core identity.

Gameplay in the first title benefits from the most adjustments. Combat feels smoother, aiming is tighter, and class abilities are more reliable. The second and third games change less, but their pre-existing polish remains. The flow from one entry to the next is seamless, and players can carry choices across all three campaigns, creating a continuous sense of consequence.

The narrative remains the series’ strongest asset. Dialogue choices shape relationships, alliances, and major plot beats. The cast is memorable and diverse, and conversations often define who lives, who dies, and how entire species react to your actions. The trilogy still stands as a benchmark for branching storytelling.

Exploration on planets and the use of the Mako vehicle in the first game are improved, with better handling and cleaner terrain design. Side missions are easier to track, reducing the friction that existed in the original versions. These changes encourage more complete playthroughs without feeling forced.

Sound design is intact and still effective. The soundtrack mixes electronic ambience with driving combat themes, reinforcing both calm exploration and high-stakes encounters. Voice acting remains consistent across all three titles and maintains emotional weight even after many years.

Technical performance is stable. Load times are shorter, framerate holds under pressure, and crashes are rare. The unified launcher simplifies configuration and saves management. Players on modern systems will have little trouble running the trilogy at high settings.

Overall, Mass Effect Legendary Edition preserves what made the series influential while smoothing its weaker edges. It is the recommended way to experience the trilogy, whether you are returning or playing for the first time. The choices you make remain impactful, and the journey still feels grand in scope.

Finally played through Mass Effect 1. The main campaign was great and once I got comfortable with the gameplay, I thought it was pretty good. Speaking of that, the uncharted planets are kind of barren and boring. You know how people complain about Starfield in regards to how you just wander around lifeless planets with nothing really interesting about them for the sake of a side quest? That's what it felt like to wander on an uncharted planet. Ultimately, that's small potatoes though because unlike Starfield, the writing's actually good. Overall, I'd probably rate Mass Effect 1 a 7/10.

Mass Effect is one of those few journeys that takes you back. The ambient sound playing when you boot up the first game is not just background music – it’s a memory of the past, a reminder of a time when games were made out of sheer passion. And for many, an echo shard of their childhood.

This trilogy has one of the most engaging narratives in the whole industry. Mass Effect, among other titles, revolutionized player interaction and relationship development in video games. In fact, the world in 2007/2008 wasn’t ready for this kind of social interaction, and certain “controversial” parts of the game made it to media outlets like CNN and Fox News. 

Although the trilogy really is [i] legendary, [/i] there are some parts that aren’t perfect. Like, if you haven’t played the series before, you might find some aspects a bit antiquated – for example combat in Mass Effect 1. Just do remember – the first two games were made in the 00s. The world was beautifully simpler back then, but also more technically limited.  

Going into detail on other aspects would require an extensive review. My advice is simply to give Mass Effect a go! Just don’t put it off immediately if it doesn’t feel like something for you. Believe me – the story, the characters, the universe, your role as Shepard – it will all grow on you.

While Bioware sadly is a shell of what they once were, their impact on the gaming industry should not be forgotten. Allegedly, they’re planning to make Mass Effect 4. Personally, I hope the studio is shut down before its release – not out of contempt, but out of respect for their legacy and name.

the choices you make are ridiculously important

secondly, kindness or cruelty to others can affect possible choices later on
in the first game its just a charm/intimidate stat; but in the other two games
do not pick middle options - always choose max good or bad 
you have to unlock the most effective options by [b]reliably picking a side[/b]

while choices are suppose to be a dynamic scale in the last two games
your charm/intimidate stat (from game one) is actually imported and added to the second game
if you pick a high intimidate stat: once in the second game, you'll need to pick cruel options
otherwise you tank reliability, and with that, your extra options fade away
[i]you cannot have both in the second game, so pick a single side to max in the first game[/i]

Rust is less of a game and more of a psychological experiment to see how long it takes for your faith in humanity to evaporate. You start with nothing but a rock and a torch, and somehow, by the end of your first hour, you're running naked through the woods, being chased by a man wearing a wolf's head and screaming profanities about your mother.

The gameplay is simple: gather resources, build a base, and try not to die. The reality? You’ll spend hours painstakingly constructing your dream fortress, only to log in the next day and find it reduced to rubble because someone named "xX420Sniper69Xx" decided your stash of raw chicken was worth a rocket launcher.

The graphics are stunning—just don’t stop to admire the scenery because that’s when Chad with an AK will pop out of the bushes and turn you into loot. And the community? Let’s just say it’s a beautiful mix of sociopaths, philosophers, and people who think "friendly" means "let me lull you into a false sense of security before stabbing you in the back."

But honestly, that's the charm of Rust. It's not about survival; it's about the stories. Like the time I joined a clan, only to be betrayed because I accidentally ate all the corn. Or the time I spent an hour taming a horse, only for it to immediately drown because I didn’t know horses can’t swim.

In conclusion, Rust is a masterpiece of chaos. It's frustrating, hilarious, and downright cruel—but you'll keep coming back because no other game lets you experience the raw thrill of chasing a fully geared player with nothing but a spear and blind optimism.

Great game, lots of stuff to learn to be able to get started tho. There's a mini tutorial and custom PvE servers where you can spend a few hours learning how to build and lay out your base and how the upgrading works. Really feels intense when you're out gathering materials or looking for weapons/weapon components to craft your own. There is a ton of freedom here and hundreds of different strategies for survival. I recommend watching some youtube gamers like Willjum play solo to get a good idea of what is possible in the game. Often times they will deliberately choose a different strategy every video just for fun. I know this description might not sound compelling but I just don't even know where to begin describing why I like this game. Really just being a beginner and trying to survive with a primitive setup is fun on its own. There are old military sites and old supermarkets full of loot, some more contested than others. You can build anywhere at all on these enormous maps and the game is pretty unforgiving, however once you get your base established then things start to stabilize and you're afforded some safety and time to think about expanding. Each time you play you will create a base a little more efficient and a little more safe and you will accomplish more and more. No other game quite makes survival feel so intense and real and important. And, if you're already a crack shot in FPS games, then you'll already have a decent advantage when you encounter other players or tribes in the wild. Highly highly recommend.

This was one game on a list I made over 6 years ago I wanted to come back to when I got a newer computer so that I could truly experience it as intended graphically and with the experience not interrupted by frame drops and technical issues due to an aging rig. Finally got what I wanted after saving up for so long a couple months ago and cannot tell you just how excited I was to come to this game again and really get do all the things I wanted. I knew this at launch was terrible and let the world know time and again but can also say after all that was shown and talked about by Hello Games and many friends over time showing me just how much the game improved and how much better the game was I can honestly say I admitted I was wrong and I am happy to see the greatest comeback in gaming history. Well let me tell you truth is certainly stranger than fiction. This game although is better than the turd they launched it as, the term "greatest comeback in gaming history" is completely false. I am here to tell you this game is not what everyone says it is and can tell you for a fact you can get everything this game has to offer including the full experience by simply watching somebody play it as opposed to doing it yourself with no repercussions or loss which for a video game should rarely be the case. Short answer is I do not recommend the game and will tell you why.

Captivating. I think most of not all would agree that the game through playing it or watching it is beautiful, colorful, and has an ok variety when it comes to planets, fauna, and flora. I do not say say great because it doesn't take long to run into the same things again and again after about the 3rd planet or so. On some planets I had to scan the exact same plant 3 to 4 times because im told the game considered these all to be similar but not the same even though they were copies of one another. Though much of the life and environments can be beautiful they are mostly drab copy paste this same thing all over and in very messy ways. Constantly things clipping into one another causing issues and overall coming off very cheap. I would say one of my favorite parts is flying around in the void of space jumping from place to place ranging from a blue system cast in this very comforting glow to a system with 3 different colored suns basking you in colors and looking so very cool. I never tired of it not even once. The space stations I used to like the look of but now they also must be procedurally generated because they look like a 6 year old kid started slapping legos together and boom random crap all over it. Every outpost, every random building, every alien structure are always the same damn ones. I don't fawn over lazy work and am certainly not about to award this game for it when it exists all over the place. Not near enough care in the area of the game that actually sells copies under false pretenses no less. 

The point of toiling....is to toil. Hello Games would have you believe that through all of these updates that there's not only thousands of worlds to explore but there is also plenty to do but let me enlighten you as this is simply not true. Your gameplay will solely consist of gather this thing to acquire thing either for a quest or someone else. This will in turn turn into go get or gather this thing to make this thing to help you gather more. Go get. gather, create. This is the entirety of the game. At the end of it you have a bunch of stuff to look at but cannot do anything with it as it serves no purpose or function other than to be oggled. You can't say it is even for combat since it isn't even real. No difficulty and no consequence for dying either. Even space battles I realized boiled down to holding down left trigger then holding the A button and I ended up killing 12 opponents like it was nothing and that is even with the awful turning in spaceships. Ground battles you use 1 of only 2 decent weapons in the game and just fire till they die while walking in a circle and even that just turns right back around into gather more stuff to create or to give someone without any real rewards other than to repeat the cycle. Unless you like collecting holographic animals to stare at for 30+ hours then what is the point of all this damn work? Why does it it take 3 or 4 real life hours to get so little done because the game insists on moving the story at a snails pace, work at a snails pace for certain items, and it just keeps flooding you with more and more junk to carry?

You only get half. As I have stated some of the visuals are great and some of the story can be told through it but they have truly wronged there players. If you read the actual logs and whatnot you find throughout the game be it crashed spaceships, derelict freighters, or old ancient sites it never actually shows you the most interesting things in the game it only tells you them. Aboard a freighter I found a space the dialogue was the most random crap ever and it couldn't decide if it was a cloud of nanites, parasites, violent, passengers, or an alien was responsible for it all but in  the end it didn't matter anyway as none of the things it describes it ever shows. They want you to imagine it but don't want to render and show it. You have to do that. They expect you to use your imagination for easily the most interesting parts of the game. My real question is then where is there any actual work put into this? Im not saying this game needs to turn into Star Trek or something but at least try and make so many things more interesting otherwise this is just a fancier walking simulator. So let me ask you this. Why were we told they wanted to do right by the player? There still aren't players flying around with you unless your in groups and only at the anomalies, day 1 mayor issues still all over the place, combat was the same as when I first tried it years ago, there are more vibrant environments but with that came even more glitches and bugs, expeditions are TO THIS DAY still timed events and have a ton of rewards that players can get only 1 once a damn year otherwise ♥♥♥♥ the player, everything is designed to waste more and more of your time while giving you nothing for it, and to make matters worse they are now using AI in the game moving forward. Seriously? I see more ways to distract the player with more stuff to look at but nothing else to actually do. Then how about you tell me how No mans Sky let alone Hello Games has actually changed? 

Quality only exists visually. With all the great visuals and even some things with real detail it pretty much ends there. I have multiple times ended in space without a space ship, launched into the sky at impossible speed and heights, clipped and sucked into objects even when im not near them, pulled into the earth underground randomly while running and sprinting, have found and tested an actual way to crash ANOTHER players game if we join a group together but does not crash me and can only be fixed if I am the one who restarts No Mans Sky, items falling through the ground anywhere in the game losing many an item, having enemies spawn inside the ground including bosses and no I don't mean underground in caves I mean I have to dig everybody out to kill them, story quests locking up, frigates and other structures merging and preventing me from completing missions and gathering items, and many many more. 

I cannot for the life of me see how anyone recommends this game unless your maybe playing with a group of friends and can overlook a bunch of this stuff but otherwise this game gets far too undeserved praise for being something it isn't. Much of its progress has been an improvement upon its original design but never actually taking any risks or putting in real work and effort to become something truly more or evolving into a better version of itself. I really tried time and again to find something really anything but there isn't.

No Man's Sky is an open world exploration game that also includes a full story line and side quests. This game has many different things to do allowing for full creativity. There are near unlimited amount of planets and systems to explore which lets players endlessly discover new wildlife and untouched planets. This game is still given updates and I have never experienced any bugs or glitches that effected my gameplay. I would totally recommend this game to anyone who is looking for: 
~ something space related
~ something open world or exploration
~ something with a long and interesting story
~ something that can be peaceful, calm and creative
~ something that is deadly and difficult

The game is perfect for grinders and pure explorers. The planets are beautiful and their ecosystems very well-designed and full of life. The aliens and their technologies also have unique design to them, as well as ships, portals, etc. The corvette is fully customizable, and the personalization doesn't stop there, there is still base building and frigate, which is a lot larger in scale and many other amenities.

However, as much as I like how the devs redeem this game over quite a long time, I gotta say the game fell short just after a while for me. First thing: lack of QoL. Things like the jetpack jump interfering with normal jump and weapon operation really ruin my combat and ore mining experience. Climbing the ladder feels like eternity and the character just auto-climb whenver near it, like in trade outposts, and this is very annoying. Accessing storage is just a hassle, and without essential ores to build a camp and claim the base it's gonna take a while to dump all the stuff into other inventories (also they do not have auto-sorting in storage). There are many more, and I feel like the game this old should have already addressed these things. Second thing: things feel flat and repetitive after a while. Sure there are a lot of things that you can do when visiting a new system, meeting aliens there, setting up camps, etc. Space combat gets boring after a few hours for me, which I think should be a main focus of a game with space as the center of its attraction. Quantity cannot beat quality, and this inevitably comes sooner or later (later for me). 

The game has its own charm, and with me those 97 hours were definitely worth it. But it would not be something that I can binge anymore. And I just wanna specify a few things for other people who just wander into this exquisitely-built but poorly-optimized jack-of-all-trade game. If you want to explore the galaxies and chill while watching two sunsets, then this is for you. But if you also want other flavors of the cake, then this may not be it.

*Papers, Please* is a brilliant indie game that puts you in the role of a border control officer in a fictional, authoritarian state. While processing travelers, you’ll face tough moral decisions and confront the human cost of bureaucracy. The game’s simple mechanics, paired with its gripping narrative, make it an intellectually stimulating and emotionally charged experience. With its minimalist art style and intense atmosphere, *Papers, Please* challenges players to think critically about power, ethics, and the consequences of their actions. A must-play for anyone looking for a game that makes you think.

Twelve years on, Papers Please serves as both the pinnacle and progenitor of its own genre of narrative-driven simulator, and despite a long list of replicators and imitators, has only been (maybe) superceded by Lucas Popes follow up.

The sheer ingenuity and creativity behind this game is staggering. The fact that somebody (Pope) thought ‘You know what would make a good game? What if you were a guard at a border crossing and you had to cross reference paper-work.’
Like, ‘ V I D E O G A M E S ‘ are where you are a hot person with a gun or a sword and you kill people and do kickflips, not a monotonous, stressful work simulator where your concerns are about sick family members, and the omnipresent conflict between your own totalitarian dystopia and the nations surrounding it. On paper, a game where you deal with a bunch of paperwork and have to pay your rent and worry about the state of the nation isn’t very fun… and it also isn’t very fun in practise either, and I say that with utmost respect.

I think ‘Papers, Please’ proves that video games don’t have to be fun (or atleast, kinetically exhilarating) to be good, which seems insane to think, but it is true. The real metric for a good video game is not ‘fun’ but rather ‘engagement.’ It doesn’t actually matter if you are having fun, if you are enthralled and engaged - this game delivers that in droves. It realizes the casual horror of living in a Soviet-era regime, and is able to tell stories from the smallest scale to the greatest of consequences, all from this one screen. From small character narratives of love and fear, to the global conflicts and wars, it all filters through your little booth. The moral choices this game places on you are so rarely seen in any form of art, and in general dystopias are not depicted in such a realistic way.

You are not in the high offices of government, you are not a soldier on the front lines, you are an immigration inspector at a border crossing, yet you have power over people lives, life and death are controlled by your stamps. Trying to walk the balance between being empathetic towards people, while not making too many mistakes, and trying to not get so many fines as to starve your family is a tense tightrope you have to walk. 

Do you let in this grieving mother, despite her papers being outdated? They are only expired by a few days. This man is moving with his wife, but his passport is obviously forged, but he is fleeing from a warzone. Another has wrong papers, yet hasn’t committed any real crime, but you get bribes for each person you detain, and your family is starving and freezing to death at home. Should you detain them for a minor transgression? Even the people the game ASKS you to detain - they are in the wanted criminals bulletin - we are never told what they have done, and they seem shocked and surprised at being detained. Are they really criminals, or have they just spoke out against Arstotska? 

Papers, Please is an example of sheer focus and execution on a single vision, and the power of video game story telling. It is also an example of choice. You could be empathetic, you could engage with everything, and read the newspapers, and help to thwart the government. Or you could keep your head down, ignore anybody who talks to you, and just do your job and keep your family safe. A timeless game which should be studied and put in museums.

It's tough to describe how fun and interesting this game is. It's essentially a job simulator. But it rises well above that, using its medium to tell a particular story of political intrigue. The player can make different decisions which culminate into wildly different endings.

Pretty good border control simulator set in the pretence of a post-war era in Arstotzka, where corruption, mistrust and abuse of power is rampant A perfect amalgam of an ADHD game with an intriguing 1984-esque  (the book) charisma to it.
This game adds a good mix of both stress and focus into the gameplay as you have to take care of your family, not just yourself.

There are 2 main story pathways for you to take, either betray your country to side with the separtists who deem the current regime unfit or stay loyal to it or perhaps play both sides. 
Either way is interesting and speaks volumes as your decisions matter.

An amazing game, I HIGHLY recommend it. The controls are easy to understand, and the game starts easy but has a very natural progression and increase in difficulty. It never feels too overwhelming, the increase is very gradual. The game also has lots of replay value: multiple endings, plenty of achievements, as well as an endless mode with multiple options of play.

As for the story, this game does a great job of showing off the world, its story, and the culture without too much information. There's no massive cut scenes or dialogue, and everything can be understood without the need to look at third-party content or analyze anything. You get to change the story in certain ways based on your actions, but you also have consequences for them. You may find yourself sticking to the rules directly, never straying from them- other times, you'll feel a moral obligation to make a choice that doesn't align with what your communist overlords want. 

Somehow, this game is able to make you care about non-animated PNG sprites so much, at times I was very attached to these characters and found myself wishing I could see more of them.

My only critique is this: I wish we could save in the middle of a level. At times it's very frustrating to have to replay entire levels just for the 1 outcome I want. [Spoiler](Spoilers: I had to replay a level 4 times because I couldn't save Sergiu.)[/Spoiler]

Overall, you should absolutely play this game. It's widely enjoyed by most people who have played it, it has a great story, easy controls, and very progressive, gradual difficulty spikes.

Some play Darkest Dungeon. While, I play the Darkest Pits of Hell. I've become a masochist after forcibly pushing myself into 100 hours of DD. 100 hours pure Stygian and enjoying the wild rollercoaster of frustration and joy that my party has put me through. Every single time I play I mentally become afflicted: Abusive, Masochist, and Irrational. Get this game to feel like Cecil from Invincible, because omg my heroes cannot do ♥♥♥♥ or contribute to anything (Reynauld).

Let ruin come to you.

From the second you open this game you are forewarned that not every scenario is winnable, and tactical retreats, and cut losses are to be part of your campaign against the raging darkness. At first you might think "sure whatever..." And then you wander into your first real dungeon run, and a skulking horror oneshots a vital party member. Directly after, another party member suffers a complete mental breakdown and takes down another team member with them, and then you are faced with the harsh reality... Overconfidence is a slow and insidious killer.

You think you learn your lesson, and begin to muster an army, you find resolute heroes capable of war with the dark forces. Several champions emerge with varying skills for both combat and survival, and having carefully planned and weighed the risk, you wager an assault onto a boss. During which your favorite champion goes out in a blaze of glory allowing your other 3 heroes a desperate escape. A success such as it is.

Darkest Dungeon does its work both on and off the screen. It is a game both played on the cosmic stage and in the darkest corners of the players mind. As successes mount, emotion becomes tied to emerging heroes that are forced into more-and-more difficult scenarios as their individual resolve and quirks mount. You will watch as they fall  (an excruciating experience) in places where there is no ability to react. Some complete failures, you'll want to walk away, you may even uninstall (I have several times).

But then you think... But then these heroes (with no individual stories, who have naught but a name) have all died in vain. So you start again. Your stress becoming their stress, a dizzying rush of pride when you do summit a particularly difficult encounter.

In time you will see your failings in the graveyard, offset by a reborn hamlet surrounded by more willing heroes ready to brave the Darkest Dungeon. When it is your time, you will be ready.

This game is incredible, the art direction is spot on and the gameplay loop, while simple, is generally fun. If I had to make one criticism I'd say the character statistics could be clearer, maybe benefitting from a more streamlined UI.
Darkest Dungeon is not for everyone, being kind of stressful and somewhat grindy, but it is oozing with flavour and the characters and environment are iconic.

Unfortunately, the sequel didn't quite manage to build upon it (though still decent).

Incredible art, sound, voice, dialogue, and overall world design.
Gameplay is decent enough, the base gameplay of turnbased party organization is good.
They do fumble the ball on some things, like being heavy RNG based, I thought I was going crazy or misunderstanding mechanics like how food works, or why the enemy took 4 turns before I got 1.

From a game design standpoint it has some heavy flaws, and its a shame the second game isnt just this wiht those flaws fixed.

I was surprised to find out the bugs I experienced were fairly widely experienced due to them using their own engine made from scratch, and rather than fix them, its up to the player to avoid them.

The artistic design and decent gameplay carry it enough to get past most of the bad design and bugs.
Some of the game ideas played with have inspired many other games, so that in itself makes this game very special.

There is a lot mechanically in this game that is just bad design, like why can I only use healing abilities in combat?
I have a healer, but I am supposed to go into my next fight with 3 hp because the dev is a moron. this is exactly the kind of thing that makes people complain about artificial difficultly.
your stats don't matter, I see enemies with 9-35 dodge who we will miss routinely. Meanwhile I can boost my dodge to over 70 and get hit nearly every attack. its really dumb, what is the point in buffing when it has no noticeable effect on performance.
and lets talk about stress, this is a mind blowingly dumb mechanic, on a long mission you might have to do 30 fights, you can reach 100 stress easily in 4-5 enemy attacks. fighting 4 enemies a battle if you take 2 turns to wipe them out 5 attacks come out really quickly, and the AI is not dumb they will target your highest stress character with stress attacks. 
At 100 stress you get a debuff that makes your characters stress their companions every turn and sometimes ignore orders. They will even refuse healing, they are going to die next turn but no, you aren't allowed to do anything about it. or worse they can literally commit suicide mid battle. 
This causes your whole team to quickly stress out, so you have 4 guys all stressing all of their teammates every turn in an infinite death spiral. at 200 stress you just die.
As soon as any character hits 100 stress you have like one battle before the run is over. This is a mostly unavoidable mechanic, as there are basically no ways to reliably reduce stress. There are some abilities that reduce it, but it only really works in low level missions as in missions at your level or above the enemy just out DPS's you.  A good high level stress heal is for like 12 stress on a single unit, most stress attacks do 20, I have seen it hit as high as 45, and may hit many units. not to mention you take stress walking around. your entire team take stress every time you get critical hit(a very common occurance), but only half your team recovers stress when you crit. 
So the only viable strategy is to kill the enemy as quickly as possible, when you out level them significantly this is possible, but at same level or higher it is not. Why you ask? because your level 6 team cannot reliably hit the lv 5 enemies, and even when they do hit them its at very low damage because enemies have armour. So you need to either debuff or blight or bleed them, which is fine except it takes time, and time means stress, which means death spiral.
And then there is food, needing to eat is random(again why, what moron comes up with this?) so you can go an entire dungeon and use no food, or you can be forced to eat 4 times in 4 hallways concurrently. 
And even on missions you do perfectly, you can still get negative debuffs and illnesses at the end of mission, again why? 
What kind of moron dev punishes players for doing good?
The game does have a one more battle charm to it, but all of its mechanics were thought up by a braindead ♥♥♥♥♥♥.

Darkest Dungeon, what is there to say about this game. Is it fun? Yes, to a degree. The art style is great, music and basic gameplay is solid, and the eldritch lore is fun. However, this game is frustrating, it is hard, and it is usually very unfair. As frustrating as it is, the community is also a mixed bag. There are a lot of modders creating great mods, new characters, and all sorts of fun things that make the game so much more enjoyable and less of a grind. However, if you have anything to say negatively about the game, you'll also be met with "get good" and "you're playing wrong". 

Is Darkest Dungeon a great representation of what Red Hook Studios was going for with their vision? For sure. Is the game a FUN game, if you like grinding, randomized chance with your characters, and needless repetition and pointless limits to make the game longer, then also yes. You may be thinking, why is this a positive review then? Well, for all my criticism of the game, it is still good. I did beat the Darkest Dungeon, and I did have fun doing so at times. The game is a very unique and provides a lot to players that enjoy this type of game, although that isn't me persay. 

The DLCs make the game worse but offer new gameplay mechanics that spice up the game and adds greater difficulty for the masochists who enjoy it. The base game is great and while there are better ways to speed up the grind, and I would recommend downloading mods to do just that, I can't help but say that there is a reason the community who loves this game is so fiercely loyal to it that any criticism is shut down. This game fills a unique niche that deserves to have this game, and if you find yourself to be a part of that then this is for sure a buy. For anyone else? Look into it, do your research, and if you end up wanting to give it a chance then get it on sale. You may like what you find.

Cities: Skylines is one of my favorite games of all time. I have an amazing time every time I play. Building cities, expanding roads and managing public transport all feel incredibly satisfying. A wonderful game for every city-builder fan.

The game is genuinely engaging, but to be honest, it becomes boring quite quickly.

First of all, there is very little additional content. I bought the Deluxe package, but there really is not much included in it. What annoys me a lot is that you have to keep buying everything separately in extra packs. It is expensive and, in my opinion, not really worth it.

The game is missing many things, such as natural disasters, strikes, or other events that would make the gameplay more difficult and interesting. There are also many logic issues in different parts of the game, and overall it seriously lacks challenges.

I saw that the second game has been released, but after reading the reviews, I was not impressed, and I definitely will not buy it unless they fundamentally change the game.

In general, I think it would be interesting if they added some kind of conflict or war element with other cities. That could make the game much more engaging and add more challenges.

The graphics and the way people and buildings come to life in this game never get old. There's something almost meditative about watching your city breathe and grow every day.
The core game is rock solid — mods make it even better, but the foundation is what makes everything work. A masterpiece that doesn't demand anything from you. Just sit back, build, and unwind.

This game is fun for a while until you see the cracks in the gameplay and the system.

The worst part for me personally is that I will never "beat" this game in terms of achievements, because a whole bunch of them are hidden behind DLC content. So if you want to able to get all achievements, you have to buy all the DLC which cost about 158 €uros at the moment - and that's with price reductions and without the base game!

The map size is locked. So you can't freely expand your city. You can only unlock a restrictive number of grid squares that don't even allow you to use the full map size at the end! And if you want access to outside train routes, highways or water freight transport lines, you HAVE TO buy/unlock certain squares.

The game mechanics just desintegrate on further inspection. The more citizens you have, the slower the city grows in every regard and gameplay progress comes to a crawl. You cannot copy city areas with a tool. The landscaping tool is bad. You can only change small areas of the map and only with approximation. There is no fixed height that you can set as a goal. But you have to deal with gigantic sloped landscapes. Infuriating. 

Traffic flow is not explained in detail and the travel behaviour of citizens makes absolutely no sense. There are no good ingame-tools to give hints on how to improve traffic flow.

There is no ban for cars. 
You can't properly connect walkways with streets. You're always required to build roads and connect buildings to them, so there is no good option to build cities without roads. Fun at first, but you eventually get into a boring cycle of building, needing money, raising money to build the neighborhood, and on to the next neighborhood. You also have to constantly deal with problems in your buildings/neighborhoods that are often small but can build up at sometimes ridiculous rates (especially with abandoned buildings), and solving them often feels more like a chore than an actually engaging challenge.

This is fundamentally a great game, but the performance on my integrated graphics is a bit spotty. Even after using tools like lossless scaling it was getting around 20-25fps (which isn't really playable with massive stutters). The dlc is also a bit pricey - and there are lots and lots of them. For something like $4AUD this game is great, but more than that when it comes to the performance it's a bit harder to reccommend. If you have powerful hardware - go for it (even maybe the second one), but now I've decided to fire up the old ps4 to see if it can run this game better. Great game you should buy, but remember integrated graphics aren't that good.

One of my favorite moments came from a completely random encounter. I ran into a grandmother quietly exploring space by herself after her husband passed away. She invited me aboard with warm hospitality and a simple offer: fresh cookies and food for any traveler passing through. I sat with her for a while, talking about her grandkids, her late husband, and the risks of wandering the stars alone. Before leaving, I gave her 1,000 credits to help fund her little adventure.

Then, just as I was about to jump away, I looked around and thought, “This is actually a really nice ship.”

So I turned around, killed her, and stole it.

Starfield has a funny way of revealing exactly what kind of space explorer you really are.

I think if you look back sequentially each Bethesda release assumes two things that escalate in intensity with each passing game. They expect the existing fans to blindly buy their games, putting up with all the things that made you're favorite title good, whatever that may be. And they seem to see a trend that I hear a lot about but I've never met anyone who has liked it.... They are playing to an invisible market trend that seems to exist because AAA games says it should?

Every game is much bigger, much harder to run specs wise.

Every game is also emptier and reuses assets more and more. The size means they are inherently less dense the what came before.

Every new game seems to multiply the size of the game by 2-4x on my hard drive.

Every game is dumbed down version of the last removing RPG elements, and adding more scripted scenes and more invulnerable NPCs. And usually adding a thing you have likely never would've asked for.

I'm sure that whatever game you came from this rings true. Because frankly? Everything since arena has lost something. Everything since doom has lost something. Everything since fallout 1 has lost something. And it continues to be lost.

All of these are still true. And star field is the worst of it yet. It runs like hot garbage, it's cities feel more lonely then the non inhabited planets, the space flight mechanic is just in orbit. I tired this game on gamepass on launch. And I played a lot but couldn't find a reason to beat it. And I got it for free with a tech purchase and tried it and will likely never play it again.


An afterword:
I am more and more finding myself discusted by AAA games. I don't touch anything made by ea, Ubisoft, Microsoft, Blizzard, Activision, or now Bethesda anymore.

I'm going to put my money where my mouth is and just focus on paying people who care when I put resources twords them. I started buying extra food when I go shopping or get drive through and giving it to unhoused folks, I started reaching out to and helping neighbors with their problems, I started exercising and reaching out to family. I am regularly reaching out to lost contacts now to tell them they are thought about and loved.

These billion dollar companies don't need or care about me or my money (Often my data is the product they are selling after all...).

My community does though. My small amount of significance to a billion dollar company is easily felt at a smaller scale. And I guess I'm greatful for the chance to see that perspective from such a disappointment of a game.

This is kind of one of those games I'd give a neutral review to. Starfield isn't a typical Bethesda game. There's a lot of content chopped up and spread out across procedural planets, with the handcrafted stuff sprinkled within or nestled together closely. If you're wanting extreme freedom, a bunch of Bethesda systems (some half-♥♥♥♥♥♥, others good, especially improved with the recent Free Lanes Update), then go for it.

If you're looking for a traditional, singular open world game that you can seamlessly travel without a loading screen to enter a ship, exit a ship, and then to enter a dungeon-- this won't be it. Starfield is a Bethesda take on a space sim-- and it has all the flaws and positives that come with that exact description.
I waited a couple years to play, I am glad I gave it a chance, bit of a learning curve but I think I am really going to enjoy this game for years to come.

[h2] Starfield for me is a rough review to make. In some ways I really enjoy the game, and others it feels like a slap in the face. I played through the game several time and played NG+ several times as a reference. [/h2]

[olist]
    [*]Combat-wise, I enjoyed the game. I don't expect crazy gun mechanics in an RPG, but it was still good enough for me.
    [*]Ship building was really fun in the beginning, but I wish there were even more options for parts. 
    [*]Flight is where I started to see cracks, and I'm not even talking about the initial disappointment of space travel.  My issue is that sometimes(ALOT) when I fly, half of the spaceport would glitch and follow me into space and then be permanently attached to my ship until I restarted the game. This could also occur with asteroids you destroyed. Now this could be corrected with mods from the community, but if you do that, you can say goodbye to achievements. Which is terrible, but flying with a GIGANTIC obstructive building or asteroid following and blocking you is way worse.
    [*]The vast emptiness of space. I have no issue with this, it's got realism to it and while that might not appeal to everyone, I found it fine.
    [*]REPETITION. In my opinion this is what killed the game for me. Once you have seen 4 different pirate areas, you've seen them all. They are all basically identical and really make you not want to do any exploration or side missions at all. BECAUSE YOU'VE ALREADY SEEN IT ALL. Some might say that Skyrim or other Bethesda games also have cookie cutter dungeons, but not even remotely close to how IDENTICAL each of the outposts in this game are. Lets play devil's advocate and say that they're prefabs lore-wise and it's just how most are made? I could accept that, if there was more lore at each site to at least spark my interest. I can handle a vastly empty space game if the areas I do explore have a giant amount of lore and content. The problem is that because each area is identical to one of the 4-5 outpost options, you know exactly where to find the loot/quest item and quickly get out.
[/olist]

All this to say, I'm about to replay it with the new DLC, so I'll update if any of this has improved.

Spent 16 hours just trying to mod the game to make it playable, it's barebones runs like crap, looks like crap, and its sanitized story and companions who are all morally white wizards. Even the pirates are kids' cartoons, bad guys. No depth, story is an inch deep, and I had to download 200+ mods to make this game something it's not. The game on its own is worth 30 bucks as a fun little game. This game has so much potential, but it feels like a pre-alpha build of something that could be so much better. They sacrificed the story to appeal to a wider audience, and in turn, it lost its depth. The fact is, with a paid mod system and a woefully incomplete game, I can not recommend this. I had so much hope for this game. Bought the headset and controller, and preordered. Unfortunately, Bethesda doesn't know its fans; it wants to appeal to everyone by making a sandbox for paid mods, and the game suffers for it. 

They can fix Starfield, but it might take years. It needs morally grey and black companions and stories that feel connected to the world, as well as an in-depth crime, contraband, and piracy system. It needs actual survival mechanics, like even just fuel usage from space travel or oxygen loss from being on a planet with no atmosphere. It's underbellies, aka the well neon and the red mile, need to feel like real underbellies of crime and dystopianism. The Red Mile needs to be a real casino, not just a fake one. Crowds should feel vastly different from planet to planet, not just a hey, they wear different shirts, but a true style difference. And your outposts should be able to function as civilian settlements like in Fallout. These are just a few very small ways this game can be fixed, but instead, you can buy a mod to do each for $5-$10, and they may not configure together well. These mods should have been in the base game. I was a big believer in Bethesda's storytelling and mechanics, but we've lost both in an attempt to appeal to a bigger audience.

This game has so much potential, and yet, does not meet it.
Through mods, I was able to get almost the experience it should have been...
However, there is something that particularly irks me with starfield...
you see, the game itself is fine, it runs well, it's reasonably fluid, and Bethesda seems to have been on top of handling the bugs, shockingly. but it's SO poorly written, with so many uninspired missions and locations. Even the lore is lacking. Now that is quite a shock considering this is a game from the same company that gave us Elder Scrolls and Fallout 3 and 4. Worse yet, the problem is obvious. How so you ask? Because every time I feel like something is lazy or uninspired, an NPC tells me they are gay, and sometimes gives me a backstory to being gay. Guards, Random encounters, Quest specific NPCs, Companions... WHY ARE THERE SO MANY GAY CHARACTERS WITH NON PLAYABLE STORY BUT NO INDEPTH STORY ANYWHERE ELSE!? I get it half the NPCs in Starfield are gay, where the hell is the story? where is the handcrafted unique locations that Bethesda fans know and love? it just isn't there. Even the book selection is lack luster, terminal entries lame, and locations and missions uninspired and bland. 
Now, WITH MODS (like real fuel, heatleech infestations and space economy), and ignoring a chunk of the story it's a decent space sim. 
Adding other mods for expanded content and locations, and fixing a few of the basic issues, it's actually a good game. I will however not be recommending the game due to the fact that mods are effectively necessary, and the vanilla game is bland.
and for the love of god Bethesda (or better yet, a modder), add a mechanic to select where doors and ladders (and stairs) are in the ship designer! I have to do Weird things to make the layout even make sense and prevent the ship from being a labyrinth.

Mostly a disappointment, unfortunately. It carries a lot over from Fallout 4, but simultaneously regresses a lot of features too. TLDR; One step forward, 4 steps back.

The biggest regression is probably the complete dumbing down of the radiant AI system they had since Oblivion. NPCs largely do not follow any kind of schedule or even go about their lives. They are just around 24/7 usually doing the same thing and behave like signposts. It's a system that greatly added to the immersion of their previous games and made it feel more alive, like things actually happened without you being there. That's completely absent in Starfield for no known reason that's ever been explained by the developers.

Points-of-interest are not a good replacement for interesting handcrafted locations from their previous games either. They repeat and do not really provide much in the way of meaningful content or environmental storytelling. There's almost zero incentive to actually visit and explore POIs once you understand this. In past games, locations always had interesting environmental storytelling and or quests associated with them, or unique items to find. Not really so in Starfield. They are effectively just a way to pad time, and a very ineffective one at that. Basically the same level of quality as an open-world Ubisoft game.

Loading screens are the bane of this game's existence and it often just can't get out of its own way because of that. Loading screens were not as big of an issue in Fallout and TES because they featured large singular open-worlds where you would spend the majority of your play time. Starfield has about 10x the amount of loading screens as any of their previous games (which is saying something because even those games already had a lot of loading screens). 

There really is no excuse for it other than that they didn't bother re-engineering the engine with better streaming technology. They can't use the excuse of "the technology doesn't exist yet" anymore because we now have NVMe drives in consoles that can pull in over 7GB/s of data with DirectStorage and these same consoles are saddled with 16GB of system RAM. With games like Cyberpunk 2077 with worlds as detailed and immersive as that, that can run on the Nintendo Switch 2 even, there really is no valid excuse other than that they couldn't be bothered to upgrade that portion of the engine.

Another system from Fallout 4 that is largely a regression is the settlement system (outposts as they're called in Starfield). Outposts play almost no role whatsoever in Starfield. You might be forgiven for not even realizing it's in the game, since it effectively doesn't bother to tell you. People largely felt that the settlement system was a bit too involved in the main story of Fallout 4, so apparently they did a 180 of that in Starfield and basically made it an afterthought.

There's some nice quality of life changes to the settlement system that does make building with it a lot better, but overall, Fallout 4 has far more breadth and depth to its settlement system. In Fallout 4, the settlement system is genuinely useful, especially with supply lines between your settlements. It is invaluable in survival mode, and you can do quite a bit with it. In Starfield, it's not good for much of anything by the time you even have the skills unlocked to make the most of it.

I would also say that the companions in Starfield are a significant downgrade from the quantity, and especially quality, of the NPC companions we had in Fallout 4. Fallout 4 actually has a lot of really good and really interesting companion characters that you'll meet during the main story or just out in the world. Their personalities are quite varied, as are the things they'll approve and disapprove of. They all have interesting stories and quirks. In starfield, not only do you have far fewer companion characters, but most of them are very similar in personality and beliefs. Not nearly as interesting or complex as the characters from Fallout 4, or as well written.

I could go on and on drawing comparisons where this game has obviously regressed, and believe me, there are many other instances where this is clear once you know what to look for (they removed the entire scrap system so junk is literally just junk, environmental storytelling is almost nonexistent, underwater exploration is gone, etc). The story is lackluster, but that's nothing new for a Bethesda game, par for the course, really. What it doesn't have, though, is any of the saving graces that previous titles had. This doesn't really feel like an immersive experience where I can just live in the world like it did in their previous titles.

None of the gameplay systems in Starfield gel together or cohere like they did in Fallout 4 and Skyrim. It feels like a bunch of systems that are disconnected from each other. They lack the kind of synergy that previous title's gameplay systems had. The gameplay loop of Starfield is not nearly as tight or as potent as it was in either Fallout 4 or Skyrim, that much is clear even after just the first few hours of playing. It's a game that feels like it's constantly stumbling over itself and just can't get out of its own way. It's a weird feeling, hard to explain, but it's obvious something is constantly slightly off when you play Starfield compared to their previous titles.

Overall, I did enjoy aspects of Starfield, but I enjoyed Fallout 4 and Skyrim far more and it's not even close. Those feel like games I can jump back into at any time, but with Starfield, I'd have to really fight myself into playing it again and I'd probably quit 20 minutes into the session again. Starfield wishes it was Skyrim in space, but really it's just a pale imitation that fails at most of what it's trying to do. 

[b]Playing Starfield feels like what a child's crayon drawing looks like[/b]: disjointed, chaotic, disproportionate, and not particularly interesting or compelling. It has most of the right elements, but they aren't connected right and lack the correct perspective. You'll put it on the fridge and forget about it in a week. It feels like corporate slop. Like it was made from a list of checkboxes that were all ticked-off one by one to meet some arbitrary quota. It's a Bethesda game that fails to capture the "Bethesda Magic" and because of that I really cannot recommend it.

So I finally decided to get it, since they finally addressed the performance issues and such.
I've played for just over 20 hours and am enjoying it very much. Good stories, decent acting, decent graphics, although they leave much to be desired. The colors are bad, but the haze/fog is even worse. Everywhere you go, it's like a room full of smoke. You can barely see an enemy if he's at a distance because of the the muck in the air. I tried one of the color filter mods, and it did look better, but then realized my achievements were no longer functioning. I had to restart the game, with no mods at all, for it to work as it was meant to be. Just wish Bethesda would address the graphics issues that so many are complaining about.
There is LOTS to do, and it can get confusing. The controls are very confusing with a controller. Lots of button pushing. Scanning is essential, but there are things you can't do while scanning. If an enemy pops up while you're scanning, you have to remember to turn it off so you can fire your weapon, otherwise you end up shooting with your wimpy geological laser. There are things you can't interact with in scan mode too. Very frustrating.
Still having fun, but it would be awesome if things were fixed.

UPDATE
I finished the game. Overall I liked it, but there are a few things I found annoying. The pathfinding is awful. Only works half the time. Cuts out and comes back, but most of the time, you have to shut it off and turn it back on for it to work....WHEN IT WORKS!
I enjoyed the mindless scanning of each planet for life and elements. It's relaxing. But the fauna and flora variety is extremely limited.  You're dealing with life across the entire galaxy, and yet, you keep finding the exact same creatures and plants. You can almost count what you find. Would it have been that hard to create more? It got really boring, where if you found new stuff every once in a while, it would have been much more entertaining.
Being hailed while out in space by different entities was very cool at first. Someone needed some repair parts to fix their ship,  and I thought it was very "realistic". But later in the game, the EXACT same even occurred several time. Same voice, same text choices. It destroyed any semblance of "reality". This happened on several missions. Same story, same options, same characters.
When I found myself lost in the galaxy, enjoying the exploration, it was these kind of things that reminded me I was just playing a game. Same old repetitive stuff. Sometimes settings and rooms were identical on different missions.
I understand that you can only keep adding content for so long, but this is a game that requires that. 
I really liked it, until I didn't.
Some games can make proc gen work. Bethesda games can't.

I had a stroke back in November of 2023, lost two years of my life and had to recap on the Youtubes.  I came home and booted up Starfield.  Friends said I hated it, and I went okay, lets find out why.  Ran it off my brand new rig.

1st - It's Skyrim in space.

2nd- The ending is Bullsh*t.  For obvious reasons, point by point, this is my own personal take on it.  The rest of you guys will say you love it, and thats great, but thats not the reason why I am throwing this up here after 3 years.

Coming back to it to play some DLC's that included bug fixes, I avoided the main quest line because I just want to play a guy.  Just a GUY.   No a legally distinct, but not really, JEDI.

The Bugs are still there, crashing me straight to desktop when I do a distress call or trying to rescue mission in the UC territory.  I didn't play the Terran DLC because I still needed a few levels, but guess what?  That wasn't the problem.  I can't get into the region because it crashes me RIGHT back into Windows. 

I'm out.  Done.  I am heavily medicated, and highly agitated.

No stars.

It's boring. The Bethesda charm only goes so far. The stories themselves I feel like are interesting somewhat but rewards are far often not enough, not needed, or I keep asking myself why bother. The combat and animations really have shown that they are running extremely outdated technologies, or have decided not to innovate or improve. I understand for Skyrim, and Fallout New Vegas, they felt great. But for Starfield, it feels archaic, not fluid, and stiff. 

More often than not I found when switching weapons, reloading, vaulting, and more, to have delays when doing those actions. You get melee'd and blocked your reload... nice, now stuck in that LOOOONG reload animation again. Vault animation is only 1, looks and feels stiff to perform. With the jumppack, you either trigger the jumppack or a vault, and you don't get to decide unless you hold down the button it seems. 

It shines with the weapons, I will say. All of them are fairly unique. In terms of design quality, branded guns (such as wooden ones from Freestar... or energy ones from Varuun). The reloads themselves feel unique, special, and fun to see, but a lot of them are too long, which ties to the fluid-ness comment. 

I'm not asking for them to be Titanfall 2 terms of fluid animations and fluidness. But Starfield feels like it's a space game designed to slow you at every moment. 

What I would have loved to have it feel like, would be Cyberpunk 2077. You are always fully in control, and move as fast, or as slow as you like, and truly let's you express yourself in terms of movement.

And I played through the main story about what, 3 times now, finished the UC campaign with the terrormorphs, and some side content which was unrememorable. I ditched the game 2 years ago, if you look at my achievements, but with the new update, I figured i'd give it another go. And I remember now why I put it down. 

It's good for a playthrough, I guess. But you can't really sink into it, at least I couldn't. 

If it won't let me be good at combat, pointless quests, and a slog to just overall play. The exploration should have been more at the forefront then, which I found the randomly generated locations were actually more memorable, the "POIs". 


I did not touch the DLC as I wasn't high enough level and I was going to grind levels to hit it, but I gave up - again - because i was bored.

My issue with Starfield is that it doesn’t do the emergent Bethesda RPG thing where you’re exploring and coming across crazy stuff as well as other Bethesda games. And then, on the flip side, it’s also a space game that doesn’t do space gameplay as well as its contemporaries.

So we end up with this mishmash game that feels very disconnected from what’s come in the past. It’s almost like they forgot what made their games great and then also ignored all the great space games that have been released in the last 15 years.

And to top all of that, the game is full of all these crafting, cooking, base-building, and engineering systems... but they can all be completely ignored. None of them matter, like... at all.

Do I recommend the game? Yeah, sure I suppose. It’s decent. But in the grand pantheon of RPGs this doesn’t crack the top ten and the real disappointment is that Bethesda should have done better.

I can say that I gave this many, many tries and just ended up frustrated each time I played. I'll start by saying I really enjoyed the universe that is Starfield. The characters and locations were well developed. The stories being told were engaging. Graphically it was beautiful. It ran smoothly with no hiccups.
     The reason for the thumbs down is mostly GUI oriented. I'm not a console player. I can't use a controller to save my life. For me the GUI was made for controllers. I do use a mouse & keyboard and I guess that's why I had the problems that I did have. For example the infiniscroll for the inventory. It's the same in every Bethesda game, like the Fallout series. They overburden you with keystrokes which I believe is done for a controller. Backing out of menus instead of just closing the menu was a chore. Each category should be a toggle. Press one key to open something, press the same button to close it. Don't add another keystroke just to close the same menu. The quest list was a mess for me. To not be able to sort the quests by location was a flaw. I would have loved to have a quest guide on screen to tell me what I'm supposed to do, because to be honest I often found myself wondering where to go and what to do. Instead I have to open the quest panel and look at vague entries that don't really tell you where you're supposed to go or at the very least what system you should be in. All of theses things, and more, are why I would play for half an hour and quit frustrated.
     I really wanted to delve deep into this game and enjoy it's many stories it has to tell. But I just can't get behind the implementation of the game. If you're a Bethesda fan and enjoy their GUI, then this should be no problem for you. From what I played, it's a great game. I just couldn't adapt to it, or any Bethesda game for that matter.

I have a few hundred hours of playtime on xbox for this game.  I bought it on Steam for the Terran Armada and Free Lanes update.  The detail and storytelling in this game is deep and impressive.

As with most Bathesda games, it's superficial - lacking character, atmosphere and weight...

I played this game, on gamepass when it first came out to completion, and ran a few new game+...  Not  thinking it was that "bad".

But having picked it up again recently, I can't help but feel I've half robbed myself.

The game works, bugs aren't really apparent.  
The aesthetic - dated but passable.  It's still essentially Oblivion NPC's that can wiggle their eyebrows and tilt their head while talking.  
The gameplay - feels light and inaccurate, with no real feedback or weight.
The story/presentation - feels like it's aimed at an 8 year old.

While I understand with Bathesda games you have to accept the dry satire and attempts at wit, along with cliche quests and tropes...  

But as a game, it does nothing new, or well.

If you just want to "space out".  Not think too hard.  And are happy to be treated like a child.  I suppose this could occupy some of your time.  

But finding nothing really profound in my first play through, and now trying to pick it back up and see what's new.  I'm left wondering where the fun is.

Once you understand the plot, playing the game becomes pointless...  

On a side note:
Game companies need to be sophisticated, mature and edgy....  I don't want to play games made for pre-teens.

If corporations spent their profits on developing new technologies and new games...  Rather than shareholders and management.  Maybe the world would be a better, happier place.

I want depth, realism, immersion and a sense of achievement.   Having no skill required to play your game, in an inconsequential world - just leaves me feeling hollow and slightly dumber...

"Here have an Alien Kabab"...

Starfield is not a perfect game, and the Creation engine is growing long in the tooth.  But, with the Free Lanes update, Starfield finally feels complete.  Space now feels alive, as you travel between planets and moons, seeing sensor blips popup that you can divert to and investigate.  It brings exploration, one of the more enjoyable parts of Bethesda games, to the space flight half of the game.

Bethesda seems to have also leaned in to telling riskier, darker stories in the new quests added in the Free Lanes update.  At launch, Starfield had some great some good quest lines, but in too many places it seemed like Bethesda was really hesitant to lean in to themes that could be uncomfortable, and it left much of the game feeling bland and uninspired.  With Free Lanes, Bethesda shows that they still have writers and quest designers who can tell interesting stories, with interesting characters.

Mods help fill the game out even further, and though the Creation engine limits what Bethesda can do in other aspects (like needing to add loading screens rather than have a seamless world) there's no question it makes modding easier.

Ultimately, while the flying is not as engaging as in Elite, while the economy is simpler than the X series, while the exploration is not as deep as No Man's Sky, and while the story and characters are not as gripping as Mass Effect, I think Starfield has made a place for itself.  The customization, and ability to chart your own path and play the way you want to makes Starfield worthy of your time.

Very fun engaging game, lots of different quests. I like the exploring too, once I figured out to focus mainly on planets with flora and fauna and getting further out away from the central star systems. The graphics are amazing. I also like the fact it's set in a specific area of space that includes the Sol system. The planets and moons that don't have life are interesting to explore but don't have a lot of variety (which is the same as happens in real life, bouncing on a realistic version of our moon and Mars is fun but after a while it's more fun to explore planets that have life. I even managed to play this on my Steam Deck by dialing down the graphics to ultra low quality and maxing the fps at 30, although that's mainly for playing when I'm away from a decent WiFi/ethernet connection, I wouldn't recommend using that as the primary device to play this game. My biggest complaint, which also applies to both the Xbox and PS versions, is that the game often crashes or freezes. I've learned to constantly quicksave to not lose my progress. Other than that it's a pretty awesome game!

This game possesses the Bethesda mark of quality. That being it's very fun to play, and can be modded to the moon and back. Those are this game's two major saving graces. Fallout 4's vanilla experience is honestly sort of half-baked. You can tell it was ambitious, but it was definitely dependent on the community to really push it to its full potential. 

Performance for this game is... well it's a mess. But that's standard fare for a Bethesda game. Not that that's an excuse. Crashes, FPS dives, stuttering, glitches galore. And if you mod the game, get ready for a lot of troubleshooting. 

All that being said, I recommend this game. Mod the hell out of it, tailor it to your liking and you're in for a fun time.

I still remember when my friend suggested me this game in around 2020-2021-ish. I have never been a fan of story based games or RPGs in general. This game to me is one of the sole exceptions. I ended up coming to this game pretty late but it is one of the greatest games I've ever touched and one of the most re-playable games I've played. Fallout 4 hits the SWEETSPOT between story telling and compelling gameplay/progression. The game never felt like a monotonous movie nor did it feel dry of gameplay; it was perfectly tuned. The world is so vast and every corner is jam packed with detail; this game FEEDS curiosity till its bloated. The combat in this game is amazing too, giving tons of different combat options allowing you to cater your character to follow different specialties and ways of playing (which adds a lot to the re-play ability. On top of that its also very satisfying getting kills in this game. One thing which also really hooked me to this game is the settlement building mechanic; it is very thought out and intentional. This game is just packed with a diverse pool of gameplay and has so much detail put into it. I found myself enjoying everything and playing it again and again, building new settlements and factories, trying new builds and weapons and trying different routes in the story. This game feels like Risk of Rains Monsoon difficulty on all the wrong kinds of steroids. The game is unfair and overly punishing, but not in any good way. Dying because of chaotic or unexpected chemical reactions and spells being a bit stronger than you expected is one thing, but many of my deaths were due to the terrain just completely bugging out and getting me stuck on, or in the walls and floors. The pixel art completely fails to communicate key information, and often goes out of it's own way to OBSCURE crucial information, blood can cover entire sections of terrain and completely hide something capable of instantly killing you. I'm sure there's a lot of masochists that love how quickly you can go from full health to dead because of the tiniest things, but when that's happening because of borderline bugs and broken mechanics I don't think it's defendable. 

The roguelike structure is admirable, and making a cool build is fun, but it takes way too many hours to actually get a run where you're finally allowed to have fun. The best strategy is to go slow and carefully, exploring and clearing as much of each area as possible before moving on. This makes some sense, but means that each run can take half an hour or more, which doesn't make sense when very few runs will give you the perks and spells you need to win, and even if you do you're still likely to instantly die for no reason, possibly rendering an hour long run fruitless. The game is addictive but only in the same way gambling is, which is what it often felt like. "Surely this next run will be the one", but it never is, and the entire game can turn into a massive time waster very easily. 

The game has very little respect for your time, and very little concern for whether or not you're having fun. If that's something that sounds nice then go ahead, but this game was definitely not for me. I'd compare it to Tarkov. If you really love Tarkov and think that that one game where you extracted with a few million dollars made the hundreds of other games full of rats and cheaters and extract campers totally worth it, you might like this game.

Despite beign extremely frustrating, Noita is undoubtly a great game. Here's what I liked the most:
- Learning how to wrap spells to craft overpowered/god-killer wands and rolling on the map while making  enemies exploding into colourful fireworks
- Exploring the world and gameplay mechanics, there's a ton of wands configurations and perks synergies to play with (it's huge, really)
- Gaining more and more power by looting spells, items, extra hearts and perks
- Get gifted with a ton of dopamine when everything is aligned (haha)

Things I raged about:
- Way too hard/random mechanics. Some will agree, others will say that's a skill issue, but for me those are too much: almost undodgeable projectiles/enemies/particles, unpredictable one-shooting creatures, shots from out of screen... You'll often beign annihilited by - let's say  "miscalculated" - highly explosive baril hidden in snow pile or one shoted while under effects of a certain potion, dying drowned because stuck  between two pixels... And that's for the most raging deaths, but there's plenty of other sketchy ways to die.
- The first 3 zones can be rapidly very very very repetitive and get boring until you find a cool wand or fun perks to play with

For me Noita is like a gigantic sandbox with roguelike elements, you can go where you want and play as you want: gathering as much spells and buffs as possible before beating up every bosses in the game, rushing through the first zones to grab the most powerful wands and beat up the main boss, grab a killer wand as soon as possible to get to an particular optionnal boss, or many many other ways to play it! 

At +100hours, +350 deaths and 0 wins, I realized that the long sessions weren't worth it and ended up starting to speedrun it. Maybe I got very lucky, but that's the way I just got my first victory, thanks to the one-shot-everything wand I could craft and a bunch of very good perks. If like me RNG and sketchy events can strongly frustrate you maybe don't invest yourself too much in very long run and play it more in a speedrunner style? Or grab as much ressources as you want to become the ultimate alchemist in super-long runs. It's dense game, you'll find something you like.

The OST is great by the way!  Thanks Nolla :))

Bioshock Infinite – A Masterpiece Beyond Time (10/10)

Bioshock Infinite isn’t just a game; it’s an unforgettable experience that transcends storytelling, gameplay, and atmosphere. From the moment you step into the floating city of Columbia, you’re immersed in a breathtaking world full of wonder, mystery, and dark secrets lurking beneath its utopian facade.

The narrative is nothing short of genius, weaving together themes of American exceptionalism, quantum mechanics, and the cost of redemption in a way that no other game has done before. Booker and Elizabeth’s journey is filled with emotional weight, shocking twists, and some of the most unforgettable moments in gaming history.

Gameplay is fluid and exhilarating, blending fast-paced gunplay with the creative use of Vigors, sky-hook traversal, and strategic combat scenarios that make every encounter feel fresh and intense. The world-building is on another level, with every corner of Columbia telling a story, supported by a hauntingly beautiful soundtrack that enhances every moment.

And then there’s the ending—one of the most mind-blowing, thought-provoking conclusions ever seen in gaming. It’s the kind of story that lingers in your mind long after the credits roll.

Bioshock Infinite isn’t just a great game—it’s a masterpiece that stands as one of the greatest achievements in gaming history. 10/10, an absolute must-play.

A game has never left such a profound impression on me, the story is the most thought provoking and interesting video game stories I think I've ever encountered. It gives hints of the original games while still being fresh and different, allowing players that are new to the series to enjoy a Bioshock game without having to understand the origins, and the scenery is nothing short of breath taking.  I don't think anyone has done a companion NPC as good as Elizabeth since this game. She feels profoundly real, she doesn't repeat the same tired voice lines (in fact she never repeats a single one) or get stuck on scenery, she comments on the world around her, and interacts with the playable character as if she's her own person, it's truly an excellent portrayal of an NPC. This game isn't just a game, it's a thought provoking story told in the most beautiful and tragic way. A truly phenomenal game <3

Does anyone else have a hard time accepting that its been almost 20 years since the beginning of Bioshock and the amazing, unique universe these games brought us? Changed the way I view games, period. STORY is the centre of focus. I wish more contemporary series followed this blueprint. Nowadays it's all about massive sandbox, multiplayer, customisation, open worlds with both a LOT in them and simultaneously vacant, void of that *something*. Infinite had some of the most creative environments, but everything served a purpose. Looking back it's quite obvious the design drew inspiration from Studio Ghibli's Castle in the Sky among others. Some of the strongest stories, characters, most distinct styles. There will never be another, Bioshock will eternally have a special place in my heart.

This game was def. a slow burn, but 100% worth it!

Being a fan of the Bioshock series, and playing this completely blind, I was negatively surprised at first. This game looks different and plays different to prior games in the series. Less horror, more action. Also, while it is cool to see the city of Columbia at its height, it does not hit the same as your first visit to Rapture.

While the combat is fun, the only thing that really made this game special to me early on was Elizabeth. Having her be your companion, seeing her interact with the environment, and her being an active part of combat is what makes this game unique. Never have I seem a companion mechanic as well implemented as in this game. Sometimes you do see an oddity from Elizabeth that takes some of the immersion away, but she is never a hindrance (very important) and elevates the game.

Later in the game, things really start picking up. What at first seemed like a very plain (boring) story-line, suddenly starts twisting and turning. You no longer have any idea what is going on, focusing instead of just getting out of Columbia with Elizabeth. The action and the story both keep building and building until the final climax and ending.....and what an ending it is. Wow. I won't spoil it in any way. But the whole playthrough is worthwhile just for that. It will put your noggin in overdrive.

While it may not feel like it at first, this game is an experience. One you will not forget. Highly Recommended!
