Bioshock Infinite  │ ★ ★ ★ ★ ★

A man sent to look for a girl named Elizabeth in a floating city that was the stuff of dreams but conceals nightmares.” The city of Columbia is beautiful: “sunlight, flags, the songs of a bygone era” but the further you go, “the more you see the twisted truth.” “Religion, nationalism, racism – it’s all packaged in the beautiful utopia what makes it so special, though, is the Booker/Elizabeth connection. She doesn’t feel like just another character to shield while you play. She acts, speaks, and is interested in things in a way that makes you feel like you're in a world that is literally coming apart at the seams while you play, and you feel concern for Elizabeth by the end of it.
Game play-wise, it is definitely a combination of BioShock core elements and completely insane, action-packed fights where you would ride these insane skyline rails to fly across the fighting area in the middle of the battle with Vigors – which are simply mana-related powers – and firearms.

While it has some issues, this game has a special place in my heart. Despite what some people think, this is certainly a Bioshock game. Beautiful visuals, but also the worst of humanity on display. While the gameplay is a bit different than the first two games, it has the same heart. You scavenge any and all items, complete many side tangents and quests- while also creatively using your plasmids/vigors. 

The start of the game is certainly the high point, as the story does get a bit muddled. However, in my opinion, upon multiple playthroughs there's a LOT of foreshadowing, and it's very comprehensible once you start to connect the dots. The story makes a lot more sense to me now. However, for a first time player, it's not the easiest to process. Especially if you aren't willing to listen to all the audio logs and side tangents!

The games DLC are arguably better than the base game, it finds a good way of developing one of the main characters (Elizabeth) in a satisfying way while also giving a pretty enjoyable story.

Conclusively, the game has some rough edges, but it's very charming. The characters are interesting, the locations are great(despite some repetition). It's from that era of gaming right before scummy game practices took over, where it looks beautiful but it still has care put into it.
It's the worst Bioshock game, but that's still a pretty decent bar to clear.

BioShock Infinite is an absolute masterpiece that I had the pleasure of experiencing first on my Xbox 360 and later on PC, and each time it left me more amazed than before. From the moment I stepped into the floating city of Columbia, I was completely captivated by the game's stunning visuals, immersive world, and deeply compelling story. The narrative is masterfully crafted, blending themes of American exceptionalism, political ideology, and personal redemption in a way that keeps you hooked from start to finish.

The characters are nothing short of extraordinary—Elizabeth stands out as one of the most memorable and well-developed companions in gaming history. Her intelligence, personality, and the dynamic relationship she shares with Booker add incredible emotional depth to the journey. The game's gameplay is equally impressive, seamlessly combining first-person shooter mechanics with unique powers called Vigors, which introduce creative combat strategies and keep the action fresh and exhilarating throughout.

Beyond just Infinite, the entire BioShock series is a masterpiece of storytelling and design. Each installment offers a rich, atmospheric environment filled with thought-provoking themes and unforgettable characters. The series consistently pushes the boundaries of what video games can achieve both narratively and artistically. BioShock Infinite, in particular, showcases the pinnacle of this achievement, delivering a profound and thrilling experience that every gamer should play. It remains a shining example of how video games can be both entertaining and intellectually engaging.

Bioshock Infinite is my least favourite of the series; originally released in 2013, it received high praise at the time, but it also took some steps away from what made the series so effective. A lot has been made about the game that was presented at E3 2011 verses the final product, but the overall experience is still well-produced and very enjoyable.

Set in 1912, Booker Dewitt (voiced by Troy Baker) journeys to the floating city of Columbia to find an elusive woman named Elizabeth (Courtnee Draper) and clear his debts. Upon arrival, he runs afoul of Father Comstock and a desperate three-way war soon erupts. With multiple factions all vying for dominance and Elizabeth at the centre, a straightforward rescue mission quickly turns into a winding trek across time and space. You'll be partnered up with Elizabeth for most of the game; she's a very likeable companion who can also aid you with her use of tear portals. 

Infinite maintains the same great thematic undertones and phenomenal voice acting of its predecessors alongside some brilliant presentation. Where Rapture was dingy and broken-down, Columbia is bright and extravagant, a setting with themes of religious fundamentalism, anarchy and robber-baron capitalism. The story's one major weakness is a lack of development for its factions; when Booker and Elizabeth embark on a multidimensional rollercoaster, the machinations of both Comstock and his biggest opponents, the Vox Populi, are pushed to the side, stifling their depth and complexity.

The core gameplay has also been altered, though not necessarily for the better; riding the sky rails is always exhilarating, but some changes feel like regressions. While Booker can carry every vigor (this game's precursor to plasmids), he can't carry more than two weapons at a time, creating a more situational structure that limits your options in combat. The sense of exploration is also even more narrow than Bioshock 2; with the map system removed, you'll often have to memorise previous locked doors. All of these changes make Bioshock Infinite move away from the franchise's immersive sim roots; the heavier use of set piece moments also takes control away from the player more frequently, something the first two games avoided.

So yes, it is the weakest of the trilogy, but Bioshock Infinite is still offers a fun time with intense combat, superb performances and gorgeous technical details. The Burial At Sea DLC is also recommended (The second episode more than the first) for the way it rounds off the series and brings everything full circle.

My extended retrospective on the franchise can be viewed here: https://youtu.be/jHe6ckTflSA?si=Gap55_9c_24IJpBr

Bioshock Infinite is a far from flawless game in a series where each game was forever forced to be put up against the genre altering shadow of the original. And yet I will never play a game that made me feel quite the same as it did. Even against the others in the series there is something uniquely itself to this game. Whether it be in it's shining city above the clouds that for a moment can make you feel larger than life, or in it's admittedly imperfect story that even though it is absolutely flawed- will forever hold a place in my heart as one that can make you feel like you're part of something greater (even if you have no idea what your doing.) Like I said of course, this game is like the others in the series- imperfect. It like all the others (especially 2) has some gameplay issues with many gameplay systems and things, and shows it's age as the years tick by. However if you can look beyond those flaws as we often do so much with the others in the series, I'd say this is a game that- though was unplanned to be the final of the series (for now at least) is a charming and tasteful change from the city of Rapture that was able to shake things up just enough from the others and leave things off from the series with a sense of true finality. So if by some miracle you read this and haven't seen or played this game yet (but like games like this of course) there is nothing I could recommend more than to give this game a chance and to see it as what it is- not what it isn't. For as the Lutece Twins said "Perception without comprehension is a dangerous combination."

Es, básicamente, uno de los mejores juegos de la historia. Es un título que derrocha calidad desde donde se le mire, conservando su excelente estilo artístico y manteniendo la jugabilidad con concepto que siempre ha estado presente desde su primera entrega. Los personajes ahora toman un papel mucho más trascendental en la trama principal y se crea un ambiente propicio para hacernos sentir afecto por los mismos, preocuparnos por sus condiciones y decidir cuidadosamente cada cosa que queremos que hagan durante nuestra partida, todo controlado por un guion magistral, que en alguna que otra ocasión se pierde entre tanta ambición, pero que al fin y al cabo logra dejar huella y convertirse en lo que en un principio se propuso a convertirse, y es que una historia como la que ofrece Bioshock Infinite no logra salirse de nuestra mente por más que queramos sacarla. Es en este título en donde vemos reflejado que, para un artista, es muy difícil dejar a un lado todas las ideas que se le vienen a la mente, porque todas esas ideas forman parte de un todo. El artista debe comprender que hay que prescindir de muchas características para lograr una obra maestra, y es por eso que muchos de los íconos de Infinite aparecen solo cuando se necesitan, actúan cuando deben hacerlo y ofrecen su aporte a la trama justo en las cantidades necesarias. Es una obra maestra, y como toda obra maestra, tiene sus imperfecciones, y por eso mismo es que es un FPS de primera calidad. Si antes se pensaba que Bioshock tenía potencial, ahora sí que es algo que se puede afirmar con total seguridad; al fin y al cabo, hay muchas puertas, todas conducen al mismo sitio, pero de manera diferente.

Replayed it recently after having it finished many years ago when it came out.

Even when put in context of shooters back then, IMO, it is just not a good game, especially when compared to other Bioshock games.

First, the (subjectively) good stuff:
- story is great! I like the meta-commentary a lot and the way it ties together all Bioshock games
- locations and environmental storytelling is top notch as well
- Elizabeth is one of the first player-friendly NPCs that doesn't need constant pampering and is actually helpful in right moments, without becoming a crutch

The negatives:
- on higher difficulties you'll face challenges which - when combined with limited ammo and even more limited salts - are almost impossible to beat without dying.
- about 70% of the time when I died - I didn't feel it was my fault, but that the game simply pushed me into a corner. Sure, it could be because of defensive, cautious choices - but it just doesn't feel good to be put in a place where you have good defensive position to hold, and more enemies than you can beat with available ammo and salts.
- secrets - they often require backtracking a whole lot, so its not unusual that you find a piece of a puzzle and then have to play for another 30 minutes before you find another. This length often feels like you missed it, and when traversing locations its not always clear whether you'll be able to return back, so you end up checking locations twice or thrice in search of something you might've missed.
- vigors - some are quite useful, but I spent almost whole game using 2-3 only as the others simply didn't warrant salt cost for the benefit. Traps were OK but rarely was I in situation where I could deploy them and wait for enemies to come to me

Finally - the skyhook mechanics felt literally crammed in. I understand that the game was far larger in original vision and skyhook was a way to traverse between various regions - but it still feels like a gimmick.


Either way - there's a lot of very cool ideas here, but they're underbaked. If someone ever makes a new Bioshock game, whether under the sea or in the sky, they don't have to look far for great ideas and references.
IMO, I doubt I'll ever play this again.

I have played through the campaign, two dlc episodes and a couple hours of Clash in the Clouds when reviewing.

Campaign was very good. Story was interesting, gameplay mechanics adequate and the atmosphere a lot brighter than previous games. Story gets dark, but it's almost always sunny in Columbia. Playing with Elizabeth was best, really enjoyable and added gameplay quirks that were brilliant. I think this game works if you haven't played Bioshock before, but being familiar with the concept adds to the experience.

For me, having played the two previous games just recently makes the two dlc episodes the crown jewel here. Especially the second Burial at sea is beyond words. I don't want to write about it if someone wants to still play the trilogy through. Hell, you can straight skip Bioshock 2 if you want. It was 75% pain to play, 25% one of the best fps ever. STORYWISE, gameplay was bad all the way.

Conclusion: Get the trilogy on sale. Find a wooden stick, set it in your mouth and play from the beginning. Bite the stick when annoyed with the gameplay in two first games, explore and enjoy the story. Get to this one and remove the stick. It's okay now, relax. Play, notice there is NO RESEARCH in this game and enjoy. You will be sad how things turned out, but satisfied with the way the story was told and ENDED properly.

If I could put an in-between yes and no rating.... I would. Game has incredible potential, it just needs.. more. 

I know its not crusader kings 3 level story telling + diplomacy, but it should have. This game could be the best medieval kingdom simulator on the market, its in the top 10, but there are other games in the overall genre that do other things better than M&B2. (city upgrades, unit diversity, viewable progression for example.)

One being overall world and character content. 

More Armor sets, weapons, character control updates, specific treed storylines, hell even an improvement to the unique characters of the game (companions) 

MOST of this stuff is available via mods, which do help. Quality of life improvements, graphic upgrades, fantasy, true medieval mods all at a really high level, thanks to the community, but it should not be down to them. For the average person, they will never know where to start with installing many of them, and its just an extra pain in the arse to deal with.

That being said, I will still recommend new players try the game, as your first playthrough will be worth the money you spend on the game. 

Writing this at 191 hours and a "few"(10?) playthroughs in, this game does have something special, the dev's just should've worked harder at developing the base game further instead of focusing on cash grab extra DLCs, there were better time investments they could've made.  I will still continue to play the base game as well as the copious amounts of mods.

Summary:
The ultimate medieval sandbox, where having a lot of lack of skill doesn't stop you from having the time of your life. TaleWorlds literally took my childhood dreams and turned them into this epic war simulator.

🎮 Gameplay:
[ ] Just press W
[ ] Fun
[x] Goodbye social life
Opinion: Honestly, I want to buy a real full suit of armor just to wear while playing. Leading a gigantic charge of soldiers straight into enemy lines may not be historically accurate, but it provides a Hollywood-worthy dopamine rush. It's simply amazing. Say goodbye to your real-world responsibilities and hello to medieval perfection.

🎨 Graphics:
[x] Runs on a microwave
[ ] Respectable pixel art
[ ] Melted my graphics card
Opinion: Somehow, this large-scale war is optimized enough to run super well on the potato I proudly call my PC. The magnitude of the battles is incredibly satisfying — watching hundreds of soldiers burning, being riddled with arrows, and desperately trying to scale the castle walls is a truly chaotic visual delight.

🎵 Audio:
[ ] Play while listening to a podcast
[x] Serves its purpose
[ ] Mozart is crying in the shower
Opinion: I literally ignored the music because I wanted to completely immerse myself in the raw and brutal sounds of war. The sound design of the battles themselves is top-notch. Hearing the chaotic clash of steel and the desperate cries of your army provides all the audio you need to fulfill your warlord fantasy. Absolutely no complaints.

🐛 Bugs:

[ ] It's a feature

I like this game, but it is not worth the price tag.  The interface is goofy, the messaging is goofy, the controls are goofy.  Actual combat is like playing with built in latency.  It is not in anyway intuitive.  Controls are not intuitive, you miss actual messages and they're just gone.  The screen shots/movies they show are some fly-by shots, but I have no idea how they've done this, because it's not in the game that way.  yeah,  you can see it coming and you can play it that way in First person, but...it's not very workable.  Also, the individual AI for the soldiers is kind of dumb, they all clump together,  For as old as this game is, i would think there would be some serious improvements to the UI and operability.  I understand that a lot of mods are used to make this game playable, or at least replayable.  Some of the base systems are implemented ideas that don't have real system depth.  They almost feel like last minute "Oh yeah we can do that." or worse, let's do this, but we don't have the time so just button it up.   The siege system needs work you really need to cheese it to make it functional.  There does not appear to be much improvement from the 2016 video for gameplay.  And don't think you'll be that good with a bow, you need to learn that skill from 0. All of your siblings will have better stat's than you, it's like you spent your youth contemplating navel lint and not preparing to lead the "clan/family/dynasty" whatever.   Of course all of that said, I still like this game, but I wouldn't pay full price for it.  The stated $49.99 is just too much for clunky non functional first person commands and actions.  Or ones you have to cheese to make work..  And this is why I lagely wouldn't recommend the game.  Price too high, controls are impossible.

It scratches an itch. Bannerlord have a style of game that could be the sweet spot between Civ style world/kingdom building with a mix of Total War battles. However the execution is verging on poor. 

Vanilla game is okay, its not great. If you want to have any kind of fun then you will absolutely need mods as the base systems and features just arent there. Mods for diplomacy, larger battles and armies are a must.

The positives are; 
- you can play the game multiple different ways (Merc, Trader, Vassel, Ruler) and its completely open as to how you want your character to play out, it doesnt force you into a specific type. 
- The world, for the most part, moves pretty consistantly and things constantly change, kingdoms lose towns and castles and armies declare war on you out of the blue.  
- Battles can be fun and do require some thinking, especially if you find yourself outnumbered. 
- Seiges are a great feature, you just lack the ability to control them well.
- The kingdoms are well thought out and styled and the database that is there could very easily be expanded upon and made to be more interactive.

Cons are; 
- Dialouges are rough and uninteresting, every NPC interaction is identical and repetitive. 
- AI lords and leaders are terrible when it comes to tactics, both in seiges and in battles.
- Wars are persistent and sometimes seem pointlessly invoked and when this happens continuously as you are trying to rebuild or work on other things it came become incredibly frustrating. 
- Graphics are very poor. 
- There is so much potential in the towns and villages if they were more interactive.
- Hideouts are bland and monotonous. 

Overall the potential was huge, but its a basic rendition and for a while it is fun. I would recommend simply for the joy of the first playthrough, after that the cracks in the game start to spoil the overall enjoyment. Buy on discount

NBA 2K23 stands out as one of the most refined and engaging basketball simulations in the series, skillfully blending realistic gameplay improvements with nostalgic content to create a well-rounded experience for fans of all kinds. On the gameplay front, the introduction of the Adrenaline Boost system revolutionizes stamina management by dividing it into three distinct segments, effectively discouraging repetitive dribble spamming and encouraging smarter, team-oriented play. This adds an extra layer of strategy to pacing and decision-making. The revamped Pro Stick controls, alongside more nuanced shooting mechanics and expanded badge customization, provide players with a fresh, dynamic feel on offense. Defensively, the AI has seen improvements as well, displaying smarter rotations, better help defense, and more realistic contesting, although occasional moments of AI hesitation, especially during pick-and-roll plays, remind players there’s still room for growth.

Visually, NBA 2K23 impresses with next-generation fidelity—player models are detailed and lifelike, lighting and shadows create a polished atmosphere, and broadcast-style overlays enhance the immersion, particularly in The City hub. Animations throughout the game feel smooth and expressive, from signature dribbles to impactful finishes at the rim. While minor frame drops and stutters can appear during busy moments in the hub world, overall performance remains solid on current hardware.

Mode-wise, NBA 2K23 shines with the introduction of MyNBA Eras, a standout feature that transports players through different historical periods of the NBA complete with authentic rosters, rules, and presentation styles. This mode serves as a compelling playground for basketball history enthusiasts. The return of The Jordan Challenge adds another layer of nostalgia, allowing players to relive iconic Michael Jordan moments enhanced by era-specific filters and cinematic presentation. Meanwhile, MyCareer continues its evolution, offering a more compact and accessible The City environment filled with layered missions and fast travel options. Though immersive, the narrative-driven approach can sometimes feel intrusive, bogging down the gameplay with fetch quests and cutscenes that don’t appeal to everyone.

MyTeam remains content-rich and addictive but is also notable for its grind-heavy progression and aggressive microtransaction model. While new modes like Triple Threat and revamped contracts add variety, the persistent pay-to-win perception and frequent meta shifts continue to frustrate many players.

Community responses reflect this mixture of praise and criticism. Many applaud the game’s realistic gameplay improvements and the depth offered by the era modes, while others express frustration with occasional sluggish movement, defensive AI quirks, and the demanding shot timing mechanics. Performance issues are rare but can surface during intense moments in The City, though most users enjoy a steady 60 frames per second on modern consoles and PC.

In conclusion, NBA 2K23 represents a significant step forward for the franchise, delivering sharper, smarter gameplay paired with a broad variety of modes that cater to simulation purists, history buffs, and career-driven players alike. While microtransactions and some narrative-heavy elements may detract from the experience for some, the game’s overall polish, depth, and authenticity make it arguably the best NBA 2K installment yet, solidifying its place as a must-play for basketball fans seeking both challenge and nostalgia.

This game is really a "mixed" bag of stuff.

Arkane Studios that developed games like Prey, Deathloop and the Dishonored series, it creates a high expectations and standards which Redfall sadly does not meet based on those games.
Having said that, I don't feel that Redfall deserves the hate it's given, but at the same time sadly it's understandable.
There is no denying that Redfall has it's bugs, from player characters gliding along/through the floor, player characters that look like they want a hug once loaded into the main base/safe house (I really find that funny), to a random Vampire that's stuck in place and enemies that spawn inside the floor or walls.

The difficulty seems to behave oddly. As 4-players we started on Daylight to find our footing and then changed it to Dusk because Daylight had very little encounters. After the difficulty change the first 2 nights of playing was interesting and felt balanced with the weapon drops and the enemy difficulty, but after that it's been one shot-one kill, even with a pistol. However loading into a single player game with the same difficulty and character used in multiplayer, one is killed by the first vampire encountered outside the safe house.

It is essentially in the same style as games like Left 4 Dead, Back 4 Blood, Earthfall, Vermintide, Zombie Army and World War Z (minus the 3rd person) but not as chaotic as these.
If you enjoy those type of games it might be worth a try, but get it on sale, I would suggest around 75% off or more, which is when we bought it.

There's a decent amount to explore in Redfall and missions to complete along with side missions and each character has different abilities.
We're enjoying it a lot, bugs and all.
I'm recommending it because I'm enjoying it and hopefully there will be more patches out sometime this year that will fix the bugs.
has a lot of potential. will likely check it out again when it's $20 in two years after development is finished.

Meh. That's all I can really say about this game. It's not terrible, but it's far from good. A few creative ideas on display, but I don't like the cartoon art style at all, the story is fairly mundane, finding bits of lore is unsatisfying (partly because you never know whether or not you've seen an item, because it never disappears from the game world or otherwise lets you know you've picked it up), and the depiction of the Reverend is absolutely ridiculous and at times deeply inappropriate.

The shooting doesn't feel solid or satisfying, and though the game seems open world, it's actually a pretty linear campaign. You can play as a few different characters, but you can't swap freely between them. The save system is not as generous or intuitive as it should be in a game where you could die at any moment. Might be worth playing if you like vampire lore and the game goes on sale, but otherwise, a purchase I definitely regret.
Game has it's issues, sure. It's nowhere near as bad as reviews/bandwagoners make it out to be. Grab a few friends, run around and kill vampires. You'll have fun.

Update after 55+ hours. So have got to the end and completed the game once over in 'Dusk' difficulty level and all the side missions + neighbourhoods. I'll admit to not reading all the little notes and I found about 60 of the 100 grave notes. 

I'll admit to really loving the game in the end and got addicted eventually, but only after the first 5 or 6 hours of play. I don't know what it is about the early experience of it that put me off, but once I was in control, I started to settle in and couldn't put it down.

I loved the game mechanics, despite a handful of minor bugs here and there (I did pick the game up for the first time 12 months post release, so I guess most things people complained about were fixed).

It's pretty immersive, the art direction is pretty incredible, loved the music - Story could have been a little better, but you do get into it more by "Burial Point" (which is the 2nd area map / set of neighbourhoods).

Looking at my game achievement scores, I can see that only 12% of people who buy this game complete it and a lot of the achievements have very low player percentage of achievement, which is a shame really. Think more people should have given the game a chance and I'm pretty surprised by the 'mostly negative' reviews.

If you're sitting on the fence about it, I would say the game doesn't disappoint. It's entertaining to play, especially with a friend and we both loved it. Most of the negativity is related to the first 12 months where the game was released at full price as a 'finished' game, but was largely buggy and incomplete. Those issues have since been resolved for the most part (although some minor bugs still remain end of May 2024).

Update after 18 hours:  So, I have to say, I am enjoying this game. The negative rep this game has attracted is not the sum of its parts in my opinion. I'm playing along with a friend and neither of us can understand why people find it awful. It's a fun shooter. 

I suppose they could have gone full horror with it, but to be honest I quite like the cheeky lightheartedness of it. That's just a matter of personal styling, I think it would work either way. There's plenty of variety to keep you immersed in the game. The loot you're picking up to gain cash which you can use to buy better weapons if available at the safe houses, restock on ammo, etc.

I like the different missions, the music design is well thought out and I personally like the graphics.

There are a few glitches here and there, opening some of the locked cases, the sliding instead of walking of team mates sometimes... But to be honest they are very small, not really noticeable and don't ruin the overall enjoyment of the game.

If this is still available at a low price, I would say the game is a steal and definitely worth the money.

So I've played around three hours or so and I don't consider this game to be as bad as a lot are still saying. It seems people are hating on it just because the pitchforks are out, or maybe it got off to a bad start almost a year ago now. I know I'm late to the party, but I'm enjoying this version as it stands so far. I wouldn't say it's the best game I've ever played, but I certainly don't dislike it by any stretch. So what do I like and dislike?

Well I got it on offer for £14.99 and in my opinion, it's definitely worth that. I like the story, I like the artwork and scenery. The missions are interesting and I'm enjoying the exchange of fire with vamps and hollowmen! So in terms of beauty and entertainment, I'd say it gets high marks.

So where is it failing? I'm playing solo game coop with a friend and we're having a lot of fun... But, the reward/loot factor is too high - We're always fully stocked up and so the challenge diminishes. This game has a balance problem and I think they could do with addressing that. I've only played a few hours, so I'm early into third or fourth mission, so the challenge could get harder...(?)

The music and sound design is excellent, love that. But the sound direction feels off. It's a bit disorientating hearing an enemy behind, but it's not there at all. Maybe that's just me, not sure.

I'll be sure to come back and add to my review after a few more hours and missions in. But so far, if you're sitting on the fence, I'd say go for it. There's a lot worse games out there at higher prices. Redfall deserves another chance!

Despite all the unnecessary hate and toxic reviews I actually found this game to be an ambitious attempt at creating a new IP that didn't tick all the the boxes that Arkane Studios had hoped.

The game has various types of bugs that are common to see in multiplayer games but nothing that has made me get frustrated with the game if anything just makes me laugh as you see an enemy slide across the screen but we know it will get patched soon as this is the norm with games these days but it didn't stop me from carrying on.

I do think the price for this type of game should be lower or should have had a lot more graphical polish made to it so its less fortnite looking and more art styled or realism looking and much better cut-scenes rather than the slide shows.

When it comes to gameplay I found it fun to play alone and with friends, some characters are better than others and could have been better abilities to make them feel more unique than a basic healing class or sniper class.

The game feels very much like a casual game to play with friends and a grind for better guns while playing on harder difficulties as the dusk level is very easy for solo players and even on hard with friends is still pretty easy but its nice to just switch off and have fun with friends.

The story is a bit weak and predictable which makes it feel like the mechanics and style of the game was chosen before the story so you kinda switch off with friends and solo you feel like you want to skip some of the cringe dialogue depending on your character.

I like that the vampire nests and wish they had done more with it or added some other things like this to break up the repetition in the game but sadly it can feel repetitive once your in the second map area.

Its a shame this game is getting so much hate as its still a fun game despite its flaws but could have been much better and maybe if the price was lower then maybe it would of been received better but this game does not deserve all the hate its getting.

This game really is not as bad as people are making it out to be. I had a pretty decent time just going solo through the story and messing around with builds. That being said, however, it is still absolutely not a 70 dollar title. With the state of which this game was released, it should have been priced as half as much. I think I speak for just about everyone when I say that I wish it was a 70 dollar game. But quite frankly, it just isn't, that's just the sad truth. Arkane is capable of much more.

Most of the negatives I have towards this game are not even that major and it really just needed more time and polishing overall. I would have loved to see more variance in the weapons or even had somewhat of a workbench/crafting type system to customize weapons. I feel like the idea of jury rigging guns to fit your needs fits the post apocalyptic overtones the game has already and would have been a nice touch thematically and also mechanically speaking. It also would have been nice to have more fleshed out skill trees for the characters. It should have more meaning as to which path you wanted to specialize in rather than just slightly enhancing an ability and getting a few generic perks. The npcs also have little to no variation and their AI is honestly pretty abysmal. It feels like you're either being absolutely lasered by an enemy while behind cover or you're watching them run slowly and aimlessly in circles with no in-between. And, as "open" as the world may be, it also feels so empty for a majority of the game. 

Gunplay overall feels alright and I don't mind the "cartoonish" graphics some people have commented on. To me, it really is a style choice. The story overall was also sorta okay despite having the cop out comic book style "cutscenes" from npcs that have little to no depth.

In short, if you can play this game for free through game pass or are getting this on sale for at least half off, it's decent enough to sink a few hours in and forget about it afterwards. I would not recommend buying this game full price unless they really make some major changes. I sadly doubt they will though and will more than likely just take their cash and just take the L.

Okay, so.. I bought the ultimate edition of Redfall. I played it for two hours on stream with my friend. I rarely, if ever, refund games, even bad ones. But with this one, I refunded it and got my money back. It is NOT worth 70 dollars.. I enjoyed what I did play, but what I played.. was perhaps, the most.. ungodly newbie ass shooter ever.. I liked a lot of the mechanics and design of redfall.. but the ai was boring as hell, NONE.. and I mean -NONE- of the enemies were a challenge, not even close. idk what version IGN was playing but that was -NOT- what we got. 

 We were playing on normal and nothing was a challenge, everything died in a couple hit. We were getting the top guns dropping left and right.. half the time the ai would just sit there and let us kill em. Most of the missions we played were simple go here, kill x I think eventually itll be good, but.. it needs a lot of work.. the characters were very interesting and unique.. but their abilities broke the game. 

My friend had the teleport ability and -any- terrain design was negated instantly due to the low cooldown rapid nature of teleporting.. any tactical reasoning was just negated instantly. Stealth is a joke, completely not even necessary whatsoever.. the shell of the game is great, but I refunded that ♥♥♥♥.. and I don't do that lightly. If AAA publishers think, that we are just gonna pay 70 bucks for broken, ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ games, they need to pull their heads out of their asses. Cuz I can go and buy a game from an indie dev team at a tenth of the cost and get far more enjoyment, longevity and willingness to financially support them, than the f'n crap we are getting from triple A publishers.

Essentially, wait for an extremely deep sale and just wait and see if they do anything, cuz the game right now is not even remotely worth the price tag.

Overall it's a well balanced FPS; I have enjoying playing it this far. The map is big enough with a lot of opportunities for exploration in between quests.

2) You can't save and if you die you return to the closest base.
If you have realistic expectations and enjoy Arkane's games, and if you managed to snag this game on sale, I can confidently say it's a decent experience.

I got the game hoping for something closer to Arkane's previous games. Yes I saw the reviews, but I held out hope that the game had since been fixed. While the game is completely playable, it didn't have any of the elements I'd hoped for. It didn't feel immersive. Stealth is hardly an option, but only if you play as Jacob and spec into the tree in a specific way. Combat is hardly better. Enemies' behavior is unintelligent, and their animations often break. Looking for a good story? Find a different game. It could have made for a very story rich game, but it seems they'd spent all their budget on the world creation. That being the only reason I'm leaving a thumbs-up. If you're the type of person who just likes to explore an interesting looking world, this one is one of the most interesting worlds I've seen in a video game. Often gorgeous. But I myself am more interested in the gameplay. Nothing special there. In fact, it often felt clunky to navigate the world due to the detail put into it. I don't know the details of why the game turned out so terribly, but it's a shame what happened anyways.

In conclusion: I recommend only getting the game if you're mostly interested in the "unique" world they'd crafted. There's apparently an easy difficulty if that's what you're looking for. The game sometimes goes on sale for 50% off, so I'd wait and get it then.

Note: In order to get through the licensing agreement when I first booted up the game, I had to do some trick I found online. I don't remember exactly what I had to do, but if you have the same issue, you can look it up.

A cautionary tale of what happens when you force a studio to pivot to live service games. Redfall is Far Cry 5 meets Borderlands 2, except just a little bit worse and with a vampire-hunting coat of paint. Gone are the immersive sim elements that Arkane is known for; it's a generic looter shooter with a modest open world and slightly above average gunplay. 

The game itself is passable, hardly the disaster that it has a reputation for being, but if you're paying more than $20 for this game then you shouldn't be. Go buy Prey or Deathloop instead.

I really like this game, it's a lot of fun, I also don't really have any performance issues, it works as it should. How there's multiple but divided main quests, some fun sidequests, helping the neighbourhoods to make sure the safe houses are actually safe. I haven't tried it out co-op yet, but it's still a lot of fun to play solo as well. 

But I got some things I need to mention that brings it down for me, enough to not give it a higher rating. Ideas that could maybe, be fixed, or not. I don't know. It's more or less just nitpicking from my side.
1. The nests respawns. Which doesn't make any sense if you think about it. You help out in the different neighbourhoods, and then cleanse the nest to get rid of .. well. The nests. So for me it doesn't make sense that all the sudden there's a new one that spawn almost on the exactly spot you already cleanse. It's an annoying grind.
2. You can't pause the game when you are playing solo. I get why, but it's still an annoying factor. I understand you can't do that when you are playing with others, but when you are playing solo. Nah. I don't like that. They should have done like Sons of the Forest.
3. This is just a nitpicking point for me, but maybe other likes it. But you can't upgrade your weapons. So you get this amazing legendary weapon. You level up. Find better weapons but not good in the extras thingies, and have to leave that legendary weapon behind. I don't like that, I wish to be able to upgrade my already good weapons in damage, and level. Use the currency you have in the game to be able to do that. Just, something.

But, despite those three points, I still recommend the game, a lot. Tons of fun :)

If you come into the game wanting non-stop combat as a key element, it won't really be what you're looking for. Approach the game the way it's presented—a town to explore that was torn apart from the inside so you need to fight to save its remaining community, with oodles of environmental storytelling bolstered by PC and enemy dialogue—and you should have a better time.

Redfall is a town beset by vampires and their cultists, but the situation had built up over time—weeks of neighbors acting strange, people going missing. That's why it's desolate, houses boarded up, shops shuttered.

This isn't a town dense with corpses turned undead. It's a town devastated by people turning on their community, sacrificing neighbors to a couple people in power. Yes, there's space between fights—because untold numbers of townies are gone, or left dead in the streets. If you take the time to look while you run around, there's a TON of crafted environmental elements underlining all that. So many notes, items, and corpses indicating clear, unique moments throughout the town.

I personally find the space between enemy groups a boon, because even while playing solo, you can't pause. So it's nice to be able to find an empty area to hide when a brief break is needed, without having to go back to a safehouse. And yeah, the game's got bugs—but what big game doesn't on launch? There's too many moving parts at this scale, within the game and for folks' respective setups. I personally haven't had anything game-breaking, so while I look forward to patches, I'm having fun poking around Redfall and figuring out just what the hell these vampire gods did.

Aesthetically, characters are stylized rather than hyper-realistic, which I personally prefer. It helps to avoid the uncanny valley 3d is prone to, and prevents looking “dated” as quickly because the exaggerations are deliberate. There's also a good bit of dialogue from key NPCs that is custom to the PC you play—using the PC's name in greetings and goodbyes, plus extra little chats that happen when you approach them. Stuff like Eva asking Dev if this is the weirdest ♥♥♥♥ he's seen, Anna asking whether he's got new inventions he's working on, etc., which make the connection to the world feel just that much more solid. I've also had the unique vampires call out my character by name, which caught me off guard the first time—can't say I was expecting the vampires to actually be sharing info with each other. I would like if there were some way to get a little voiced banter with the other PCs while playing solo, because I LOVE all the characters. But it's also fun reading the color-coded notes on the whiteboards, and Devinder's solo commentary is absolutely AMAZING on its own, so I am well entertained.

What really got me into this game is the tone. I really like the graphics and the autumn style. The gunplay is great, the story alright. The only thing that really annoys me is the braindead NPCs, and I mean only the human NPCs. The vampires are alright. The game is a blast on PC.  

Performance:  It struggled to run properly until I updated my graphics drivers and set the game to windowed mode, now it's flawless.  I hear console players are struggling a bit more.

Content:  It's not an action packed non-stop roller-coaster like some of the ads suggested.  It's basically an abandoned town setting with a few groups of humans and the random vampire scattered around.  There are main quests, side quests, repeatable quests, open world enemies, many many chests/bags/trunks to loot, and from what I hear... a whole second map after you finish the first one (I haven't finished the first map yet, cannot confirm).  

Quests:  The main quests are fun and not cookie cutter.  There's definitely stories to go along with them, if you choose to read them.  You get side quests from NPCs at your main base and from the "Safe Zones" that you unlock.

Setting:  "Spooky" doesn't do it justice.  Between the music, the lighting, and the little details strewn about, it can get quite intense.

Replayability:  There are several methods of "grinding" (for xp/loot) like the repeatable "Nests" and the "Rook Storm" just to name a couple.  

Difficulty:  There are three difficulty settings at the start (easy, medium, hard) and "very hard" locked until you beat the game.  I've been playing on "hard" since the start and I find it just challenging enough.  I die occasionally but I feel strong when I wipe a group of vamps or gunners.  I look forward to playing the "very hard" mode.

What I'd like to see added:

The ability to upgrade/customize your own base.
Improvements to human AI (vamps seem fine).
More performance improvements.
More encouragement to play multiplayer.
The ability to join random games (can currently only play with "Friends").

I rate it a 8/10.
Even with the poor performance aside, the world is mostly empty/lifeless. Feels only partially finished which is especially bad given Arkane's usually amazing world building AND the 70$ price tag.
Happy to see this games been fixed I have no issues fun game to play and well worth a try, I can easily recommend getting this game enjoy.
Thank goodness I got this for free. I couldn't imagine paying 70$ for a AAA game with less content than most EA games. Arkane really botched this one.

Devs seemed to have ♥♥♥♥♥♥ off as well, so don't get bite back. those characters are not appearing. ever.

I bought the deluxe edition off CD Keys for cheaper than the regular version on Steam. I have played through the game on 2 characters and here are my thoughts. The game has a solid premise and is "fun" but I cannot recommend it for the price or length of the game. If you can find it on a crazy good sale or have game pass and can play for free then give it a try, but don't have high expectations.

Pros:
- Difficulty does not effect achievements or in game drops (from what I can tell)
- Fun premise, think Borderlands and Resident Evil 4 had a vampire Psycho baby and let it run wild.
- The characters are all unique and their abilities make each one fun in their own way.
- A decent story that unfolds through a play through with added bits in "files" you can find and audio collectibles (gravelocks)
- Each character has a legendary weapon type specifically for them.

Cons:
- A multitude of glitches that have yet to be patched. From main story missions glitching on steps until you exit, change difficulty and reload (sometimes multiple times if the quest has several steps that can glitch), side quests showing up that you've already completed, environmental collision, poor enemy AI (even on harder difficulties), sound glitches that make alarms continue going off until you exit the game and reload, 
- Overall limited weapon selection. Few legendary weapons that are shared between all characters: 4 pistols, 4 shotguns, and 3 assault rifles. All other non legendary weapons have little variety and aren't far off from the legendary in stats
- No random match making, you have to have friends lists
- Must always be online to play even in single player
- Story is fairly short, you can play through the campaign on easiest difficulty in about 8-12 hours depending on if you want to explore some side quests (which are also limited)

This game had so much potential but sadly I doubt it will ever get fixed to the point it should have been at launch. Maybe we'll get another No Mans Sky rise to glory but I doubt it.
Surprisingly fun, good romp. on the whole give it a good 8.5/`10

Sadly whatever chance this title had at even a modest comeback has been utterly destroyed thanks to Microsoft. By no means was this game a banger at launch but from the same company that made Prey no less it's disappointing to see how far they fell from the mark.

game: The Lord of the Rings: Gollum
text:
I got this 90% off a few days before the Winter sale. In the current sale they bumped it DOWN to 80% off, which is pretty gross. I wouldn't be surprised if it's given away for free on Epic this year, or in the future. 

With 90% off I couldn't pass it up and had to just see what all the drama was about and with 90% off I still felt the price was too steep. I'm just gonna wait for this to be free, and probably never play it very far through, as my current experience shows it's so meh that it's depressing. 
People will not be shocked that I am now refunding it after 0.6 hours. 
The platforming is awkward, inconsistent and repetitive. It feels like the game was designed to make you fail regularly to redo sections again, and again, an again. 

I feel [b]genuinely[/b] bad for this game, and I did [b]not[/b] want it to fail, but it failed hard. 
Management failed this game. The devs truly put some heart into achieving a LOTR atmosphere. 
This was a rushed game, with terrible mechanics. The thing is... I was excited for this until launch day, having faith.... then saw the failure of what it was and was through footage and reviews... I'm just downbad sad. I don't even think the game looks that ugly, nor do I dislike the premise of seeing Smeagol/Gollum be a main character. The gameplay just doesn't work. The Hobbit by Sierra is one of my fav games, and even that platforming was rotten clunky at times, yet somehow FUN... this game somehow felt equal to and even worse at points. It's not fluid. It's not fun. It just needed more time and thought, and more likely, less management worrying about bottom dollar. 

I really want LOTR IP to make good games, but lets be honest... so many LOTR games are "bad" due to corporate IRL drivel that they get run through. This game got the real short end of that stick.

game: The Lord of the Rings: Gollum
text:
I don't usually write reviews, but for heaven's sake, stay far away from this disaster of a game. This game is far worse than the travesty Fellowship of the Ring game from PS2/XBOX. The only thing I will say that is positive about Gollum is that I do like the art style and visuals of Thranduil, if you can stomach making it that far. Some of the character models are also neat. This game isn't even worth a sale price. I bought it because I loved the Lord of the Rings movies and played every other LotR game over the years. I wanted to get this before they pull it off Steam which should have honestly happened already with the state this game is in. This game is truly that bad. Playable and only crashed to desktop twice, but a total disaster.

Horrible platforming, awful camera angles, bad stealth segments, and ugly/muddy graphics aplenty torment this game throughout. It feels like a low budget shovel-ware PS2 game. The story is okay, and likely what kept me playing, but it is very predictable as it takes place between The Hobbit and Fellowship of the Ring. Chapter 2 and 4 sent me into infinite death loops upon dying, forcing me to reload previous chapters and re-complete them just to have another chance at not dying next time to prevent the death loop from locking in again. The platforming is disgraceful as Gollum misses platforms quite often, yet the game has him grab ledges like a magnet, despite obviously missed jumps. Other times, you just flat out miss, so it's hard to distinguish if you will make a jump or not or whether or not the magnet will kick in. Cutscenes oftentimes kick in mid-jump, including the partial ending before you do one more brief segment that leads to the actual ending, and it's jarring because you think the game froze or crashed. 

The controls are not always responsive. Sometimes you jump and he doesn't, and instead falls to his death. The camera gets stuck on every wall imaginable and oftentimes forces you to make blind jumps. Loading screens regularly appear twice in a row, meaning the loading screen goes away and comes right back before making you wait longer before the game actually loads in. Every single time you load the game, you immediately hear the sound of Gollum crunching against the ground from a high fall before you see a cutscene or start playing. Gollum gets stuck on the floor and runs in place on numerous occasions forcing you to jump to unstick him. Puzzles glitch out, like the door on chapter 10. I had to reload checkpoint 5 times and do the exact same solution before it finally triggered opening so I could enter and move on.

Overall, this game is painfully torturous to play. I'm astounded I actually played this disgraceful game to completion. It doesn't feel like Lord of the Rings in the slightest other than through the dialog and constant name dropping of main characters that aren't even in the game. This easily could have been any other random fantasy game and it wouldn't have fared better. It is very rough, buggy, unpolished, and glitchy, almost like a Beta. Nothing will ever be patched as this game is obviously abandoned. Your 15 hour or so playtime is better spent on anything else. Steam didn't count my actual playtime as it really took me 16-20 hours to get through this mess.

game: The Lord of the Rings: Gollum
text:
It was October 2015 when it happened, the worst thing ever to happen to me actually. I was playing and immensely enjoying Bloodborne on PS4 and was having the biggest blast of fun in my life with a video game. I was so full of unfiltered joy for being able to play such a great game that I literally had to take a break of all that fun just to let all those feelings of happiness and pure joy settle inside of me. I was outside at my porch, peacefully smoking a cigarette and enjoying the sunset while also planning my next route inside of Yharnam. And it happened right then - a seagull flew right at me with its beak wide open. Before I had any chance to duck or dive I was already done - it plucked both my eyes with a single stroke of its pointy beak. From then on I remember not many details nor can I reckon much else from that day on. Darkness surrounded me, blood started pouring out of my empty eye sockets, despair settled in my heart as the realization struck my mind just how a smith's hammer strikes the nail - I was not gonna be able to play my beloved Bloodborne ever again, nor was I ever gonna be able to play any other video game again. I've already seen my mom, my dad, my whole family for the last time in my life, from here on the memories of their beautiful smiles would be all I've got connecting me with them. Bitter tears started filling the two grotesque empty holes left on my disfigured face where my eyes once were, now turned a seagull's lunch... 

From then one it's been 8 years. 8 long and hard years of struggle as I slowly started adjusting to my new way of life. It's been though, not gonna lie, as I've only been able to listen to the audio of all the new bangers like Red Dead 2 and Elden Ring. My imagination frantically travels across lands and maps, dreaming of what it's like to enjoy them gloriously but the thirst to *see* those could never be quenched even vicariously as I've got no close relatives or friends who take any interest in video games, nor do I have eyes anymore. Fast forward to 2023 and the hottest blockbuster game "Gollum" is about to be released! My body was shaking, my mind was numb, my knees were weak at the anticipation of the game's release. I was set to listen to it all, ready to crank up the audio of a 20-hour long walkthrough of "Gollum" on my headset and let my imagination run wild, fill the gaps and do the rest for me! I was so excited... It was at this moment, mere two hours after the game's release, when my mom entered my room. I felt her calm and comforting presence fill the whole room. She came close to me and with a narrow soulless tone said she's just watched the reviews for "Gollum". My heart started pounding - something seemed off. She crouched next to my chair and whispered in my ear. Her words? 

[i] "For the first time in 8 years I'm glad you lost your eyes to that seagull, son. It's a blessing you aren't able to see how Gollum for PC looks, even on an RTX 4090 @ Epic settings it's worse than the most vicious nightmares you could imagine." [/i]

My brain froze as I was unable to process what I was just told. Before I could do anything I just heard a weird *pop* sound right next to me followed by two hollow thumps on the floor as my mom plucked her own eyes bare-handed overwhelmed by grief having seen this game in action. Unable to keep living normally with her sight still intact now that she's already seen gameplay of "Gollum" she took the easy path to preserving her sanity. I can only imagine what a horror show of a game this evil piece of software is but for the first time in years I'm glad I have no eyes.

game: The Lord of the Rings: Gollum
text:
[b]Is this the game for me?[/b]

Before I get into the meat and POH-TAY-TOES, I would first like to help you determine if this is a game for you. If you're a LotR fan, and you are seeking to further indulge in one of the greatest fantasy settings, then you'll probably get at least a fair amount of enjoyment out of this game. I loved skulking around the various areas [spoiler] such as the Black Tower and Lothlórien.[/spoiler] Personally, I'm not normally into stealth-focused adventures, because you can always fall back on combat, until the game decides that at specific points this is "against the rules", and fails you immediately. There are no such issues with this game, it is VERY rare that combat will ensue, and even rarer for it to be worth it. The game has a very similar vibe to the old Tomb Raider games, again, minus the combat. The movement can be a bit odd to get used to, but other than that, it's a very low barrier to entry, and the few puzzle-like sections are very welcome when they arise.

In short, if you like Lord of the Rings, and the settings therein, and don't mind not being able to hack and slash or blast your way to victory for a lack of patience, then you are likely to enjoy this game, if not simply for the settings and story alone.

[b] The Story. [/b]

Speaking of which, the story is quite short, I went out of my way to make the game last longer than it otherwise could have, and completed my first playthrough just before posting this review. The story itself tells the story of what happened to Sméagol/Gollum just after his capture by servants of the Dark Lord Sauron. [spoiler] You help him survive the slave pits, remembered during his interrogation by Gandalf at Lothlórien, shortly after Aragorn captures him at the Dead Marshes. Followed by a truly intriguing exploration and adventure through Lothlórien and uncover some mysteries therein.[/spoiler] It's not the most world changing story [as it really can't do much with the time it is set], but it is a fun one that keeps you wondering what is next, and how Sméagol/Gollum will get from here to where we eventually meet him in the Two Towers. 

The characters, both new and familiar, are interesting and have a certain depth to them, and there are few "fan-service" moments, making the ones that do exist quite well-earned and unexpected. I truly did enjoy learning about these various people you are introduced to, and their motivations. The stealth focus lends itself really well to Sméagol/Gollum's character, and doesn't feel forced - it seems like the most prudent way to progress. 

A personal highlight for me, was breeding a bird, and how your choices [apart from one] truly do impact the story.

[b]Completionists.[/b]

If you are a kindred spirit indeed, then you also like to rack up completions wherever the wind takes you. Let me warn you now, this game is a fairly simple and easy completion, until the last achievement. Complete the game without dying. I have not yet faced this challenge, though I fear it is going to take some cheesy strategies regarding Cloud Saves vs Local. As it is a platforming game, there are times where death simply comes too easy, and too fast to react to. Aside from that, it will take a minimum of 2 playthroughs, one making Gollum Choices, one making Sméagol choices. If, like myself, you refuse to use a guide, there will be some moments of frustration, and not all the collectibles are incredibly obvious in their placement. But it is definitely possible to get all of them in one foul swoop, and then rush through your proceeding playthrough(s).

Due to the last achievement, I would hesitate to recommend this to any but only the most masochistic of completionists. But I do think it would be a badge of honour, and a true testament to your patience and willingness to roll with the punches.

[b]The Negative Reviews.[/b]

If like me, you saw the negative comments and despaired, fear not. Yes, the game has some issues, but no, they are not too different to what you may find in other similar titles. As always, 3D Platforming is a bit dodgy, and can lead to some unexpected [and unfortunately not always amusing] problems. Such as, trying to leap to something, and you miss. Simple things like that, really. Speaking to the issues with performance when running the game, I had only a few moments where it seemed to stutter or suffer frame drops. In the spirit of transparency, I have an i5-10600k, 16GB RAM and a 3070ti, and had it on "high" settings at 1080p running at 120 frames for 99% of the time. Though, I have a [rather unfounded] hunch that the main difference between me and the comments who are peeved is, I ran it off an SSD, a little trick that Starfield taught me.

[b] One Conclusion, to Rule Them All. [/b]

In conclusion, I would definitely recommend this game, though I would highly recommend waiting for a sale, I had a good time playing this, but I don't think it's quite worth its RRP. If you could get it for 30% off or greater, it would be very much worth the money.

game: The Lord of the Rings: Gollum
text:
And thus it was, a new age of gaming began, and the playing of “Gollum”, eternally bound by bugs and bad level-design, was ended. 19 hours to the day since the Steam Sale sent me on this long journey, I found myself looking upon a familiar sight. I was at the final credits. How do you pick up the threads of an old life? How do you go on, when in your heart you begin to understand, there is no going back? There are some things that time cannot mend. Some hurts that go too deep…

It’s hard to know where to begin with a game like this. I had heard this game was pretty awful (and it is) but the overwhelming negativity piqued my interest enough that when it recently went on sale for a few dollars, I caved and just had to try it out. Sometimes it’s fun to watch a bad movie right? I thought this would apply here, thinking the worst sin would be how boring it was. But I was wrong. So, so wrong. Not only is the game boring, but it is terribly designed. And if I’m being completely honest: unfinished. Nothing about this game comes together cohesively in any shape or form that can remotely be described as “fun”. My biggest compliment is that most of the music is pretty decent, and some of the sound design is alright.

Everything else is terrible. This game is a stealth platformer with awful stealth and worse platforming. Gollum handles terribly, you will be fighting with him FOR YOUR LIFE to do basic jumps. Jump heights are inconsistent, fall damage is inconsistent, and a lot of his animations (ie ALL of his death animations) are not even finished. Levels are designed with highlighted paths (think yellow paint from Resident Evil, but this time in the shape of highlighted rails and glowing shrubbery) to guide you through these large nonsensical environments. But sometimes Gollum is unable to make certain jump distances. You will then encounter jumps later on that look farther so you will look around for a connecting piece because, surely you can’t make this jump right? No this time you CAN make the jump because the game will just teleport you for the final stretch that your model falls short of. You didn’t account for the teleporting distance that this particular jump lets you have. The maps themselves don’t even follow their own rules: Gollum has a wall running mechanic for larger gaps, but there are plenty of times where you can just jump these designed gaps with your basic jump and bypass wall running completely. Conversely, there are highly designed sections that clearly intend for you to follow a particular path, but the nonsensical pathing stumped me a few times such that I could only brute force my way through it by jumping a huge distance and taking fall damage. Maybe that is how those sections were designed, but I doubt it. However if there was a proper path I never found it for a couple sections. 

Comically on most of the larger, open platforming setpieces the camera will pan around the environment, seemingly in an attempt to show you where you are going. But 95% of these camera pans are just useless: they show you an overstuffed, jumbled mess of a platforming level that has no flow to it. What is even funnier is that in some capacity the developers must have realized this because they gave Gollum a detective mode (think Arkham Batman but much, much worse) that just HIGHLIGHTS THE PATH YOU HAVE TO TAKE. Let me tell you then: with guided pathing, camera pans, a detective mode with literal line to follow-  let me tell you how impressive it is that you will STILL get lost in these garbled levels. And don’t get me started on the resulting arbitrary deaths. There’s an achievement in this game for beating it without dying. I call BULL. That is impossible, this game just straight up KILLS you left and right. In the first level, in the tutorial, the game gave me this incredibly scripted “learn how to drop” section. That’s it: press B and drop. The game told me to do this. And Gollum fell to his death. This will happen to you hundreds of times.

And from nonsensical level design we will move on to the equally nonsensical plot. This game is the definition of “tell, don’t show”. The plot to this game is impossible to follow. It almost seems intriguing at the beginning with a few of Sauron’s upper ranking minions plotting around. But that quickly dissolves into nothingness. The entirety of the game is just telling you to go to this point. You will go to that point, and the game will say: now go to this point. And character motivations (if they even exist) get further watered down with awful dialogue. Now the dialogue is awful in a different kind of way: the individual sentences the characters have a Tolken-eque feel to them, but the conversations aren’t cohesive at all. Characters just spit sentences at each other, they don’t TALK with each other. It feels like they just came up with a bunch of alright sounding lines and just taped them together as best they could. And on top of all this, it is just so boring. Seriously, you start this game playing multiple chapters of just being a slave in Mordor. Sounds bad right? Partway through the game the story will shift and you will be out of Mordor and dealing with Elves and it just gets so much worse. By the end of the game I was almost begging to be recaptured and brought back to Mordor cause at least Mordor’s characters/environmental designs were kind of interesting. 

Entire “gameplay” mechanics are just forgotten about. In the beginning you will hatch a bird that can retrieve things, get a tutorial on using it, then use this mechanic maybe twice, and then never again. You will also get a tutorial on “guiding companions”, use this mechanic  once, and then never again. This is even funnier because later on you will meet a [spoiler] blind character [/spoiler] and you will think to yourself “oh surely this is where the mechanic will pay off” right? Wrong, you never use it with her. And honestly that is for the best because, like the rest of the game, it’s just a half-baked mechanic anyway.

I haven’t even mentioned the UI, but to your lack of surprise: it is bad. The visuals: extremely lacking. Gollum design: no comment. Performance: comically bad for the games extremely limited visuals and mechanics.

I wrote all this out because I actually do think there is a version of this game that works. Gollum is a unique character in terms of his movement, which I think could lend itself to a cool platformer. A highly cinematic, borderline horror styled, Mordor to crawl through does sound compelling to me. But this game is none of that, and so much less.

Do yourself a favour and avoid this game. It’s really not even worth a hate-play. No, you have so much to enjoy, and to be, and to do. Your part in the story will go on.

game: The Lord of the Rings: Gollum
text:
The game is not as terrible as the reviews say, though I only played the first chapter. The cutscenes were not very cinematic and did not build up the story or the mood, they just seemed like filler. I also wanted more connection with Gollum, who could have been more relatable if we had seen his softer or pitiable side from the beginning, just like Bilbo and Frodo did (“it was Pity that stayed his hand”). The game felt like a platformer for kids, and even though the game design was well done, Gollum looked too cartoonish, while the rest of the world and characters looked more realistic and terrifying, so it wouldn’t be suitable for little kids. The game was not a total waste, but it was not a great or realistic Lord of the Rings game. 

The game should have either been a kid-friendly game with more colorful and cheerful graphics and less dark themes, or an open world game that allowed the player to explore and roam around Middle Earth. If the game wanted to focus on adventure and stealth, it should have added more variety and challenge to each action and scenario. 

As it is, the game felt like a mismatched platformer for kids with graphics and themes for older audiences. The game should have been a $20 game at launch, and it’s worth buying it on sale at $5. If you're paying more than $15, you'll be disappointed, but at $5 it should be a grab.

game: The Lord of the Rings: Gollum
text:
[quote][url=https://store.steampowered.com/curator/6142328/][b] With 1,300+ reviews, make sure to follow The Cpt Froggy Club Curator Page for plenty more![/b][/url][/quote] 

The game begins with you as Gollum locked away in a dark Elvish prison cell. You soon have visitors, the Eleven King and Gandalf. The Elven King tells Gandalf that someone had tortured him long before the Dúnadan found him, and it is suspected that he has been to Mordor. Beyond the Elven borders darkness spreads and Gollum cannot stay among them. 

However, Gandalf wishes to know Gollum’s story, why did he change direction after going east, tracing Bilbo Baggins footsteps some sixty years prior but then upon reaching the Lonely Mountains decided to head south. No Hobbits would have welcomely gone that way and the “precious” that Gollum so desires returned to him would not be found in those lands, the Land of Shadows. So, what calls Gollum to those lands, whispers on the winds, calling his name, Gandalf needs to know. It is from here that Gollum's story will unfold... 

You’ve spent time hidden away in an old cave in the Misty Mountains, out of sight from the ever-watching eye. The cave once had a filthy Orc living there but now it’s your hiding spot; however, the eye has many spies. It is one of those spies which makes you leave the safety of the cave and chase it down before it can report back to its master. You chase after the spies which is a black Raven, however, there are Orc which roam the mountain, so you’ll have to sneak around the place out of sight, staying in the shadows and using the environment to your benefit. 

You will have to move through plants which will hide you from the patrolling Orcs. To move around your environment you must climb, drop down, jump to new areas from ledges and walls, you can also run along certain walls or swing from bars. Shadows will also help you move around although sometimes you will have to create darkness and shadows by using stones to close lanterns. These will give you time to move in the dark and by the enemy, but the enemy will soon relight the lanterns, so you’ll have to move fast. If you get caught, then it will be game over and you will have to restart from the last checkpoint. 

At certain points in the game, you will have to make choices, you will then have top debate with yourself. Do you go with Gollum’s thinking, or do you go with Smeagol? After deciding which choice, you want to make you will then have to persuade the other personality to agree with your choice. Once agreed then the choice will be made, and the action decided upon will happen. 

After chasing the spy, you get caught and are sent to mines where you will have to do various jobs all while you gain clues which can help you escape this place. 

If you find yourself stuck Gollum does have a vision which can help show you the way. However, I did find this extremely frustrating, most of the time I needed its help it would not show me the path I needed to take.  I could see where I had been at times but never where I wanted to go. You do have a cursor which indicates where your objective is but does not show you how to get there. So, I would have to trial and error to find which way I needed to go, this would mean dying over and over again.  

I’ve never played a game where I have died so much, I don’t mind dying but the reasons for dying most of the time were for silly things. Things once time you must set some barrels alight and escape before they blow up, however, you light the barrels and then you are unsure where to go, I’d try finding which way to go without any luck, even used Gollum’s vision but still no joy.  

Then I would spot somewhere which looked like it could be an escape, so I run towards it and there pops up an action, Press Q to view. So, as I reached the edge, I pressed Q thinking it was going to save me with a video clip or something. This did not happen; it did load the view of the area like intended and this feature can be found throughout the game. So, I was totally bamboozled in where to go as you only have seconds to escape, so I went to YouTube to find the answer. I found the answer but that just left me shaking my head in disbelief, I was right in going to the viewpoint however, before reaching it and being able to Press Q a video clip would start showing Gollum making the jump into a passing cart. This didn’t happen to me and it’s silly things like this which left me frustrated a lot and dying even more. 

Sometimes while crawling along the floor trying to get underneath a table out of sight of a passing enemy, I would just stop being unable to move forward, which resulted in me getting caught and dying. Trying to drop down on a ledge wouldn’t work at times, so I’d move closer and just fall off, so many silly deaths. Then there are the deaths I suffered from because of the game stuttering or lagging for a couple seconds at a time and then screen would spin as you catch up. 

The graphical and performance issues were early in the game, and I did have everything cranked to max with RXT and DLSS set to Quality. I later turned RTX off and the issues seemed to get better as I progressed through the game. It wasn’t perfect but it was a lot better than earlier on in the game.  

I’ve enjoyed playing the game, especially the further I got into it, even though the game has its problems but is it as bad as some say, I don’t believe so. I would have loved to have had graphics like this back in the PS1 days like some have claimed they come from. Me personally think the graphics are good and even better with RXT on, maxed out on Epic settings and HDR turned on. 

Hopefully they can sort the performance issues out quicker rather than later as I would like to play without the problems. 

In the options there are settings for Graphics - Graphic Preset, Display Mode, Resolution, Vertical Sync, Gamma Correction, Anti-Aliasing, Shadow Quality, Texture Quality, Texture Streaming, View Distance, Visual Effects Quality, Gollum Hair Simulation, Ray Tracing Mode, Upscaler Type, Nvidia DLSS Mode, Nvidia DLAA, Sharpness, Nvidia Relex Low Latency, Frame Generation. 

Controls - Camera Sensitivity X and Y, Invert Camera Y-Axis, Crouching, Running and Vibration. Interface - Text Language (English, German, French, Italian, Polish, Brazilian Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, Turkish, Chinese and Korean. Sound - Master, Music, Sound Effects and Speech Volume Sliders. Key Bindings (Keyboard and Mouse) and Controller - Profiles and Button Layout. 

Positives  
+ Good story 
+ Nice graphics 
+ Sneaking’ 
+ Has achievements 
+ Has cards 

Negatives  
- Performance issues 
- So many unnecessary silly deaths 

Which personality will win out in the tough decisions that Gollum and Smeagol will face as they try to survive the Dark Tower of Barad-dur and Mirkwood all while you search for your “Precious" and the thief who stole it. 

6/10   

[quote]Key provided by developer/publisher for review purposes via THE CPT FROGGY CLUB!   

Any opinions expressed are entirely my own! [/quote]  

[quote]For more reviews, great game giveaways and much more.  

Come join us @ www.Zeepond.Com and [url=https://steamcommunity.com/groups/thecptfroggyclub]The Cpt Froggy Club[/url] Steam Group[/quote]
