game: The Lord of the Rings: Gollum
text:
Performance X
Customization X
Beautiful Graphics X
Storyline +
Gameplay X
Bugs XX
Support X
Re-playability X
Recommend X

Time Played: ~7 hours
My System Specs: 
   •	Windows 10
   •	Intel i7-7700K @4.2GHz
   •	32 GB Ram
   •	NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 Ti

Performance:
   •	I am still able to play most modern games fairly well on my system. Lord of the Rings Gollum is poorly optimized. I have a fair amount of shuddering and screen flickering while I played. Loading screens can take a little while.

Graphics Setting:
   •	There aren’t many options to customize graphics setting for smoother performance. I played on high.

Graphics:
   •	Overall, this game doesn’t look like a game I would expect from 2023 at this price point (~$50). Gollum’s model looks odd. The physics for hair is really odd at times. Some things are shiny for no apparent reason. The Nazgul are not scary looking. The orcs are not scary looking either; they just look odd. There is the occasional scene or texture that looks pretty good, but but again not worth the price.

Storyline:
   •	So far the story isn’t terrible. It is about Gollum’s time after Bilbo Baggins took the ring, so don’t expect anything super exciting.

Gameplay:
   •	Gameplay can feel repetitive. This is mainly a stealth game not a fighting game. The controls can be finicky, and it’s easy to get hung up on the terrain or other obstacles even when nothing is there. The atmosphere so far is interesting.

Bugs:
   •	I have a tendency to ignore mixed reviews, and I did so with this title thinking it is a Lord of the Rings title “how bad could it be?” This is the first time I somewhat regret it. Unfortunately, this game has some serious bugs. Right now, I am stuck in an infinite death loop and the only way for me to progress through the rest of chapter 2 is to restart the chapter and not die again. Others have reported similar and just as serious bugs found later in the game as well. There is also no way to skip cut scenes to speed up the process of getting back to where you left off a little bit faster.

Support
   •	This game has been patched once since it came out in 2023 which did include a bunch of bug fixes. However, nothing has been done about the remaining bugs yet over a year later.

Re-playability
   •	This is a game that would require the player to have multiple playthroughs to earn all the achievements. Due to the bugs I have encountered so far, I will not replay this game. The hassle is not worth it. 

Summary:
   •	This game needs some serious polish. It is absolutely NOT worth $50-$60. If you are going to buy it, get it on sale for $10 to maybe $25.

game: The Lord of the Rings: Gollum
text:
The Lord of the Rings: Gollum is a game built on a fascinating premise—an intimate, character-driven look at one of Tolkien’s most tragic figures—yet almost every aspect of its execution falls short of the promise. Developed by Daedalic Entertainment and published with Nacon, it attempts to carve out unexplored territory in Middle-earth by focusing on the years when Gollum wandered between the events of The Hobbit and The Lord of the Rings. This is a compelling chapter of the lore, ripe for moral nuance, psychological exploration, and atmospheric stealth. Unfortunately, the game struggles to translate this potential into a satisfying narrative or gameplay experience, offering glimpses of what could have been but never forming them into a cohesive whole.

From the outset, the game’s approach to stealth and traversal reveals both its intentions and limitations. Gollum is not a warrior—he relies on sneaking, scrambling across ledges, hiding in shadows, and luring enemies away. This is thematically appropriate, yet the controls feel loose and unreliable, undermining the tension the game tries to build. Platforming segments often hinge on imprecise jumps or awkward climbing animations, and stealth sequences lack the structured design needed to make sneaking feel deliberate and rewarding. Rather than creating tense cat-and-mouse encounters, the game frequently devolves into trial-and-error, where success hinges more on stumbling into the correct path than on thoughtful strategy. The promise of integrating Gollum’s split personality into gameplay—the interplay of Sméagol’s timidity and Gollum’s vicious cunning—is similarly underused. While dialogue choices hint at internal conflict, they rarely produce meaningful consequences or alter the flow of the story.

The environments, which should have been a major draw given the richness of Middle-earth, further dilute the experience. Locations like Barad-dûr, Mirkwood, and Cirith Ungol offer natural opportunities for rich visual storytelling, yet the game’s presentation feels surprisingly flat. Many areas are sparsely detailed, muddy in texture, and repetitive in layout, failing to evoke the sense of place that Tolkien’s world demands. Even moments meant to convey grandeur or terror are rendered with outdated visuals, stiff animations, and lighting that does little to enhance mood. The art direction gestures at oppressive darkness and ancient dread, but the technical execution simply doesn’t support it. Players are left wandering environments that feel empty rather than atmospheric, functional rather than captivating.

Technical performance also undermines the experience in significant ways. Reports of frequent bugs—characters clipping through terrain, missions failing to trigger, broken enemy AI, inconsistent checkpoints, and occasional crashes—create constant friction. The camera is often a source of frustration, swinging unpredictably during platforming or snapping into angles that obscure hazards. These issues don’t just diminish polish; they actively interfere with comprehension and control, turning what should be tense stealth sequences or careful climbs into moments of irritation. A game already struggling to maintain immersion cannot afford such disruptions, yet they occur frequently enough to define much of the play experience.

Narratively, the game contains the seeds of something far more compelling than what ultimately unfolds. There are moments where the writing briefly captures Gollum’s fragmented psyche—his desperate cunning, his fearful paranoia, the haunting echoes of what he once was. These passages remind the player how complex and tragic the character is. But such moments are scattered and isolated. The broader story lacks rhythm, depth, and emotional build-up. Instead of fully exploring Gollum’s internal torment or presenting meaningful relationships and turning points, the narrative often feels like a series of errands framed loosely around survival. The tension between Sméagol and Gollum is treated more as intermittent flavor than as the thematic backbone of the game.

What ultimately stands out most about The Lord of the Rings: Gollum is the sense of unfulfilled ambition. The concept was bold: a grounded, stealth-driven portrait of a character defined by obsession, trauma, and duality. But the limited gameplay systems, technical shortcomings, uneven pacing, and lack of visual impact create a disconnect between the idea and its execution. Instead of a gripping look into Gollum’s twisted journey, the game feels like a prototype of an experience that never reached its intended shape. The world never comes alive, the mechanics never evolve, and the story never deepens into something emotionally resonant.

For Middle-earth fans who are eager to explore every corner of Tolkien’s universe, the game may hold some curiosity value—mainly as a rare attempt to focus on one of the series’ most troubled characters. But for most players, the experience will likely disappoint. As a stealth adventure, it lacks tightness and clarity; as a character study, it lacks insight; as a technical product, it lacks stability and refinement. The Lord of the Rings: Gollum will be remembered less for the story it tried to tell and more for the disconnect between its promise and its reality—a sobering example of how even rich source material cannot overcome execution that falters at nearly every step.

Rating: 4/10

game: The Lord of the Rings: Gollum
text:
Dear Developers 

Hello. I actually loved a lot of what you did, I loved so much of this game and I am sorry to all the people that made the parts of this game good... I will change my review IF you fix this game... This game had so much promise..... the part where the Wraiths are closing in on Gollum was terrifying... I love the Styx games, so I loved the idea of a Styx-like set in the Lord of the Rings universe... 

I got to the point where I was almost at the map room and died... from that point onwards Gollum would just auto-die at the start of the checkpoint, over and over again... So then I replayed from the Day 1 checkpoint, losing all that progress... Only to discover, when ever I got to the same point... Gollum would just auto-die at the start of every checkpoint on loop...

The only way to play again, was to re-start from Day 1 checkpoint AGAIN only to get to the same point and re-auto-die on loop again...... I did everything, tried to let it play itself out, tried to uninstall and re-install... Nothing worked, I can just play Day 1 and get to the same point and then continuously auto-die on loop.... Too infuriating, I did what I've never done before and requested a refund.

game: The Lord of the Rings: Gollum
text:
Warning: This game is not followed anymore by the devs so DO NOT expect any patch to solve the multiple problems and the broken parts.

Warning: This game is really BROKEN, this is not a joke: your saves game are corrupted and get you into a death loop forcing you to restart the level over and over.

I’ve played 6 hours and it is unfortunately enough to confirm all the bad things that have been said about this game. And I should say that I bought this game on sale. First thing first, this is not a finished game, at best a beta version. Too many things are broken and could have been fixed if the developers had the time and money to finish it. In summary:

1/ the worst thing: saving system is broken and your saves are going to be corrupted forcing you to restart the level and some time the level before.

2/ The controls are very approximate and put you in trouble.

3/ You need to decrease your graphics setting to get roughly 30 fps. For the record I play Starfield without issues. You will need a RTX 3080Ti to play the game.

4/ Even with a RTX 3080Ti, this is going to look ugly! Why, the game art is ugly… Sad but true

5/ This game is very easy. Good for me but hard core gamers are going to finish the game very quickly without challenge.

All this is very heartbroken because the game itself is not so bad. Just greedy people trying to make money with a good IP. Nothing new under the sun, but still miserable.

game: The Lord of the Rings: Gollum
text:
Solid good time. I enjoy LOTR but I'm not a die hard fan by any means. would I buy it while it was on full price? no absolutely not. but on sale it was worth the price for sure. the game lasted me about a week. some parts, especially at the beginning, dragged on for too long. but it was really nice to play a game that I could play for 15 minutes at a time here and there and actually make progress. I'm too old and have a family to have all night gaming sessions now so being able to find time to play and it be meaningful time spent was refreshing. 
I never had the game crash and ran just fine, never running into moments where the game didn't work. 
There were times the puzzles stumped me and i stopped for the night and had to come back the next day. but they weren't too easy to where it felt like i was going through the motions and they weren't too tough to where i had to look up how to solve it. honestly looking back besides the slower parts that bogged it down, i can really see where the design of the game was going and what their intention was, and you can just feel the higher up corporate strain in the game while you play it, it honestly has so much potential but i think the suits got in the way of taking it from a 8/10 at launch to a 2/10. 

the suits expected the next Skyrim, but if you except it for what the workers who made the game made it to be. its a great time.

game: The Lord of the Rings: Gollum
text:
Gollum. Where does one even begin to assess this… curious game. I mean, I wasn’t against the idea that some claim they were, although that appears to be bandwagon jumping as much as anything else. The idea of a stealth game in Middle Earth isn’t a bad one, and that kind of is the point of Gollum as a character.

The problem with this game is that while the plot is actually decent and some of the visuals fit the mood, the gameplay is so horrible and half-baked that the developers, rather than apologising for the technical issues, have apologised because it just isn’t a good game.

The biggest sadness with this game is thinking of those who made it, how surely about a third into production they knew they’d crafted a turd, and just had to keep going with it – yes, it looks like a PS2 game, albeit the ray-traced reflections aren’t bad at all and the environmental visuals are half-decent in places. But yes, the NPCs and our own Gollum are very poor on the eyes, and look maybe 20 years out of date.

It's a real shame with this game, as it could have been a lot more than this. It has ideas which don’t come to pass, it has some good characters actually (Mell and Candle man are solid) and the length is decent.

But it’s just so uninspired in terms of the core design of the game.

It does feel like a very lengthy indie game from 10 years ago, and that’s rather useless for a AAA game in 2023.

That yes, is priced as one. I got it for £20 off ceedeekees and for that money it was adequate value, but £42 in the UK, which itself is a modestly-priced AAA price, is still way too much for the quality gamers are getting here.

It’s a game which sounded good in someone’s head but when they went to make it, clearly direction went wrong, team work went wrong and someone’s then had to come in, clean up the scraps and get a broken product out in time. And working. Vaguely.

Is it the worst game ever? Actually no. I did like bits of it. Ish.

But goodness, it was underwhelming and just a bit of a waste of everyone’s time really.

game: The Lord of the Rings: Gollum
text:
I got the game to see if it's actually as bad as people say it is, and ooh boy where they kinda right.

yes... kinda...

Whilst the background visuals were good, and the music is definitely good enough for the franchise, most other parts of the game fell through immediatly.

Gameplay was decent, until you had to platform or perform stealth actions. As a stealth-3D platformer, you might see my issue.
Graphics tended to pop in, and details sometimes got added minutes after loading a scene.
Controls were clunky at best, though I have to commit, I did play the game using mouse and keyboard, sometimes entire control schemes didn't work. During two instances, I had to reload a level to get the "jump" function and "holding on to a ledge" function to work as intended again.

I would recommend this game to people who want to gift their friends a game to get absolutely mad about for the reasons stated above, and only if the game is on sale for 50% or more.

A Way Out is a good cinematic co-op experience that forces players to cooperate in inventive ways, making it a must-play adventure. While it excels in storytelling, the final act delivers a dramatic, controversial twist that might throw off players expecting a straightforward prison escape. It pivots hard from teamwork to a jarring adversarial climax that can feel out of place. However, this shocking turn also makes it incredibly memorable and emotional. Despite some clunky gunplay mechanics and a potentially polarizing ending, its unique, mandatory two-player design and high-stakes drama ensure a fun, solid journey. Overall experience 8/10 .

After finishing It Takes Two, my wife and I decided to explore Hazelight’s earlier title before moving on to Split Fiction. A Way Out is a similar co-op experience, but it takes a very different approach. Where It Takes Two thrives on constant interactivity and playful mechanics, A Way Out leans into cinematic storytelling. It’s less about gameplay variety and more about immersing you in the journey of its two protagonists. Despite the reduced interactivity, the narrative and characters are engaging enough that you find yourself rooting for them throughout their escape.

From a gameplay perspective, A Way Out feels closer to titles like Uncharted. It blends puzzle solving, stealth sequences and action packed scenarios, and occasionally introduces immersive sim elements where multiple solutions exist for a given situation.

A Way Out is a game I recommend to anyone looking to share a memorable experience with a friend or significant other. It’s a cooperative adventure that balances cinematic storytelling with interactive gameplay in a way few titles attempt.

[spoiler]Even though it's a good game and one I highly recommend, our enjoyment was ultimately overshadowed by what we felt was one of the most jarring betrayals in gaming history. The late-game twist left us feeling robbed of a more satisfying conclusion, and that single narrative choice colored our overall impression. While the journey was compelling, we personally didn’t like how it ended.[/spoiler]

I went in expecting a simple prison-break co-op game, and somehow ended up in a buddy-crime movie where teamwork mostly means yelling “press the button” while both of us fail at basic coordination. The story is the main reason to play it. It keeps moving, changes situations often, and makes the two-player setup feel like the actual point instead of a cheap multiplayer sticker slapped on the box.

Gameplay-wise, don’t expect deep mechanics or some genius puzzle masterpiece. Most of it is light stealth, quick-time events, small choices, driving, shooting, and co-op interactions that are easy to understand. But honestly, that works in its favor. It is best played with someone you can laugh with, argue with, and then blame when everything goes wrong, because apparently emotional bonding now comes with prison escapes and bad decisions.

Wasteland 3 is an overall enjoyable experience, with its turn based tactical combat standing out as incredibly fun, However, it’s not without its flaws.

The game suffers from persistent technical issues during co-op, with frequent crashes and freezes. Be sure to save often if you plan to play multiplayer. I haven’t tried solo mode, so I can’t comment on that side of things.

The narrative is entertaining and straightforward, but it often underdelivers on its stakes. The game builds up characters only to offer minimal rewards. This tendency can diminish the impact of its storytelling. Additionally, the humor is hit or miss; some jokes work well while others drag on too long.

On the DLC front, Steeltown stands out as a well integrated and essential part of the story, while The Holy Glow should be skipped due to its unengaging plot and reliance on gimmicks, which break the momentum of the main game. It’s just bad, really save your money.

In summary, Wasteland 3 is a good game that delivers a fun, co-op combat experience, despite its technical issues and lack of fixes. Save often and enjoy the ride!

Definite improvement over Wasteland 2 ...

[+] Gameplay: Fun combat similar to XCOM with a clean UI and substantial depth.
[+] Theme: Balanced a mostly believable post-apocalyptic setting with wacky and *interesting* factions, locations, and characters.
[+] Visuals: The levels, characters, equipment, effects, and animations all look great for the genre.
[+] Writing: Not mind-blowing, but the story was compelling and the dialog occasionally funny.
[+] Soundtrack: The "boss" fights feature creative tracks that are sure to be memorable, and the voice acting was surprisingly solid.

[-] Performance: Did not feel particularly well-optimized. Maximum settings pushed the RTX 2080 pretty hard when the refresh rate was unleashed to 165 Hz, but limiting the refresh rate to 60 Hz was a sufficient solution. Aside from that, there were occasional locations (Ranger HQ, Steeltown, etc.) where the frame rate would tank seemingly at random.

There are certainly some bugs to work out, but all in all, this game is all around an improved version of Wasteland 2. You want another comparison? Imagine if Fallout 2 got a graphical update and had a baby with a more recent XCOM, but that baby ended going to a religious school and would bring things up at really weird times.

Too specific and/or you don't recognize Fallout 2 because that was ages ago? Well, that's how it's gonna be.

Multiple endings and there is, in fact, a peaceful option which is what I aimed for. Not all the guides you read out there are accurate/up-to-date so be careful.

It's apparent before you even get into actual gameplay; reading the tooltips and descriptions for weapons, perks and stats;

Assault Rifle: Assault rifles are the wasteland all-rounders. Mobile, quick to fire, with decent range, and decent damage. They also penetrate all but the heaviest of armor. Get one for everyone on your Christmas list.
Submachine Gun: If you've got a clear shot (like you're shooting some punk in the back) an SMG is devastating. They're not so great against enemies in cover or heavy armor, so you've gotta be mobile and pick your targets. But when you do - damn!
Explosives: Allows you to deal more damage with explosives, defuse bombs, and avoid losing your digits when they go off.

Improvements i wished for:
- Soundtrack is great, yet there is no way to play tracks during World Exploration (tracks played should end up in the Archive, so we could enjoy them during the Kodiak exploration slog; 
also, kickstarted the game and didn't get the Deluxe/OST extras come on)

- The Kodiak should be at least 50% faster during World Exploration (base speed, no upgrades); 

- Add Fast track to explored areas, or to exit some area (b.heights to HQ and/or world map,  or to/from anywhere in the worldmap).. can/should still be interrupted by random/scripted events...

- Improve inventory management (pooled party inv is great, but there should be some "mark for sale/as junk", sort by lvl/dmg/armor/etc, filter proficient with checkbox (to hide items a party member cant use), even a item name search..)

- There should have been armor/weapon mod merchants/npcs scattered around the world... i mean everybody ends up setting up 1 ranger as a base upgrader... might as well merge these into 1 skill or a couple of perks.

- Attribute/Skill balance is OK (strike charge tied to charisma feels weird and xp gains bonus tied to attributes should just be removed as it messes with balance)

- Initiative / Alpha Strike needs a rework... found myself starting combat with the stealth sniper shooting, cleaning floor aka "alpha striking" 80-100% of most fights 1st turn (otherwise the reverse would happen, as half the rangers skipping their 1st turn after the whole enemy team got to act first already feels dumb.)

Anyways, will replay later,

The story's a blast, and definitely a whole lot more serious than what you see in Fallout 1 & 2; Yet with a fair bit of silliness to compensate. There's a few themes that are present throughout, and they're mostly really good.

A lot of people complain about how you can't avoid a lot of things using the dialogue trees, and it usually comes in a few couples. In order to avoid most of the fights I was able to avoid, I needed to have a dialogue tree *extremely* leveled up **or** an item / discovery made beforehand that would enable the dialogue.

Sure, there's some pieces where there's endings that you have to choose between one or the other, but that's the nature of it. There's multiple factions in this game and, truth be told, the main thing here is that you will have to choose between them. You won't be able to make everyone happy. This is the main tone of the game. At some point, you'll have to choose who's head you're going to put a bullet in.

It's not got XCOM tier combat, but it isn't XCOM. The UI is a bit confusing at first, but in general, it's easy to use. There's some hitches here and there, but the game in general is extremely solid; If you're looking for a post-apocalyptic CRPG with nice graphics, fun explosions, a mature setting and don't care about suffering & hard choices being the omnipresent theme, look no further.

Great RPG with turn based combat. One playthrough lasted me about 40 hours but this is with me trying to do almost every single quest and side quest. There were some side quests I missed but only a handful. I'm guessing rushing through the game will last roughtly 20 - 25 hours. 

Everything outside of combat is in real time, which is interesting because you can get into good positions before you initiate a fight. Lots of mechanics within fights, though you never really need to get too deep into any of it. Just aim for bigger damage numbers whenever possible. There are weaknesses against certain enemies and all that, but the bonus for these weaknesses are too minor to go through the trouble of changing loadouts for them

A lot of the decisions you can make do have lasting consequences going forward, though it's easy to see what those are because certain dialogue options will tell you exactly what kind of skill is needed to perform that option. 

There were only a handful of fights that I had to redo, mostly because the enemy got a massive first turn advantage over me and basically wipe me out immediately. It was pretty easy to counter these disadvantages though like sacrificing one unit to save the rest of the squad. I did eventually double the size of my squad through npc companions. Though they aren't very powerful, they are certainly useful to attract aggro and to finish off one or two enemies. I've only had one NPC companion die because I couldn't figure out how to heal it outside of battle. I played with controller and some of the buttons aren't exactly intuitive, so I recommend playing with mouse and keyboard instead.

The humor within the game is a great touch and certainly cuts through the bleakness of the reality within the game's world. I absolutely love the toaster related stuff.

Wasteland 3 **wants** to be the successor to Fallout 2, a good third-person CRPG set in a post-apocalyptic world.  It fails at that because of the uninteresting plot, poor role-playing options and lack of NPC interactions.  This game is a good third-person tactical turn-based game set in a post-apocalyptic world, in the vein of Fallout Tactics: Brotherhood of Steel. Now that is not a bad thing since Fallout Tactics is a good game for what it is.  Wasteland 3 doesn't live up to its own hype.

The roleplaying aspects of this game are very poor. The first problem encountered is, who am I?  You start out the game with a pair of characters, am I one of them, both? No idea.  Later in the game, you get to make choices for a whole group of characters, but not clear on why I even have that authority (still don't know which character has the authority).  Hell, even in Fallout Tactics characters got ranks so did have that problem there.  NPCs can join your group but the interaction between you and them are very shallow, NOT like in Fallout 2.  The character attributes do not help with the role-playing actions of the game, just with combat.  A character with high Charisma only gets more experience, but doesn't help with NPC interactions (well, maybe indirectly by using experience points to gain attributes).  A character with high Perception doesn't notice thing that help with the role playing.  All attributes are for combat, which doesn't make for a good role-playing game.

The plot fairly simple but really paints itself in a corner in endgame, so it uses an NPC to force an outcome without any good motivation.  Pretty poor writing.  Thus, there are effectively two obvious endings to the game (there are more, but pretty hard set into motion). The game has uninteresting antagonists with poor motivations. I didn't care for any of the characters in this game.  The world to explore is fairly limited with uninteresting places to visit.  Just wanted to do my duty and get the hell out of Colorado.

Buy Wasteland 3 if you want to play a decent turn-based tactical game.
loved it

This is yet another game that makes me wish we had a better review system than up or down. This game is like a 6/10 for me. This is one of the few rpgs that I didn't finish. 

The story is just boring and the all sides are awful approach just made me not care about anyone and loose the desire to even bother finishing the story. The side quest are kinda fun and wacky, but don't really fit in with the darker more serious main quest. It kind of made both feel out of place. The companions have no content. They just occasionally comment on what your doing. They don't even have a quest. They are basically just premade characters that need respec anyway to make them optimal. 

There are also several QOL and design choices that aren't deal breakers, but add up to a poor experience. First there is no pause, which to me was annoying. There is no rest function, all healing is done through items and you have to invest at least 1 point into first aid to use the items. post fight healing is just tedious. There is no fast travel and areas are broken up into small maps. The main town is like 6 maps, hope you like loading screens. Respecs cost too much. For a game that doesn't really explain how to build your character or even show you what perks a skill has until you commit, respec should be a flat low rate. Itemization is exponential so early game you low damage and battles are fairly tactical, mid to late game weapons do so much more damage that fights are over in 2 turns. The combat is team turn instead of round robin so it is super easy to start a fight be attacking and killing any enemy with your sniper then killing several more enemies before they get a turn. Vice versa if the enemy gets the jump on you through dialog combat they will kill 2-3 of your squad.

For me there are to many things that add up to poor experience to recommend this game.

For a game that took years to develop succeeding the first game and with all other similar games that could have inspired the developers, this game is a shameful disappointment.

Pros
1. Better graphics than Wasteland 2
2. Toaster is still in the game.

Cons
1. 6-unit group is WAY too much to handle, there's no connection to the characters and feel like consumables.
2. Uninspiring combat system with nothing new to showoff.
3. Terrible story progression and uninteresting characters filled in.
4. Lack of variety in armors and weapons.

The game has a well-crafted story and atmosphere.
The humor and cynicism are unparalleled. The cutscenes are very well directed.

The combat and tactics are decent, though not exceptional.
There's plenty of equipment, the skill tree is okay, and there are some cool Easter eggs.

For difficulty, I'd say it's advanced, and the boss fights are quite enjoyable.

Some decisions in the game have significant consequences.

9/10 points from me. A solid mix of real-time and turn-based combat.

For full price? Nah, absolutely not. It goes on deep sale frequently, 80% off or more. If you're willing to buy a key from third-party-that-steam-won't-let-me-mention you can get it for $3 due to how often it's been bundled.

Loved the vibes. Hated how unfinished it feels. It really seems like they walked away before putting out one last big polish patch to clean it up, bugs, audio issues, quest triggers, etc. There's a lot of big unfinished stuff too, though, and it's railroady. Ends rather suddenly. Heavily frontloaded; nothing ever lives up to the standard Colorado Springs sets.

But did I mention the vibes? Love that ♥♥♥♥. Areas look great and dialogue is fully voiced, with some important dialogues even having the pseudo-cutscene dialogues you can see in the previews. The balance is in a weird spot if you play the DLC, but before that hard mode had me struggling in a few spots and very much forced me to interact with the combat/crafting systems in full. 

Cool concepts. Great setting. Fantastic music, when it doesn't bug out, or loop eighteen times in a row because it's the only track in a massive battle. 

Get it for 80-90% off, never take the Sniper Ambush perk, quicksave before every combat in case it freezes. Expect jank. Revel in it.

Sure it's got the occasional issue (rotating the view using Q & E is rather clunky, being able to shoot your own party members by mistake when looking in your inventory or trying to change weapons and some of the dialogue options are quite limited) but I found it stable and fairly easy to use and understand and develop the characters and their skills. I liked it enough not only to replay it again on a higher difficulty with a different team  making alternative choices but to buy the earlier versions of the game too.

While this won't be everyone's cup of tea, if you can pick it up on substantial discount it's definitely worth a try IMHO.

I played Tomb Raider, the 2013 reboot, a couple of years ago, and it blew me away on every level. Mature, fun, action-packed, and graphically? Over 10 years later, it still puts 90% of new releases to shame.

Then came Rise of the Tomb Raider: great game too, but mostly more of the same.

Shadow, however, is a downgrade in almost every respect, including visually. You heard YouTubers praising its graphics? Me too. They lied.

The story? I’m not even sure what it’s about. It’s mostly Lara cosplaying in the jungle and talking to invisible NPCs during buggy side quests. Frankly, I don’t care. And I tried. Really. I put 32 hours into this thing.

Didn’t finish it. Never will.

Of the three, this is the one you skip.

Shadow of the Tomb Raider is good. Not great, not bad, just good.

The graphics and environment are genuinely incredible. I'd even argue Shadow is probably the best tomb raider game when it comes to visuals. Everything feels "alive" and realistic, plus some of the views are genuinely breath taking. There's multiple tombs (that are not just press this and jump), a lot of collectibles, side missions and challenges to complete, so the map doesn't even feel empty. The tombs also gave new skills and outfits which worked as motivation to fully explore everything.

The combat is interesting but heavily favours stealth, which I didn't mind necessarily, but it made some sections annoyingly frustrating as the enemies only needed maybe two strikes to almost kill you. The stealth sections were very fun though and seeing Lara coat herself in mud to better camouflage herself in the environment was a nice touch that made her feel more powerful.

Cosmetics were disappointing. The recent tomb raider games have always had a lot of outfits and while Shadow had multiple, being able to use them freely was not possible. You are mostly restricted to the tribal outfits when in Paititi, and a lot of them do not look... the best. A new gameplay function was being able to craft a top-part and lower-leg-part and mix-n-match them, which was okayish, but I'd have liked having more customization options for them since they were just added on-top of the core outfit.

The story is decent but unfortunately really slows down during the paititi section (and some sections feel very unbelievable), but it's not bad. No spoilers, but it follows a similar premise to Rise which is a shame, since it causes the game feel more repetitive. At least the side missions were interesting and unique. One of them gave a LOT of insight into Lara's character and how she feels about herself on the inside, and I would've liked to see more of that side in the main story as well.

tldr: Visuals are stunning, combat is good but favours stealth, cosmetics are somewhat lacking and story could've been better. I'd still recommend it especially if its on sale as I enjoyed it a lot regardless of my complaints

Shadow of the Tomb Raider is a disappointment after its superior predecessor, Rise of the Tomb Raider. Following on the heels of that game, this story does all the things that you'd expect from a Tomb Raider game: plenty of crypts and tombs with puzzles, running and gunning, and a little bit of story.

* STORY: Unfortunately, the story is uneven and predictable. The character dialog in particular is clunky and weak. The fact that the lead writer from RotTR left the company after that project is evident.
Props for trying to make some interesting storytelling with actual Mesoamerican culture and including surviving people who embrace that heritage. If only they were interesting to talk to.

* GAMEPLAY: Controls are extremely clunky and unpolished compared to the prior entry. Lara's movements are jerky and erratic, and the game frequently disregards inputs. Lara can run and leap straight on to a platform, only to be invisibly pushed sideways and to her death; some climbable surfaces suddenly can't be climbed when you need to, for no reason; jumping over obstacles sometimes results in Lara suddenly teleporting back into contact with the ground while at the height of a jump. All of these result in a cavalcade of frustrating deaths that don't make the tombs any more interesting to navigate, they just force you to keep starting over... and over... and over, because the controls are terrible.

* NEW SYSTEMS: The game introduces rappelling, so that Lara can rope down from above, and makes use of overhang climbs, where Lara climbs upside-down. Unfortunately, the camera work on the overhang climbs is inconsistent, so that sometimes it's impossible to tell where you're supposed to go while hanging upside-down like Batman. Rappelling would be interesting but its primary purpose is to swing places, but the controls for rappelling are also bad, like the others: You'll find yourself randomly switching between your movement controls climbing up and down the rope or swinging back and forth, resulting in Lara suddenly shortening her rope by climbing up when you're trying to swing and jump.
SotTR also makes use of many side missions, which you pick up in various hubs. Side missions, despite their innocuous nomenclature, can be quite lengthy. They do feel like they are not "side" missions but necessary upgrade missions, as they tend to lock pieces of gear or skill improvements.
Gunplay uses the usual pistols, rifles, shotguns, and bows, with various upgrades to unlock and outfits that provide various small boosts. Stealth gameplay now includes covering Lara in mud like Schwarzennegger in Predator and then hiding against muddy walls. Someone on the team really liked making Lara dirty, because she spends significant amounts of the game covered in mud.

* NEW GAME: This entry features a new game+ version, in which you keep your unlocks and then replay the game. Unlike RotTR, in which you finish the story and then can traverse the map and unlock all of the secrets, the gameplay in this is pointedly designed for you to NG+ it, because you can't access many areas due to missing pieces of gear at the time that you first find them, which means you have to progress further and then go back for them, or else speedrun the end, get all of the gear, and then NG+ for your "actual" playthrough. It's a frustrating attempt to make you play the game again to generate more hours of "content" without actually adding to the game.

* LAYOUT AND LEVEL DESIGN: The one area in which the game shines is, unsurprisingly, in its level design. Areas have lots of tight switchbacks and winding spaces, to help hide secrets and maximize the traversal space. Verticality is fairly common, and in a fitting concordance with Mesoamerican mythology, large chunks of the game take place in underwater cave systems. The vistas are beautiful (and staged with forced cameras, of course), and the levels provide interesting playspaces and a lot of room to search for hidden secrets. If only the core gameplay of traversing the levels were enjoyable!

Overall, this is a somewhat lackluster entry, with attempts to add new systems that feel rushed and unpolished, and a game that demands precision timed movement yet has extremely clunky and unresponsive controls. It almost feels like an indie platformer with a shined-up graphics engine.

For the first 10 hours, I was really confused why this was considered the worst in the trilogy, sure some of the side missions are pointless filler, but once I got to Paititi, that's when the cracks started to show. For those who are unaware Crystal Dynamics was about halfway through the development of this game, until the publisher Square Enix got the license to make the marvel's avengers game. Riding high on the peak popularity of the marvel movies, they tasked Crystal Dynamics to make that game and assigned Eidos-Montreal to finish this one. And it clearly shows. In the end this move was a terrible decision as the avengers game lost the publisher $200 million dollars as the game was terrible.

The story starts off really good, picking up where Rise left off, the graphics in its lush environments are beautiful exploration is the best the series has to offer. The tombs are hands down the best in the series as well also has a lot of time and attention to detail in its puzzles. And while it can be tedious to go back and collect what you missed, there is so much variation in its environments It didn't bother me too much. Especially with difficult options for puzzles, exploration, and combat its nice to have some of these more challenging puzzles have some hints if you are struggling.

Its combat is a ton of fun with a lot of new things added, like fear arrows to make enemies commit friendly fire before dying. The mud camouflage to blend with the environment, climbing up trees and either do an aerial takedown or use rope arrows to hang them from the tree. More guns to get with various upgrades, with gold to purchase things like ammo pouches, increases, and attachments, though the shotgun is pretty underwhelming. However, as people have said, there isn't a lot of combat throughout the game compared to the first one, which is a shame. It's a waste to use your skill points for combat early in the game, as it’s better used for other skills. It's also really frustrating that enemies don’t respawn in an area you revisit to add some additional combat, and worst of all, unlike Rise, there is no level selector or combat mode, so if you want to fight some more, you have to replay the entire game over. All of these cool mechanics, like the mud stealth that took a lot of time and effort to create and playtest is barely used in comparison to what you will be doing in the game. The final boss was one of the most boring and underwhelming I have experienced in a long time. Yeah, the arenas look cool, but it boils down to run and gun dodging his attacks and shoot enough times to melee him rinse and repeat like 5 times, and that's it. 

Also, the game is way too easy on hard. I get that there is a harder mode above that, but it's only available on New Game Plus, and most people won’t do that, so it makes the game kinda easy, especially when you carry so many healing berries by the end. Because of this, the other things like using other berries to increase the slow down aim time or perception vision all feel pointless as i never actually used them, unless you want to 100% everything, the game is easy enough to navigate as it is.

Its story is the worst part of this game, as i said it starts off really good and I was hoping for the same level of quality as Rise did (despite that game having some pointless filler, it was still excellent), but once you get to Paititi it all falls apart. Initially, it was fine as Laura believed they had the box, but once she realized they didn’t have it that should have been the moment to move on. But no she stays and helps people with their mundane problems, as they exposition dump the tribe’s culture. It would have been fine, but the writing isn't very good as it gets boring and confusing as they mix people, places, and concepts, some you get to see in the game, and other things you don’t. In the context of how this game starts, I don’t care because I wasn’t made to care, as all we are after is the box. They throw a curveball that the villain is somehow closely connected with Paititi, and Trinity controls them. This felt like this connection was thrown together at the last minute in an attempt to make this part of the game make sense. The flashbacks of Laura as a kid were a nice touch. Also, they did my boy Jonah so dirty in this one; they get split up a lot and always seem to make it to where I take the long, dangerous path. It isn't until the last quarter of the game that he does anything to help Laura at all. And that ending, nothing makes sense, nothing is explained, nothing feels like it connects from the story prior, and you are left confused by the end as the credits roll. I did about 85% of the game and there was still a couple side missions left and some things to pick up but there isn't any incentive to 100% it 

Overall, it isn't a terrible game, but this is definitely the weakest out of the three. I’d say it's still worth playing for its exploration and tombs, as that is the best part of this game. The trilogy constantly goes on sale for all of them for $10 - $15 its absolutely worth getting the bundle for that price.

The third entry in the modern Tomb Raider trilogy was a bit of a letdown. Visually, the game looks great, the gameplay remains fluid and enjoyable, and it runs well on modern hardware. It also introduces several new gameplay mechanics that improve the experience, particularly the expanded stealth options, which add more variety and depth to encounters.

The tombs are another highlight. Some of them are genuinely creative, offering interesting puzzles and rewarding exploration that capture the spirit of the series. These moments are among the game's strongest and most memorable.

Unfortunately, the writing is nothing special. The story feels generic, and many of the conversations with NPCs are forgettable and lack personality. While the game excels in presentation, exploration, and gameplay, it struggles to deliver a compelling narrative. Overall, this third chapter feels like a step down compared to its predecessors and ends the trilogy on a weaker note.

Overall: 5-6 / 10

I will start of with Yes, Rise is the better game.  However, I did enjoy this.  

The game did suffer in Development hell and it does show in certain ways.  The story with Trinity doesn't really go anywhere (new head writer for story) and the facial animations with Lara are a step back from Rise.  

That being said, I'm a sucker for games set in a Jungle/Forest type setting.  Maybe I just love the color green... The game is gorgeous.  The focus being more on stealth felt a little better and more realistic to how This current Lara would approach an armed conflict situation.  The Rambo style of Rise was enjoyable albeit feeling a little over the top (but still fun regardless).  There are plenty of tombs to explore with decent puzzles.  That might be why I enjoyed this a tad more than Rise due to the focus being on exploration this time.  

If you're looking for more exploration and less combat in your TR game and your not super invested in the story being fleshed out from the previous two games, than this is a good choice.

[b]Introduction :[/b]
Shadow of the Tomb Raider, sorti en 2018, est un jeu d'action-aventure développé par Eidos-Montréal et édité par Square Enix. Troisième volet du reboot de la saga, on y suit Lara Croft dans la jungle péruvienne et au Mexique pour empêcher une apocalypse maya déclenchée par Trinity. C'est l'opus le plus sombre, le plus sauvage et celui qui mise le plus sur l'exploration des tombeaux.

[b]Ce que j'ai aimé :[/b]
[list]
[*]🌿 La jungle est sublime, dense, oppressante, avec un sound design des bruits de nature qui te plonge complètement dans l'immersion
[*]🏛️ Les tombes annexes sont nettement plus inspirées que dans les deux opus précédents, avec de vrais petits puzzles environnementaux qui demandent d'observer
[*]🗡️ Lara devient une vraie prédatrice de la jungle, les phases d'infiltration furtive boue + plantes sont jouissives et changent enfin du Rambo en jupette
[*]🏙️ Paititi, la cité cachée, est le meilleur hub de la trilogie reboot, vivante, colorée, avec plein de side quests et de PNJ qui ont une vraie personnalité
[*]🎭 L'évolution psychologique de Lara qui flirte avec la noirceur est bien amenée, on sent enfin le poids des conséquences de ses actes
[*]🌎 Les langues anciennes (quechua, yucatèque) parlées par les PNJ ajoutent une couche d'authenticité rare dans le genre
[/list]

[b]Ce que j'ai moins aimé :[/b]
[list]
[*]🔫 Les combats restent simplistes, même en difficulté élevée tout se résout en mode arène avec couverture, aucune profondeur
[*]🧩 Les énigmes des tombes principales sont souvent trop faciles, Lara résout tout à voix haute en 3 secondes
[*]🔄 La formule globale n'évolue pas, on est exactement sur le même squelette que les deux précédents avec un coat de peinture différent
[*]📖 Quelques twists scénaristiques téléphonés à des kilomètres, l'écriture est plus faible que dans Rise
[/list]

[b]Conclusion :[/b]
Shadow of the Tomb Raider, c'est la conclusion convenue mais soignée d'une bonne trilogie reboot. Si tu accroches à la formule, tu en auras pour ton argent : c'est beau, immersif, l'exploration est plus généreuse que jamais. Si tu attends une vraie révolution du gameplay, passe ton tour. À 996h sur le compteur (DLC + tombes challenge + collectibles compris), je peux dire que la rejouabilité est réelle pour les complétionnistes. Solide.

[b]Note :[/b] ★★★★☆

If the main game doesn't tickle your fancy, there's also a moderately-sized list of player levels made by other indie developers, such as the infamous Notch. The list is arranged by difficulty, and each level provides a different plot that changes the perspective of the main character and the atmosphere in general.

This is a classic game that's withstood the test of time. If you've never heard of this game and would like some rough oldschool challenge, now is a good time as ever. Music slaps too, and it has a level editor.

The game is a delightful challenge, very cleverly constructed at various points, and is mostly deliciously difficult, though at some points quite frustratingly so. The music is top-notch. 

A continuous complaint I have about this game, though, is how difficult the UI is to use. SPACE or ENTER are well-established as the default button to proceed or confirm. I can't count how many times I've had to dig through a whole series of menus because intuitively I want to press SPACE or ENTER when the button I should press to select an option or go forward is Z. The game also has far too many confirmation dialogs. "Are you sure you want to...?" "Yes, I'm extremely sure. ENTER and get on with it already, oh right, in this game, I have to use Z. Aaargh."

(EDIT: It seems SPACE it supposed to work in most places. I'm not sure why, but I somehow recall there being situations where SPACE didn't work when I wished it would.)

Made to resemble an old C64 game both on sound and graphics department, VVVVVV (that's 6 V's, and there's a pretty good reason for that) is a charming short game about limited controls, pixelated graphics, pulsating soundtrack, and Nintendo-hard difficulty.

You are Viridian, the captain of a spaceship whose traveling has gone wrong, and must rescue all the remaining five crew members. Your only control options are moving left and right, and inverting gravity - but only when you are on a floor or ceiling, not floating around. This simple control scheme allows for MANY puzzles, frustrating moments, and crazy stunts to be performed all around the game!

It's a bit short, and the second playtrough can be made in about an hour or two, but definitely worth the trip.

Warsim: The Realm of Aslona is a game that redefines the text-based genre with its depth, complexity, and charm. Don't be fooled by its ASCII graphics, this game offers a rich and immersive experience that rivals many graphically intensive titles.

The game's kingdom management system is deep and engaging, offering a level of complexity that will keep you on your toes. With millions of procedurally generated races, events, and areas, every play through offers a unique experience that keeps the game fresh and exciting.

One of the standout features of Warsim is its emphasis on player choice. Every decision you make has an impact, adding a layer of strategy and thoughtfulness to the game. Whether you're dealing with political intrigue, managing resources, or leading your forces into battle, your choices shape the world of Aslona.

The ASCII graphics, while simple, add a certain charm to the game. They serve to stimulate your imagination, allowing you to create a vivid world in your mind. It's a testament to the game's design that it can create such an immersive experience with such simple graphics.

In conclusion, Warsim: The Realm of Aslona is a must-play for any fan of text-based games or strategy games in general. Its depth, complexity, and emphasis on player choice make it a standout title in the genre.

I’ve come to discover that the most surprisingly noteworthy thing about Warsim has been the sheer impressive nature of emergent story created through an intuitive level of engaging & thematic game-play that leads to a tremendous amount of inherent replay-ability.

if you like text based games then you will probably love this.

Interesting concept but very repetitive, for example: kingdom size is measured in "lands" and each turn (in-game year) you may attack one opponent for one land; mopping-up defeated opponents may take a decade. After an hour you have largely met the recurring cast of characters gracing your throne room. There is limited diplomacy, no political marriage, no wives or even courtiers who aren't stand-ins for a kingdom management sub-window. If you are still enamored by procedural generation 28 years after Daggerfall gave us a gorillion villages to visit and be bored by then I don't know what to tell you.

Once, while wading through the day's hundreds of unwashed peasantry whining about my soldier killing their favorite goat, a lone Goblin arrived.

"Hello your majesty! I come from a small tribe of goblins, and my tribe dared me to approach Your Eminence to ask simply for one single gold coin. I know you're a busy monarch, so I understand if you have better things to do than indulge a lowly goblin like myself, but I thought nonetheless it would not hurt to ask. And, you have a generous reputation, besides."

He politely stood and waited for a response

I was a bit bewildered at the request, but flicked a single coin his way. Let's face it, the story alone was worth a gold piece.

Then I stood aghast as the goblin yalped in joy, jumped up and down, swallowed the coin whole, choked to death, and died.

My guards looked at me, I looked at them, we all shrugged, and they dragged away his dead body.

10/10 game highly recommend

Warsim: The Realm of Aslona is in my opinion, one of the best games I've played. I dont at all regret purchasing it and am recommending you purchase it as well so that you may experience the glamour of it.  Warsim has numerous starts, ranging from ascending to the throne through birthright, or from a game of chance. Perhaps you wanted infinite power, so you pledged yourself to the demon lord in exchange for the kingdom. Maybe your morals are mixed and you wanted to become a kingdom of slavery. The possibilites are endless. The exploration feature also adds so much that it could take hundreds of years (turns) to find and exploit everything, or just burn it all to the ground and leave it to the vultures! I love this game, and I hope you will give it a chance too. Thank you for your time.

The game is described as a "deep, rich kingdom ruling sim", but it's just a lot of very simple subsystems that barely even influence one another. I kept digging, trying to find something interesting, but aside from a modestly entertaining exploration portion, everything else was very shallow. It's just a "numbers go up" game with a peculiar interface.

I absolutely recommend this game. The design itself calls back to older titles like Rogue and Dwarf Fortress, while using modern simulation to create a unique, light-hearted, and complex sandbox text-based game. You can focus on the war and conquest or have a goblin heckle you in your own throne room. As the description says, there is depth in every direction. The developer is passionate, talented, and deserves recognition for his work.
The only perceived flaw I originally had with the game was the comedic direction it took, as many simulation-based management games in older styles take a hardcore, serious approach to their story-telling and gameplay. As I played though, his style grew on me and made the entire game a unique experience. You can simulate an orc whistling and tell him it was trash, then throw him in the dungeons. A hardcore game wouldn't have added a procedural music system for bards and musicians just to allow you to dunk on them, and Warsim wouldn't be Warsim without it.

2026 Update: He's actually still going. Huw, are you ok? Blink twice if Warsim has a gun pointed at you right now. 
If there's even a slight chance you might enjoy a game with ASCII visuals you are doing yourself a disservice by not trying Warsim. Year after year, updates just keep coming and the content just keeps piling up - I'm convinced it would take years to see all this game has to offer and by the time you do you'll be ready for another go.

2022 Update:
Everything I said before holds true, Huw is a maniac and he's still adding new content all these years later and still has plans to overhaul and improve the older outdated stuff. The game's worth far more than the asking price.

Warsim is a phenomenal and unique experience. It stretches far and wide with a frankly ridiculous and way over the top (in a good way) amount of content to explore and much of it changes between playthroughs meaning you rarely play the same game twice. I've played the game for six hours, lost and started a new game and been baffled by the amount of stuff I found that I hadn't seen before. You can spend an hour easily exploring some of the locations and there are hundreds of locations large and small for you to explore and the experience is so engaging that I'm genuinely saddened when I occasionally find somewhere that isn't finished being added yet. I was given a code for free via my youtube channel a year or two ago and I was distraught to find I hadn't left a review for it yet. The developer is lovely, and if the game was bad I would tear it to pieces in a review regardless. Since I was given a code free, I have bought it for three of my friends because I truly believe this game should be supported, it's a unique and frankly daring move by the developer to make an ASCII game today but he pulled it off with aplomb.

Warsim is an interesting game, and one that certainly hits my points of interest but may not be the thing for everyone. Given that you're reading this review, after having viewed this game's page, you probably have at least an inkling of interest and, hopefully I can bolster your intrigue. 

What you're looking at is a game that feels like a love letter from the days of ZORK - a game that, back then, would probably have been the most incredible feat of fun and engineering imaginable. You're greeted with either a quick start button or an in-depth world creation that feels similar to Dwarf Fortress, so there's a lot of customization. I will say that it is a shame we cannot edit our own race, we all always simply "Human", with no defining features or characteristics, but the rest of the world can be tampered with. 

...I actually don't have much else to say. I mean, about the game or anything. Its fun. Its a specific type of fun, but still a flavor of it. Basically what you see is what you get - there's a lot of silliness that makes me feel like I'm playing a Discworld novel, or something equally as mystical-fantastical which brings great pleasure. Conquer countries, fight demons, build an empire, drop goblins through a hatch door into an endless pit - or do the opposites of all of those.

This wasn't a very good review and I probably didn't bolster any intrigue. A foolish shame. But the game is fun!! Uhh...Yeah! I don't know what else to say.

Actually a really good game, and it is alongside the more beginner friendly side of games like Caves of Qud or Dwarf Fortress, which are still amazing in their own rights due to the systems they use.

It is essentially a kingdom management simulator, but not only that, there is many things to do but my favorite thing is to bet on fights at the arena.
TO THE DUNGEONS WITH THEM ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, DON'T LET A SINGLE ONE ESCAPE

My first game began with me winning the throne in a bet with the king, so of course I gambled as much as I could once I started actually playing. Without knowing any betting strategies I went broke very quickly, but not before eradicating a goblin nation, at least. Eventually I was in such a dire position (no money, lands getting invaded every turn) that I stepped down from the throne. Somehow, the people mourned my death, using my conquered lands to create a somewhat successful democratic nation... until they got invaded by the demon scourge and were all killed. Oh well.

After that, I began my second game where things went much better for me. I won the throne of Wargard by pulling a sword from a stone and began utilizing mercenary groups to do lesser invasions for me. Apparently, hiring a mercenary group and funneling your soldiers into them is actually a really good strategy. As long as you have mercenary barracks they'll never disband, their battlescore will be much higher (depending on the mercenary group, but generally they'll be higher than a typical soldier's battlescore) and you won't need to pay them. Also, they can do multiple battles in just a single turn, most I've seen was six. 

Using these mercenary groups I was easily able to defeat all of my adversaries, the goblin nations, bandits, and whatnot. About a third of the way into my rule a comically evil alliance was formed between lunar halflings and blood orcs. This was a big problem. I established alliances with the three other nations, the Mist Men, the Dawn Dwarves, and the Golden Elves. Together, we fought against one common enemy. It took many battles to defeat the lunar halflings, wasn't too bad though, just took some skirmishes to whittle them down before I began invading. 

The blood orcs, on the other hand, were extremely, extremely strong, and I did not realize this until I began my first skirmish with them. Which I failed miserably in. It took many years, but eventually I was able to hire foreign mercenaries to fight for me, and I ended up recruiting about 30 blood orc champions (about 3,000 battlescore each!!!) to fight for me for a hefty 120,000 gold. I took the offer and as soon as I did I was able to finally start winning my fights against the evil blood orcs, until eventually they were vanquished from the realm and there was finally peace in the lands. The last remaining leader of the blood orcs was captured by my soldiers and hurled off the side of a cliff. With battlescores like that who knows if that even killed him. Afterwards, I made my own holiday celebrating this victory, where the people of the empire of Wargard would light ceremonial bonfires, hold festivals, and exchange gifts.

But now, there were no bandits, no goblins, no dark alliance. With the lands of our foes conquered, we had become an empire, one of the three super powers of the realm. 

After my victories, I used the gold I had plundered from my conquests to focus on building up my kingdom more, making tax offices, improving the roads, building libraries and knightly orders. Very cool stuff. 
Eventually, while adventuring, I came across a portal to a demonic realm where the demon horde that ended my previous reign resided. Of course, with the powerful army I had, I entered, and destroyed all of the demons and their leader.

With only a few years left of my reign, I built various monuments and statues of myself, fully upgraded my throne room, I even paved the roads with gold (and had to hire a guard force to prevent people from stealing the gold) until eventually I died in my sleep. The outcome of my empire was good, the people had decided that I was some sort of deity after I perished and engaged in my worship, and with the bandit levels extremely low, the demons vanquished, and the dark alliance destroyed, the people of my empire were able to live peacefully for centuries to come. 

I left out many details of course because I don't want this review to be a novel long, but this game is just magnificent. It felt like I was really living through a story, and the amount of interactivity and the sheer amount of outcomes there are really makes this game something special. Though the game was sort of complicated at first, you can get the hang of it really easy. I never felt that a system was too complicated, and the simple user interface also helped with making the game more approachable (for me at least), all possible options were always laid out before you at all times.

For the price, I think this game is very worth it and I strongly recommend that you give it a try if it interests you at all. I really enjoyed the time I spent playing it, and I think you will too.

If you like Tycoons you're gonna love Tavern Master! I'm posting this, for context, as I'm nearing the end of the tech tree and did all events multiples times.

Pros:
- There's enough research and new mechanics to make things interesting
- The things that are automated are automated well
- Cute graphics, and overall game design captures well the essence of what a tavern could have felt like
- Overall super engaging semi-idle gameplay. Very chill vibes

Mehs:
- I wish some things could be done in bulk: assigning waitresses to tasks/floors for example
- Some sort of copy paste would be nice, especially for placing tables and chairs
- Some things (like the brewery) could be better explained, although I appreciate a game that lets players figure things out

Cons:
- It seems that, for what it is, the game could be optimized. It eats up a lot of CPU.
- So far I encountered only two "disasters": fire and thieves. It could be a bit more varied because they end up just stopping the action once you get a hold of how to deal with them. They're not stressful (as they should be), just game-stopping annoyances.

If I was being *really* nitpicky, there are two problems I had with the game. 

First, I’d say that the game is crying out for different sized/styled build plots, you only get 2 maps (not sure what the dlc adds) and the build plots on either are a decent sized rectangle but feel super restrictive when you’re getting to the mid game. 

And second, about halfway through the tech tree, there are toilets. 

I don’t know if this is on me for not looking through every item on the tech tree before building, or the game trying to screw with me, but 5 hours into my run, I unlock the toilets. I thought they’d be cosmetic or outhouses – nope. It turns out these are a vital part of the customer needs, so the second you unlock toilets, every customer suddenly decides to remember that they need to use the bathroom, despite it NEVER coming up before. Before you unlock it, no one needed the toilet, now suddenly EVERYONE does. 

So my tavern was carefully built up, 3 floors packed with tables, bars, kitchens, decorations, everything is placed and styled just how I like it, only for the toilets to unlock (you need them to progress too) and then I had to spend half an hour moving everything about and rebuilding because now each floor needs a room with a bunch of toilets and basins. All of which take up at least four squares if you want more than one sink – and you have 1 night to set it all up, else the customers complain. No heads up that this was going to happen, at all. 

That’s a ♥♥♥♥ move, Game.

They do give you a Garden to compensate, so you can put all the tables you tore up to make room… but nine times out of ten it’s raining, and there’s no outside cover, so no one sits out there.

[h1] Assassinated by Capitalists Because GDP Drops During Deep Global Recession [/h1]

TL;DR at bottom (I ramble on this one) 
 
 
Democracy 3 is a domestic policy simulator that tasks players with the management of a given nation. The player will have to deal with any problems faced by their country, such as reducing unemployment, poverty and crime whilst improving GDP, average educational level and average citizen health. The player does this by implementing new policies and adjusting existing ones, each affecting various features of your nation (such as GDP, Energy Use or Racial Tension). Based on these elements, urgent negative situations may arise (such as Street Gangs) which increase your expenditure and have a knock-on effect on your nation, such as increasing unemployment or crime. There is no ‘goal’ in this game, except the available achievements (which present challenges like creating a Green Utopia). All in all, Democracy 3 is a unique management game that can prove challenging. 
 
The simulation of the game is rather detailed. The main game screen forms a web within which every policy, social statistic and urgent situation is interconnected. It can take a lot to discover the origin of a problem, let alone where to make adjustments. However, this proves a satisfying challenge and the main ‘point’ of the game. The information needed to solve problems is readily available for the player, even if it’s not obvious. There may be a learning curve for new players, but if the player thinks carefully then they should be able to solve the scenario. In real life, the solution isn’t as simple as raising or lowering a particular tax, and Democracy 3 captures this excellently. 
 
The available policies are somewhat interesting. Players can choose to implement tax subsidies, enhance aspects of law enforcement and more. They all come with descriptions and provide information on who approves of them, who doesn’t and the impacts it has on budget, society and other situations. Nothing is really hidden from the player; impacts are measured in percentages, which means the game isn’t deliberately making things difficult in this area. You can engage with the policies however you wish; you can choose ideological justifications or you can simply look at the statistics and play more like an opportunist. Either way, the game provides an interesting challenge and an engaging way to interact and manage your scenario through the policies. This is only improved by the most subscribed mods that add in even more policies for you to choose from. 

 

Voter response to policies is complex, perhaps too much. The policies do describe how different groups will respond to them with a percentage. Yet, it often seems like nothing can be done. For example, as my title suggests, I was repeatedly assassinated because Capitalists hated the fact my GDP dropped. Yet, GDP improvement is obscure and difficult; policies only improve GDP by a small amount. Even still, GDP is often more affected by the state of the global economy, which you obviously have no control over, often to the point that your actions are either too small or would take too long to have an effect. I was only playing on the default difficulty, so to be assassinated on 4 out of 5 playthroughs was frustrating. Sure, this is just the challenge of the game, but sometimes difficulty could just be ‘cheesy’, sorta like Dark Souls. Some may not mind the challenge, but beware; you may find frustration on your learning curve.  

 

There are perhaps systematic issues with the game though. The game prescribes certain responses for each group to different policies, but these may not reflect what you know to be true or perceive to be true. That’s not necessarily a fault of the game, but rather it’s reflective of the complexity of political ideology. For example, different ideologies (like Liberalism or Socialism) would perhaps see different groups responding in different ways to a given policy. The game itself also admits that people can belong to more than one group (I.e. an individual can be socialist, a parent and a trade unionist). The issue, which is perhaps an inescapable one, is that the game’s statistical simulation may not be able to fully represent the complex discourses of politics. That being said, it doesn’t do a bad job, but I will perhaps suggest you think more about the game itself than real-world politics. 

 

The game also only allows the player to interact with domestic policy. I kinda wish there was more depth overall to the game in the sense that managing a nation is more complex than developing domestic policy. Then again, as I’ve just said, reality is often far too complex for a video game to literally recreate. So, despite this, I will still be able to recommend the game. 
 
All in all, Democracy 3 is a detailed and complex political simulation game which can often prove complex and frustrating. However, there isn’t a game out there like it and I can honestly say I had a *lot* of fun figuring out all the issues. If you like a good puzzle or just untangling a problem, you will probably enjoy Democracy 3. 

 

6.5/10 
 
Pros: 
+ Detailed  
+ Engaging 
+ Addictive 
+ Provides a complex puzzle for the player to untangle 
+ Lets the player do what they want 

 

Cons: 
- Can be too complex 
- Player can be punished for things happening beyond their control 
- May not be able to fully capture what it feels like to run a country 
- Could be frustrating in certain circumstances

Two main problems with this as a game

1) In no universe is this ever worth the full $25 asking price. If it is 70% off as part of a sale THEN it becomes viable, but that leads to the second problem: 
2) It's still far too easy to run a country to whatever you want it to be. 

Here's how the game works. At the start you are presented a number of problems to eventually solve, and each turn you get points to make policy adjustments in order to solve those problems. Small policy changes like "Legal Aid for the Poor" will only cost you about 3-4 points, Creating a "CO2 Tax" will cost you lose to 39 points, and you get about 10-20 points a turn depending on how your cabinet is staffed, which is randomly selected for you at the start of the game but you can clear out and restaff at will. 

That's all there is to it. You adjust a bunch of sliders on policies to try to solve problems presented at the start. Some other problems may pop up later in the run, but if you make it past your first term you usually can build up enough points to handle whatever else gets thrown at you later in the game. Picking different countries presents you with different problems to solve, but overall there's not much to it. The workshop does okay with creating new nations and introducing new policy ideas to play with in addition to the base game, but overall this game as a political simulation is very surface level and not very deep. I only sunk 130+ hours into the game to get all the steam achievements as part of a completionist phase I am going through, found out that two of the achievements can only be earned if you download mods of the workshop, and then I was quick to move on from this game after that. 

There is a "Democracy 4" game which is VERY SIMILAR to this one, I have no desire to check that one out and feel like I got as good of a taste as I will get from this game.

Lot of potential. This Genre is relatively underdeveloped and the UI is really cool. However, the game play is often linear, and, if you want your country to succeed, then you are forced down a set path every time.  I'd like to see your choices have more effect on other choices. For instance, selecting to build better roads increased oil pollution and consumption, regardless of the fact that previous policies might have eliminated gas-powered cars altogether. Fun game and thought experiment for a few hours, needs work to be anything more than that.

This game should be called Elected Dictator Policy Manager because that's exactly what it is. 

There are two giant problems with this game. One, there are no advisers. No congress. You're just unilaterally making policy decisions with only a vague idea of what all they will affect. 

The other problem is that the population is incredibly spastic, but I guess I would be spastic, too, if the country were run by a dictator and an obesity epidemic could come out of nowhere because of...agricultural subsidies. Or a rare-Earth mineral crisis can be caused by no longer being a technological backwater. Or if simply having a high GDP could completely destroy the environment in less than a year - as in, the primary reason listed for my environment tanking was my GDP increasing. I could see this happening over the course of a decade, but months? 

Yes, policy decisions will certainly affect things, but the effects are amplified well beyond all reason, and don't reflect the overall apathy of humanity. 

When your GDP is consistently rising, your debt and unemployment is vanishing, and the Capitalists still try to assassinate you, something isn't working right. 

The game is easy enough to win, so long as you're using a third-party guide that tells you which policies just plain don't work because the negative effects are so completely overboard compared to the positive effects. Most of the policies are just wrong ways to do things. 

Also, $25 for this? I don't remember where I got it - likely on sale or in a bundle - but even if the game worked in a rational manner, it's maybe a $10 game. It's just menus which, irritatingly, don't even have a search function (and there is definitely a need for a search function!)

All of the above might not be so bad if there was anything actually fun happening in this game. Instead, you're literally just picking canned policy decisions to which the population does not react in any realistic way.
Sure, I nominated this one for "Best Alternate History" award. Through a series of graphs and decisions, you could make the kind of idyllic society you'd want to actually live in.
