The game had a lot of promise, but I didn't end up having any real fun with it. The UI and Art are good, but that's about it. The biggest problem with the game is that it seems virtually impossible to succeed with anything other than very specific strategies, usually the ones which are social-democratic in the european model (even when playing non-european countries such as America). If you don't want to play with that strategy, then you're going to be fighting the game every step of the way.

One of the biggest drawbacks of the game IMO is the way the game starts you off. You don't get to pick your cabinet without burning political capital (the core resource of the game), so you're stuck with a conflicted set of advisors that are constantly losing political will and loyalty if you displease their supportive demographics. In addition, you're stuck with spending levels, policies, approval ratings, and crises that really put you in a rough spot to start.

I played America exclusively, and the starting political situation for America is outdated, inaccurate (again resembling a European model), and hurtling towards chaos at 90 miles per hour. Deficit spending and a pre-existing debt are virtually at crisis levels from the get-go. If you do nothing, within a few rounds you will have a debt crisis and your credit rating will be downgraded quarter after quarter and things only get harder from there. This necessitates immediate action, but unless you significantly cut both state pensions and military spending while also growing GDP, you're likely not going to be able to get it under control before it becomes a problem. If you do try to control spending in those areas however, people get extremely angry with you and soon your approval rating is tanking. It's a catch-22. If you ever end up with a declining economy, it's a death spiral. If I could pick my cabinet, pick my party activist demographic, and maybe even customize the initial state of the country in terms of crises and strengths, the game would be fun to tinker with to see what happens. As-is, you're just dealt a set of problems with very limited tools to respond.

This might all sound reasonably realistic to you - and maybe it is. This is a simulator, but IMO it's not a very good one. Information is presented in unclear ways, your actions don't have clear connections to outcomes, and it's extremely difficult to make strategic decisions. There are variable time delays when implementing policies, your advisors have variable levels of efficacy, every policy has different levels of support in general, and every policy also has an impact on a dozen other key values you're trying to track. The result is that it's almost impossible to understand what any given decision will really do except in the most vague and relative terms, and you aren't exactly certain when you should expect to see the results of your actions. Crises emerge spontaneously, and without warning - you might have an obesity epidemic emerge in your country, but you had no idea you even needed to worry about obesity, or what influences the obesity stat. The issue just isn't shown to you whatsoever until it becomes a problem, and it's always harder to solve the problems in the game once they've emerged. So you're pretty much flying blind. I dropped from a 36% approval rating (not great but I was working it up) to an 11% approval rating in a single round due to an unexpected crisis tanking my approval - I have no idea what I was supposed to do to predict it, it just happened. It's not fun to be blindsided like that.

Finally, there are terrorist groups which can attempt to assassinate you. In my experience, it's almost impossible to actually reasonably prevent terrorist groups from emerging, gaining membership, and making an attempt on your life. If you decrease intelligence agency funding, you are pretty much guaranteed to be assassinated, even if you institute an organized crime bureau, even if you invest heavily in police, and so on. The only other options to try to drive down radicalism appear to be instituting a police state, and I didn't want to do that. You're going to anger some random demographic which will radicalize and ultimately kill you, and that's game over. It's just not fun if you don't even get to see the midterm election you were working towards - a sandbox/simulator is most fun to see what will happen, but this predictable outcome is just annoying and ends the fun prematurely.

The game would be better if it allowed for a wider range of strategies, simulated something other than the European democracies, and had a clearer presentation of information to make more informed choices and avoid random pitfalls.

Great mix of economics and politics. On one side, you're trying to attract voters, which takes a bit of effort. On the other side, you also have to have a balanced economy that functions.

There's plenty to mess around with. I love how much data is available in-game, and it doesn't take too long before you learn how to intuitively use it all.

My least favorite part is how easily you wind up with radicals or terrorists that try to assassinate you, but that maybe just be a result of my playing style.
